Gerrymandering is when a political group tries to change a voting district to create a result that helps them or hurts the group who is against them. It is named after Elbridge Gerry (1744-1814). Gerrymandering works by wasting votes. It puts more votes of winners into the district they will win so the losers win in another district. If we have Group A and Group B both trying to win in a district. Group A has 40 votes and Group B has 50. In another district Group A knows Group B will win for sure. Group A changes the voting district so that 11 votes are moved to the other district. Now they will win 40 votes to 39. Mycenaeans of Ancient Greece were the first inhabitants of the island. It represents the first advanced and distinctively Greek civilization in mainland Greece with its palatial states, urban organization, works of art, and writing system. Title: How to Delete Apps on Android Android devices come with a plethora of features that allow users to customize their experience according to their preferences. One such feature is the ability to delete apps that are no longer needed or used. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on how to delete apps in Android, along with key tips and guidelines to ensure clarity and practicality. **Step 1: Unlock Your Device** Before you can begin deleting apps from your device, make sure it's unlocked. Most modern Androids require a pattern, PIN code, password, or fingerprint scan before they can be accessed. *Unlocking your phone ensures that only authorized individuals have access to its contents and settings.* **Step 2: Open the App Drawer** The app drawer houses all installed applications on your device. To open it, tap the icon usually located at the bottom center of your home screen. It may look like a circle containing dots or squares, depending upon your launcher. *Accessing the app drawer allows you to view every application installed on your device, making it easier to find those you wish to remove.* **Step 3: Locate the App You Want to Delete** Scroll through the list until you find the app you want to uninstall. If necessary, utilize the search function by tapping the magnifying glass icon at the top right corner of the screen and typing in the name of the app. *Finding the correct app is crucial; removing unnecessary apps frees up storage space while improving overall performance.* **Step 4: Enter the App Info Screen** Long press (press and hold) the selected app's icon. After a few seconds, a menu should appear. Tap "App info" or the 'i' symbol next to the app's name. *This action reveals important details about the chosen app, including permissions requested and amount of data consumed.* **Step 5: Initiate Uninstallation Process** On the app info page, locate and tap the "Uninstall" button. A confirmation box might pop up asking if you really want to uninstall the app. Confirm by tapping "OK." *Initiating the uninstall process removes the unwanted app, thereby freeing up valuable storage space.* Key Tips & Guidelines: - Be certain you truly wish to delete an app before doing so. Once deleted, some apps cannot be recovered without re-downloading them from the Google Play Store. - Regularly review your installed apps and consider deleting any rarely used ones. Not only does this clear clutter but also enhances battery life and device speed. - Remember that system apps - pre-installed software essential for proper functioning of your device - typically cannot be removed unless your phone is rooted. - Always double check whether an app is downloaded directly onto your device or accessible via the cloud. Deleting cloud-based apps won't affect their availability when connected to Wi-Fi or mobile data. By following these steps carefully and adhering to the provided tips and guidelines, you'll successfully delete unnecessary apps from your Android device, thus optimizing both usability and efficiency. What d' you have on your neck? diaste wrote:I have an original cell. I have to find how much larger the consecutive cells are. The percentage is 6.3. Every succeeding cell is 6.3% larger than the previous. When I solve for the second cell I use x + .063x = x If I substitute 10 for x the second cell = 10.63. The third cell then is 10.63 + .063(10.63)= 11.29 Yes? No? That is virtually correct, but if you factor out the in your equation, you'll get: ... which ends up being simpler, and this is where their less precise 1.06 comes from. To put this into a general formula, we can use the subscripts to differentiate between any given that follows it: So, this is basically saying, 'whatever size cell number is at any given time, the next cell (cell number ) will be 1.063 times larger'. Now I have 18 cells to consider. The book is telling me that the 18th cell is going to about 2.85 times larger than the original cell. Fine. They ask if (1.06) to the 18th power or (1.06) to the 19th power describes the relative size of the 18th cell to the original. I know the answer is (1.06) to the 18th but why? .063 to the 18th power is no where close. Well, the original cell, let's call its size , is the size every other cell is relative to. Since the question is using the less precise value of 1.06, I'll use that value here. Therefore, the eighteenth copy will be = 2.85 times larger than the original cell. But when I consider any two consecutive cells and attempt to find the percent smaller of a previous cell the percent is approx. 5.9%. The book says this is correct but how is it possible? Each cell is 5.9% (and not 6.3%) smaller than the subsequent cell because the difference in size between the two cells represents a smaller proportion of the larger cell compared to the smaller one. It might be easier to see if we use a different example. Let's say each subsequent cell is 100% larger than the previous one, that is, each subsequent cell is twice as large. Now look at it from the other direction. If each previous cell is half the size of the subsequent one, each previous cell will be 50% smaller. If it were 100% smaller, it would have a size of zero, which clearly can't be right. Back to our original values, and then rearranging to make So, the smaller cell is 94.1% the size of the larger one, or (100% - 94.1% = 5.9%) smaller. [CIC is up and walking toward the camera] Which is bigger United States or North America? And when you need it you don't put it. A professor is an accomplished and recognized academic. In most Commonwealth nations, as well as northern Europe, the title "professor" is the highest academic rank at a university. In the United States and Canada, the title of "professor" is also the highest rank, but a higher percentage achieve it. In these areas, professors are scholars with doctorate degrees (typically Ph.D. degrees) or equivalent qualifications. Most professors teach in four-year colleges and universities. An emeritus professor is a title given to selected retired professors with whom the university wishes to continue to be associated due to their stature and ongoing research. Emeritus professors do not receive a salary. However, they are often given office or lab space, and use of libraries, labs, and so on. [pretends to pour into the toy cups] Title: How to Get The Weather Channel Push Notifications in Wunderground's iOS App Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of enabling push notifications from The Weather Channel within the Wunderground app on your iOS device. This will allow you to receive crucial weather updates directly on your lock screen or notification center without having to constantly check the app. By following these steps meticulously, you can ensure that you are always informed about changing weather conditions. Step 1: Launch the Wunderground App Locate the Wunderground app on your iPhone or iPad's home screen or app library. Tap on its icon to open the application. If you don't have the app installed yet, download it first from the Apple App Store. Key Tip: Make sure your device is connected to the internet before launching the app as some features may require an active data connection. Step 2: Access Settings Once the app is opened, look at the bottom menu bar and find the gear icon which represents 'Settings'. Tap on it to proceed. Guideline: Navigating through different sections of any app becomes easier once you familiarize yourself with the layout and location of various options. Spend a few moments exploring the settings page to understand where everything is located. Step 3: Select Alert Preferences Under the 'Settings' section, scroll down until you see 'Alert Preferences'. This option allows you to customize what kind of alerts you want to receive. Step 4: Choose Your Location If you haven't already set up a location, you'll be prompted to do so now. Enter your city name, zip code, or select from the list provided. After setting up your location, move forward. Step 5: Enable Severe Weather Alerts Within the 'Alert Preferences', toggle on the switch next to 'Severe Weather Alerts'. These alerts come directly from The Weather Channel. They include information about severe thunderstorm warnings, tornado watches, flash flood warnings, and more. Key Tip: Enabling only necessary alert types can help avoid unnecessary disturbances while keeping you updated about significant weather events. Step 6: Customize Additional Alerts (Optional) Apart from Severe Weather Alerts, Wunderground offers other categories like 'Pollen Count', 'Air Quality', etc., which can also be enabled based on personal preference. However, please note that these additional alerts might not necessarily be powered by The Weather Channel. Step 7: Save Changes After making all desired adjustments, make sure to save them. Most apps automatically save changes made in settings but double-checking never hurts! Step 8: Test Your Setup To confirm if everything is working correctly, try simulating a test alert. You can do this by going back to the main screen, selecting your saved location, and then choosing 'Alerts & Radar'. Here, manually trigger a test alert if available. By completing these steps, you have successfully configured the Wunderground app to provide you with critical weather updates via push notifications from The Weather Channel. Stay safe and informed! Title: How to Block a Facebook Group on Android Facebook groups can be a great way to connect with like-minded individuals, stay updated on topics that interest you, or engage in discussions about shared hobbies. However, there might come a time when a particular group becomes overwhelming, inappropriate, or irrelevant. If you've decided that it's best to stop seeing updates from a specific Facebook group but aren't sure how to do so on your Android device, this step-by-step guide is here to help. Before we start, it is important to note that blocking a Facebook group will prevent you from receiving any notifications related to that group, as well as hiding its posts from your news feed. This action does not delete the group nor remove you as a member; you simply unfollow the group without leaving it permanently. You may rejoin or unblock the group at any point in the future if needed. Now let's begin! 1. Open the Facebook app on your Android device. The first step is to launch the official Facebook application installed on your smartphone or tablet. Locate the familiar blue icon with a white 'f' lettermark, then tap on it to open the app. 2. Sign in to your account (if necessary). If you are already logged into your Facebook account, you will be taken directly to your News Feed. Otherwise, enter your email address or phone number along with your password to sign in. 3. Navigate to the group you wish to block. From the News Feed, locate the search bar at the top of the screen. Tap on it and type in the name of the group you want to block using your virtual keyboard. Once the correct group appears in the dropdown list, tap on it to access the group page. 4. Access the group settings menu. On the group page, find the three horizontal dots located near the cover photo, usually in the upper right corner. Tapping these dots will reveal a contextual menu with various options related to the group. 5. Select 'Unfollow'. Scroll down until you see the option labeled 'Unfollow', which should be somewhere in the middle of the list. By choosing this option, you effectively block the group, preventing its content from appearing in your News Feed while remaining a member. A confirmation message will appear briefly at the bottom of the screen indicating that you have successfully unfollowed the group. 6. Verify the change by checking your News Feed. To ensure that the group has indeed been blocked, navigate back to your News Feed. Refresh the page by pulling downwards with your finger. After refreshing, scroll through your feed for a few minutes - you should no longer see any posts originating from the group you just blocked. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Be aware that blocking a group will not remove you as a member. It only stops you from seeing their content on your News Feed and mutes notifications related to the group. * If you ever decide to rejoin the group, you can do so by visiting the group page again and tapping the 'Join' button. Note that joining a previously blocked group will automatically refollow it, causing its posts to once again appear in your News Feed. * Remember that muting a group does not affect other members' ability to interact with you within the group. They will still be able to tag you, mention you, or send direct messages regarding the group's activities. * Consider snoozing the group instead of blocking it if you only need temporary respite from its content. Snoozing a group hides its posts for 30 days before they reappear in your News Feed unless you choose to extend the period. He cake going to be in his mouth. Maybe you better give them a drink. Title: How to Cough in Public Without Appearing Sick Introduction: Coughing in public can sometimes be unavoidable, but it might make others uncomfortable or concerned about your health. This guide will help you cough discreetly without drawing too much attention to yourself, making those around you feel at ease. By following these steps, you'll maintain personal hygiene while also being considerate of other people's space and comfort level. Step 1: Be Aware of Your Surroundings Before coughing, take note of where you are and who is nearby. If possible, move away from crowded areas or create some distance between yourself and others before coughing. This simple action reduces the chance of spreading germs and minimizes disturbance to those around you. Key Tip: Always carry tissues with you; they serve as both barriers against droplet spread and handy tools for masking sounds when needed. Step 2: Clear Your Throat Quietly If you feel a tickle in your throat that may lead to a cough, try clearing it gently first. Gently swallow or breathe in deeply through your nose, then exhale slowly through pursed lips. These actions can often alleviate the need to cough altogether. Guideline: Avoid forceful throat-clearing noises, which tend to draw more attention than necessary. Step 3: Use a Tissue When Possible Should you still need to cough, cover your mouth and nose with a tissue. Press one side of the tissue firmly over your nostrils and let the other side rest loosely over your mouth. The dual purpose of this method catches any airborne particles and muffles the sound of your cough. Key Tip: Dispose of used tissues immediately after coughing to prevent potential contamination. Step 4: Perform a Silent Cough In situations where using a tissue isn't feasible (e.g., during conversations), perform what we call a "silent cough." To do so, keep your lips lightly closed together while slightly opening your jaw. Exhale sharply but quietly through your nose, producing a minimalistic cough noise. Remember to turn your head away from others if possible to minimize discomfort for them. Guideline: Practice this technique silently several times until mastered; ensure there's only slight movement visible from outside your mouth area. Step 5: Apologize Politely (Optional) After coughing, especially if it was louder than intended or drew notice from others, offer an apologetic smile or nod to acknowledge the interruption. This small gesture shows consideration towards their peace of mind, reinforcing positive social etiquette. Conclusion: By practicing these steps consistently, you can effectively cough in public without appearing sick or causing undue distress among passersby. Maintaining proper hygiene practices remains crucial even when trying to be discreet - wash hands regularly, avoid touching your face, and stay home if feeling under the weather. Stay healthy! A CERTAIN Jackdaw was so proud and ambitious that, not contented to live within his own sphere, he picked up the feathers which fell from the Peacocks, stuck them among his own, and very confidently introduced himself into an assembly of those beautiful birds. They soon found him out, stripped him of his borrowed plumes, and falling upon him with their sharp bills, punished him as his presumption deserved. Upon this, full of grief and affliction, he returned to his old companions, and would have flocked with them again; but they, knowing his late life and conversation, industriously avoided him, and refused to admit him into their company; and one of them, at the same time, gave him this serious reproof: "If, friend, you could have been contented with your station, and had not disdained the rank in which nature had placed you, you had not been used so scurvily by those amongst whom you introduced yourself, nor suffered the notorious slight which we now think ourselves obliged to put upon you." MORAL. Great evils arise from vanity; for when we try to place ourselves in a position for which we are not fit, we are liable to be laughed at, and, when we would return to our former state, we find we have lost the esteem of our former friends. I think he mainly showed me foods that he eats at home or that he recognizes. - For Teachers As this grammar point is overwhelmingly taught when students are still young, this article concentrates on young learners. However, most of it is relevant to or adaptable to low level adults. "It is" and "They are" might be the first piece of grammar that young learners come across, and is an important one in class as it can be endlessly recycled once it is presented by the simple expedient of using some flashcards with two or more objects on when doing future areas of vocabulary. It can also help reinforce the need for -s after plural nouns. The ideas below should hopefully make this structure a pleasant introduction to English grammar for kids. As mentioned in some of the game ideas, this can also be tied in with minimal pairs and/ or phonics by getting the kids to spot that "They are both sh words" and "It is a ch word". Issues with It is/ They are Before first teaching It is/ They are, there are several points to think about. The first is what you want to do about the contractions "It's" and "They're". The six possible approaches are: - Just use the contractions, perhaps avoiding "Yes, it is" and "Yes, they are" to make that possible. - Just do the full forms and leave contractions until later. - Present the full forms but use natural contracted forms during listening comprehension, e.g. the listen and touch games described below. - Use both forms in the right contexts (e.g. full forms in writing but contractions in speech bubbles) but don't explain the differences or force the use of one form or the other. - Use both forms in the right contexts and expect students to do the same, but without explaining. - Use this as a chance to present the idea of contractions, doing both forms and explaining the differences. As briefly mentioned in the list above, the main differences between uses of contractions and full forms are that the full forms must be used in short positive answers and tend to be used in writing. It is possible to explain this and/ or prompt the relevant form without use of L1 with tactics like holding up your index finger and middle finger apart to represent the full form and together to represent the contraction. You can also draw a speech bubble around the contraction and draw a pen next to the full form. The main argument for going to all this trouble is that native speakers almost always use contractions where they can in speech, making comprehension difficult without some work on this point. There is also the slight danger of unintentional overemphasis by saying "It IS a pen". However, I have recently become very doubtful that these two things make it worth all the effort, especially at this early stage, and especially when most of their communication will probably be with other non-native speakers who will also avoid contractions. The next issue once you have that one sorted is "It is" and "This is", including the common but possibly confusing "What's this?" "It's a pen" classroom exchange. In many classroom interactions such as presentation of vocabulary "This is" is the more natural option and sentences like "What is it?" can sound strange. The same thing is true of many of the games below. However, This is/ They are doesn't really work as a grammar point to present and "It is" will be more useful for their later lessons introducing other subject pronouns, the Present Continuous tense, etc. As there is no chance of explaining the difference at this stage even in L1, your four choices are: - Stick to "It is" even when it sounds unnatural. - Use whatever sounds natural but design activities so the production will mainly or entirely be "It is". - Use them naturally but don't correct mix ups from the kids. - Use them naturally and correct (some) mix ups from the kids, but without explaining why. My own choice tends to be the second of those options. Presenting It is/ They are This is such a simple point that I tend to present it for the first time in the middle of one of the practice activities below, for example doing some normal "What's this?" "It's a stocking" practice with the slow reveal flashcard game and then adding the twist of a flashcard that has more than one object on it and so cannot be got from the teacher by saying "It is " as has been the case until then. Another way of introducing the point by stealth can be used if they already know S for plurals. You can smoothly move on from getting them to draw, label, draw a line between etc "apple"/ "apples", "orange"/ "oranges" etc to doing the same with "It's an apple" and "They are oranges" without needing to present the grammar at all. A variation on this can also be used to lead up to a grammar presentation for those who don't know S for plurals yet, with "It's a banana" and "They are houses" possible to match just from knowledge of how to read the nouns, but "It is a chair" and "They are chairs" needing the grammar point of the day. Practising It is/ They are The activities below start with games and then move onto more general activities. The games near the top are ones I often use also to present the language for the first time. Games for It is/ They are It's a ball/ They are balls Throwing a ball around can be a great activity for revision at the start of any class, e.g. with students asking and answering basic questions or counting as they do so. I often finish this stage with "What's this?" "It's a ball" before moving onto presenting or revising vocabulary with the same phrases, and it is an easy and amusing step from this to trying to throw and catch two balls with "What are they?/ What are these?" "They are balls". Run and touch games for It is/ They are Another thing I like to start lessons with is students running around and touching that classroom objects that I say like "table" and "ruler", shouting out a sentence to identify it like "It's a table" when they do so. This can be extended to include "They are" in several ways. One is for them to touch only one object if they hear "It is" and more than one object (perhaps at the same time if it is possible) if they hear "They are". A more manic version is for them to touch every example of that thing in the classroom before they say the relevant sentence. These games can also be played with students deciding for themselves whether they need "It is" or "They are", touching and shouting out the former if they think there is only one example in the classroom (e.g. "It is a door") and doing the same with the latter if they can find more than one (e.g. "They are windows"). A simpler variation, and maybe the most suitable for presenting the language for the first time, is to very slowly say what they should touch starting with "It is " or "They are ", so that they hopefully start to use that as a clue about which of the things in the classroom they have to touch. This could also be extended by the person speaking never saying the name of the thing but just clues like "They are toys", "They are round" etc until someone guesses, touches and shouts out the name in a full sentence. Any of these games can also be played with flashcards on their tables, spread across the floor, stuck up around the classroom or hidden around the classroom. It is/ They are flashcard games Any games you usually play with flashcards can have this grammar point added to them by making sure some of the cards have two or more items on them, e.g. mixing up pictures and/ or words of "a toy car" with "dolls" and "jigsaw puzzles". Slowly revealing a card for students to identify is even more fun this way as they must wait to put up their hands until they are sure if there is more than one object on the card or not (or take their chances and guess). Another way of doing this is to present vocabulary that has things in common, for example presenting "(It is a) ball" and "(It is a) yoyo" and then eliciting that "They are circles". This can be done with the words with things in common presented straight after each other in this way, or cards can all be stuck to the board after they are presented and students can try to spot any similarities at any point after that. You can also make sure the "It is " structure comes up when talking about similarities by making sure the pack has a mix of objects with things in common and ones that don't match each other at all. When all the cards are up on the board, students are asked to mention any sentences like "They are hard" that hadn't come up with during the game and to spot and explain any which don't match with sentences like "It is in the bathroom" and "It is a lizard". The game above can also be played with several objects with things in common on one card, e.g. slowly revealing a card with different toys on it and getting students to identify both the category and at least one of the examples. This can also be done with specific examples of one word, e.g. for "palace" you could have a card with at least one which they would know so they can say "They are palaces. It is Buckingham Palace." It is/ They are pelmanism Pelmanism, also known as Pairs or The Memory Game, is a popular card game and TEFL game where the whole pack of cards are spread face down on the table and students try to find pairs that match each other in some way, e.g. two clubs, two toys or two words starting with "ch". There are several ways in which this game can have "It is" and "They are" added to it. The simplest way of adding this language is to get students to identify the first card after they turn it over (e.g. "It's an apple") and the second card in the same way ("It's an apple") before saying the same thing for both to explain that they have a pair ("They are apples"). Alternatively, they can find singular and plural cards for the same object ("It is an apple" for the first card and "They are apples" for the second) or cards that match completely ("They are apples" with "They are apples"). The same thing can be done with things that have things in common, e.g. "It's a steak", "It's an apple", "They are red". The game can be set up so the similarities are colours, shapes, sizes, categories of vocabulary (e.g. half the cards are body parts and half are animals), etc. Students can also be asked to say both a similarity and a difference (e.g. "They are transport. It is big. It is small.") Higher level classes can be given a random selection of recent and/ or upcoming vocabulary to use try and think of their own connections between in a game I call Random Pelmanism, e.g. "It's a clothes horse" "It's an aerial" "They are both metal". Another variation of pelmanism that also includes both "It is" and "They are" is for students to try to turn over two cards which are related plus one more card which is different. They then identify the similarity (e.g. "They are dangerous") and difference (e.g. "It isn't dangerous" or "It is cute"). It is/ They are brainstorming races The kinds of descriptions that students are asked to come up with in some of the games above can also be given to the students for them to come up with examples of. They then draw and/ or write those things and get points for saying and/ or writing sentences identifying both the category and the individual examples, e.g. "They are toys. It's a toy car". The same game can be played with things that are more difficult to think of, e.g. "They are big. One is friendly and one is dangerous". It is the odd one out This is a common vocabulary exercise that can have this language and more fun added to it with a competitive element. At least three pictures and/ or words with an odd one out is quickly flashed up, e.g. with a PowerPoint slide, OHP or on an A3 flashcard, and students rush to identify the odd one out and the similarity between the others with sentences like "They are in the classroom. It is in my house". This can also be done with two or more categories and an odd one out in the list, e.g. "They are on your head. They are on your feet. It is on your body." It is/ They are stations Stations is a quite popular young learners game in which students touch one of two walls in the classroom depending on which thing they hear or think is suitable, e.g. the right wall if they hear "ch" and the left wall if they hear "sh" or the right wall if they think the word takes "a" and the right wall if they think the word takes "an". The same can easily be done with "It is" and "They are", e.g. by students touching one of the two walls based on how many things are on the flashcard, in the classroom, in their city, in the view outside the window, or in the world. Guess how many As well as the slow reveal flashcard game mentioned above, students can also guess how many things are in your hand, in a plastic bag, under a cloth after you removed stuff, etc. They then make a suitable It is/ They are sentence depending on whether they think the number is one or larger. It is/ They are target practice Students are given two balls and throw one of them at something in the classroom. If they can identify the thing they hit (e.g. "It's a curtain"), they get one point. They can then keep that one point and stop there, or take a chance and try to throw the other ball at the same (kind of) thing. If they do take that chance, they can get four points if they can do so and say "They are curtains" or lose all their points if they hit something different. The same game can be played with pictures, e.g. large flashcards around the room or pictures projected on the board or a screen. Using picture books for It is/ They are Perhaps the most fun kinds of books are ones in which things are hidden and need to be found, e.g. Where's Wally and I Spy books. Perhaps with the text covered up, the teacher calls out or writes up the name of an object or kind of person and the students must race to find how many there are of that thing and shout out the correct sentence, e.g. "They are wizards". This can also be done with pictures in front of the whole class, e.g. with an OHP, or students can even make similar pictures to help each other. A less fun but more educational version is to use a picture dictionary - both A to Z and ones with words in categories work fine. Open a random page of the book and let students compete to use both "It is" and "They are" to identify objects on the page and the theme of it, e.g. "It is a zoo. It is a zebra. They are Z words" or "It is a tractor. It is a combine harvester. They are on farms". Using videos for It is/ They are This is simplicity itself - choose a video where objects appear both singularly and in groups, and get students to stick up their hands whenever they think they can make a sentence that no one else has yet, e.g. "They are presents" and "It is a teddy bear" in a Xmas video. You Will Need - A pair of shoes with laces, like sneakers - And hands! Put on shoes Put on your untied shoes. Sit or kneel Sit or kneel on the floor and bend your knee so that you can easily reach one of your feet with both hands. You can also sit on a chair and bring one foot up so that it's resting on the seat of the chair. Get foot comfortable Make sure your foot is comfortable in the shoe. If the tongue is scrunched up, tug on it to smooth it out. Hold shoelaces & tighten Now hold the right-hand shoelace in your right hand, and the left one in your left hand. Pull gently but firmly on each to tighten the shoe comfortably around your foot. Cross the lace in your right hand over and then under the one in your left hand. Grab it with your left hand to pull it all the way through. Grab the other lace with your right hand. Pull to tighten Pull both laces to tighten them. Make a loop w/ right lace Make a loop out of the right lace and hold the bottom of it close to the shoe with your right hand. This part can be a little tricky. Everyone does it differently, so experiment until you figure out how it works best for you. Make loop w/ left lace Make a same-size loop with the left lace, and hold it near the shoe with your left hand. Now do exactly what you did in the beginning with the laces: cross the right-hand loop over the left-hand loop, and then under it. Grab loops & tighten Grab the loop that's now on the left with your left hand, and grab the other loop with your right hand. Pull tight, and your shoe is tied! Repeat w/ other shoe Repeat with the other shoe, then hit the road! Did I do that to you yesterday? Creating a Pareto chart in Microsoft Excel 2010 is an effective way to visualize and analyze data that contains both quantitative and categorical variables. A Pareto chart displays bars representing different categories, sorted from left to right in descending order based on their frequency or magnitude. Additionally, a cumulative line curve shows the running total as you move from left to right along the x-axis. This type of graph is particularly useful when analyzing quality control issues, defects, or any other problem where there are several potential causes. In this comprehensive guide, we will walk through all necessary steps to create a Pareto chart in MS Excel 2010 using a sample dataset containing various types of defects found during production at a factory: 1. **Prepare your Data** Before creating a Pareto chart, ensure your data is organized into two columns - one with category labels (in our example, these would be 'Burrs', 'Scratches', etc.) and another column with numerical values associated with those categories (representing quantities of each respective defect). For instance: | Category | Count | |--- | --- | | Burrs | 45 | | Scratches | 38 | | Dents | 27 | | Misalignment| 23 | | Other | 9 | Organizing your data like so allows Excel to quickly understand what information needs to go into the creation of your Pareto chart. 2. **Select your Data Range** Highlight the cells corresponding to your prepared data by clicking and dragging over them. Make sure to select both columns - Categories first then Counts. Title: How to Get a Boyfriend When You Don't Consider Yourself Attractive Introduction: This guide is designed to help individuals who don't feel attractive find love and companionship. It's important to remember that attractiveness goes beyond physical appearance; confidence, personality, and shared interests can be just as appealing. This article will provide you with steps and advice on building your self-confidence, expanding your social circle, and ultimately finding a boyfriend who values you for more than just your looks. Step 1: Improve Self-Confidence * Explanation: Low self-esteem often stems from negative self-talk. Challenging these thoughts and focusing on personal growth can significantly improve your overall confidence. * Key Tips: + Practice positive affirmations daily (e.g., "I am worthy of love," "I have unique qualities that make me special"). + Identify your strengths and accomplishments, writing them down if necessary. + Surround yourself with supportive friends and family members who uplift you. + Engage in activities that challenge you and boost self-efficacy. Step 2: Focus on Personal Growth * Explanation: Investing time in developing new skills or hobbies makes you a more interesting person, which can increase your appeal to potential partners. * Guidelines: + Explore different interests and identify what brings you joy. + Set achievable goals related to personal development. + Join clubs, groups, or classes centered around those interests. + Document your progress and celebrate small victories along the way. Step 3: Expand Your Social Circle * Explanation: Meeting new people increases the likelihood of meeting someone compatible. By broadening your network, you open up opportunities for romantic connections. * Key Steps: + Attend social events, such as parties, meetups, or community gatherings. Title: How to Be a Pretend Baby on Club Penguin Club Penguin (CP) is a popular online multiplayer game that allows players to create their own penguin character and explore a virtual world filled with fun activities and social interactions. One unique aspect of CP is role-playing, where players can take on different roles or characters and act them out within the game. This tutorial will focus on how to be a pretend baby on Club Penguin, providing in-depth explanations, key tips, and guidelines for achieving this role-play experience. Step 1: Create Your Penguin Character To begin your journey as a pretend baby on CP, you'll first need to create a new penguin character. During the creation process, consider choosing a name that sounds cute and childlike, such as "BabyCakes" or "LittleMissSunshine". For clothing options, select simple and colorful items that resemble baby attire, like bibs, overalls, or onesies. However, keep in mind that there are no specific baby clothes available in the CP catalog, so choose what comes closest to conveying a babyish appearance. Step 2: Choose an Igloo That Reflects a Nursery Theme Your igloo serves as your virtual home within CP. To enhance your pretend baby persona, design your igloo to look like a nursery. Use furniture items such as cribs, teddy bears, building blocks, and other toys to create a cozy and playful atmosphere. You may also want to add wallpaper and flooring designs that evoke feelings of warmth and comfort. Remember, creating a welcoming space will help attract fellow role-players who might be interested in interacting with a pretend baby character. Step 3: Learn Basic Communication Skills As a pretend baby, you'll want to communicate using simplified language, emojis, or even baby talk to maintain consistency with your chosen role. Practice typing short sentences with basic vocabulary and proper grammar. Avoid using complex words or phrases, as they don't align with the communication skills expected from a baby. Using emoticons or pictures can further emphasize your character's emotional state, making it easier for others to understand and respond appropriately. Step 4: Interact With Other Players Now that you have established your pretend baby character, it's time to engage with other players. Approach friendly-looking penguins and initiate conversations using your simplified language skills. Ask questions about their day, share your favorite toys, or invite them to visit your nursery-themed igloo. By being open and inviting, you increase the chances of sparking positive interactions with fellow CP community members. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always respect other players and adhere to Club Penguin's rules and Community Guidelines. * Maintain a consistent role by staying in-character at all times. * Engage in age-appropriate activities, such as playing games, attending parties, or participating in mascot meetups. * Don't reveal personal information about yourself or ask others to disclose their real-life details. * If another player becomes aggressive or unkind, report them immediately to prevent any harm to yourself or others. * Take breaks when needed to avoid burnout or becoming overwhelmed by the virtual environment. By following these steps and guidelines, you can successfully become a pretend baby on Club Penguin and enjoy a fun and engaging role-play experience! If you don't watch me you'll learn. Title: How to Perform the Disappearing Styrofoam Trick The disappearing styrofoam trick is an impressive and mystifying science experiment that you can perform using just two common household items - a flame and a styrofoam cup or plate. This activity demonstrates the chemical reaction between heat and polystyrene (the main component of styrofoam), which causes rapid sublimation - changing from solid directly into gas without becoming liquid first. By following this detailed step-by-step guide, you too will be able to captivate your audience with this fascinating display. ### Materials Needed 1. A small piece of styrofoam (cup, plate, or any other item) 2. A lighter, matchbox, or candle 3. Tongs or tweezers (optional but recommended) 4. Clear surface area 5. Fire extinguisher or baking soda nearby as safety precautions **Key Tips:** Before attempting this trick, ensure there are no flammable materials near your workspace, keep yourself at a safe distance from the fire source, and have adult supervision if necessary. Additionally, consider performing outdoors or under proper ventilation due to potential fumes generated during the process. ### Step 1: Choose Your Piece of Styrofoam Select a suitable size of styrofoam based on the intensity of the flame available. Smaller flames may require thinner pieces while larger ones might handle thicker sections better. Keep in mind that excessive heating could cause unwanted ignition; hence starting off with smaller portions is advisable until mastering control over the procedure. ### Step 2: Prepare Your Flame Source Choose your preferred fire starter such as matches, lighters, or candles depending upon availability and convenience. Make sure it's placed firmly on a stable base where it won't tip over during the performance. **Safety Guideline**: Always hold the lighter horizontally when lighting up to prevent accidental burn injuries. If using matches, strike them away from your body and discard safely after usage. ### Step 3: Set Up Your Workspace [the second pair of silver men falls over] [Garb rings the other ball to the table] Title: How to Follow a Board on Pinterest Following a board on Pinterest is an excellent way to stay updated with pins related to your interests or projects. By following a board, you can view new pins as they are added, engage with the content by liking or commenting, and even save them to your own boards for future reference. This tutorial will guide you through the process of following a board on Pinterest in simple, easy-to-follow steps. **Step 1:** **Log into Your Pinterest Account** Before you begin following boards, ensure that you have logged into your Pinterest account using your email address, phone number, or connected social media platform (Facebook or Google). Logging in allows access to all features available on Pinterest, including the ability to follow boards. *Key tip:* If you don't yet have a Pinterest account, sign up first! Visit <https://www.pinterest.com/> and click 'Sign Up'. Follow the prompts to create a profile, then return to this tutorial to start following boards. **Step 2:** **Navigate to the Desired Board** There are multiple ways to locate a specific board on Pinterest. You may already know the URL or name of the board; if so, enter it directly into your web browser's search bar. Alternatively, utilize the Pinterest search function by typing keywords relevant to the board topic in the site's search box at the top left corner and pressing Enter. Once you arrive at the board page, proceed to Step 3. *Guideline:* When searching via keywords, consider browsing categories for more targeted results. Click 'More' next to the search bar > select category > type keyword(s) in search box > press Enter. **Step 3:** **Confirm the Correctness of the Selected Board** Prior to clicking the 'Follow' button, double-check whether the chosen board aligns with your preferences. Review its title, description, and sample pins to make sure it suits your needs or matches your interest area. Following irrelevant boards might clutter your feed and distract from useful content. **Step 4:** **Click the 'Follow' Button** Once confirmed, find and click the blue 'Follow' button located either below the board's cover image or above the creator's profile picture. The word "Following" should appear once clicked, indicating successful subscription to updates from this particular board. *Key Tip:* To unfollow a board later, simply revisit the same location where you initially followed it and click 'Unfollow.' **Step 5:** **Explore More Boards (Optional)** If satisfied with the current board, take advantage of this opportunity to explore similar boards created by the same user. Scroll down to discover other boards they manage, which could potentially spark further curiosity or provide additional inspiration. Simply repeat Steps 2-4 for any interesting finds. By completing these five straightforward steps, you now know how to follow a board on Pinterest effectively. Enjoy staying informed about topics that matter most to you while engaging within the vibrant community built around shared passions and creativity. Franklin Delano Roosevelt (January 30, 1882 - April 12, 1945) was the 32nd President of the United States from 1933 until his death in 1945. He served 12 years as President, dying shortly after beginning his 4th term, the longest ever spent in office. After his death, the 22nd Amendment came into effect, limiting how long a person could be President. Before becoming President, he was Governor of New York from 1929 to 1932, Assistant United States Secretary of the Navy from 1913 to 1920 and a state senator from the state of New York. But she knows what feet hands um fingers. Encrypting a Kindle Fire can help protect your personal data from unauthorized access if your device is lost or stolen. By following these steps, you'll create an encryption password that will be required every time your device boots up or wakes up from sleep mode. This process may take some time depending on the amount of data stored on your Kindle Fire, but rest assured knowing that your information is secure. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A Kindle Fire tablet with the latest software update installed 2. Access to the device settings 3. Patience and attention to detail ### Step 1: Back Up Your Data Before beginning the encryption process, make sure to back up any important data saved on your Kindle Fire. While encrypting should not cause loss of data, there is always a small risk involved when making significant changes to your device. To back up your data, consider using Amazon's Cloud Drive service or another cloud storage solution. Alternatively, connect your Kindle Fire to a computer via USB cable and manually copy files to your preferred backup location. ### Step 2: Navigate to Device Settings On the home screen of your Kindle Fire, locate and tap the gear icon labeled "Settings" to open the device settings menu. ### Step 3: Enter the Security Section From within the settings menu, scroll down until you see the option titled "Security & Privacy." Tap this option to proceed. ### Step 4: Initiate Encryption Process Within the security section, find the subheading "Encryption" and select "Turn On" next to the text that reads "Device Encryption." Confirm that you wish to continue by tapping "Enable Device Encryption" when prompted. ### Step 5: Create an Encryption Password Now it's time to set up your encryption password. Choose a strong, unique passphrase containing letters, numbers, and symbols for optimal security. Remember that this password will be required every time your device starts up or awakens from sleep mode. Once satisfied with your chosen password, enter it twice to confirm accuracy before continuing. ### Step 6: Wait for Encryption Completion The actual encryption process can take anywhere from several minutes to multiple hours, depending on the quantity of data being encrypted. During this time, avoid turning off or restarting your Kindle Fire as doing so could interrupt the encryption procedure. It's best to leave your device alone while encryption takes place. ### Step 7: Verify Successful Encryption After completion, you'll receive a notification stating that your device has been successfully encrypted. If you do not receive such confirmation, double-check that the encryption process was not interrupted prematurely. ### Key Tips and Guidelines: * Always remember your encryption password! Losing it means losing access to all data stored on your Kindle Fire. There is no way to recover this password without resetting your device completely, which would result in permanent deletion of all encrypted data. * Use a complex yet memorable passphrase to ensure maximum security while still maintaining ease of recall. * Regularly back up your data to prevent potential losses due to unexpected issues or accidents. * Keep your Kindle Fire updated with the latest software releases to maintain both functionality and security. What is blood pressure? Blood pressure is the amount of force that your blood puts on your artery walls as it moves through your body. Here's how it works: Arteries are blood vessels that carry blood from your heart to the rest of your body. When your heart beats, it pushes your blood through your arteries. As the blood moves, it puts pressure on your artery walls. This is your blood pressure. What is high blood pressure? High blood pressure (also called hypertension) occurs when your blood moves through your arteries at a higher pressure than normal. Many different things can cause high blood pressure. If your blood pressure gets too high or stays high for a long time, it can cause health problems. This content has been supported by Forest Laboratories Inc. One way that plants could differ in their susceptibility to herbivores is through defense trade-off. Defense trade-off theory is commonly used to be seen as a fundamental theory to maintain ecological evenness. Plants can make a trade-off response to resource allocation, such as between defense and growth. Defenses against herbivory on plant diversity can vary in different situations. It can be neutral, detrimental or beneficial for plant fitness. Even in the absence of defensive trade-offs, herbivores may still be able to increase plant diversity, such as herbivores prefer subordinate species rather than dominant species. [walks across the room to see something else] Bee Life Cycle Honey Bee Life Cycle Honey bees are active all year round; they do not hibernate like other bee species. Honey bees collect nectar from plants, trees and flowers in the summer; they then reduce the water content of the nectar which becomes honey. Honey is the food source for bees; a surplus of honey is stored for use over the winter months. There are three types of honey bee: 1. A queen, which under normal circumstances there is only one. In a healthy honey bee colony, the queen is the only one which lays eggs. 2. Workers, which are sterile females that make up the majority of individuals and carry out most of the daily duties such as nectar, pollen and water collection, brood care and honey comb building. 3. Drones, which are males. The drone's sole purpose is to mate with new queens. Drone bees have no sting. Development stages of honey bee eggs. Queens: 3 days as an egg, 5 days as larvae, 8 days as pupae. 16 days total. Workers: 3 days as an egg, 6 days as larvae, 12 days as pupae. 21 days total. Drones: 3 days as an egg, 7 days as larvae, 14 days as pupae. 24 days total. Note: there are always variations in times. How honey bees reproduce. Honey bees way of reproducing is quite complex. When a colony becomes too large for their current nest they make new queens, the original queen leaves the nest taking approx half the bees with her. This is called swarming. Once they have left the nest they usually cluster on a branch or some other object and send scout bees out to look for a new home. Once a new home has been found the mass of clustered bees takes off and flies en-mass to their new home. The new queens which have been produced in the old nest hatch, normally the first queen to hatch will seek out the other queens (still encased in their cells) and sting them which kills them. This queen will then take over the colony. Sometimes more than one queen hatches and smaller swarms will leave the nest, these are called cast swarms the queen in a cast swarm is un-mated. Bumble Bee Life Cycle Bumble bees have a different life cycle to honey bees. In the winter months a queen Bumble bee is in hibernation, they do not store honey to feed from like honey bees do. Queen Bumble bees normally hibernate in the ground. When the temperature starts to rise in spring, the queens emerge from their winter sleep and start to feed on nectar producing flowers. The queens search out a suitable nest site, often the same areas are used year after year. Once the nest site is chosen, the queen makes a beeswax honey pot which she then fills with a small amount of honey. Bumble bees do not make enough honey to harvest in the same way as honey bees do. Pollen is also stored in the nest for the queen to use in hard times of bad weather. The queen lays a small amount of eggs on a ball of pollen and then keeps the eggs warm during the incubation period of around 4 days. Young bumble bees are fed pollen. There are several different species of Bumble bee and pupating times vary. When the young larvae are ready to pupate into adults, they spin a silk cocoon where they undergo transformation into adult bees. In late summer queens and drones are produced which mate, the drones die and the queens go into hibernation ready for the next year. Masonry Bee Life Cycle Unlike Honey bees or Bumble bees, masonry bees are solitary bees. Solitary bees do not make any honey or wax. Masonry bees emerge in the spring from their cocoons. Males emerge first. When the females emerge, they mate and the males then die. Females collect nectar and pollen to feed their offspring. Masonry bees like to nest in small tube like holes, often these are in brickwork and people mistake them for wasps. The female packs mud into the hole, then nectar and pollen, she then lays an egg and packs more mud to seal it all in. She will keep doing this until the hole is filled. Male eggs are laid last in the tube ready to emerge first. Once the hole is filled the female will plug the hole with mud. By midsummer the larvae have used all the stores provided by their mother and are ready to spin a cocoon to pupate into adults. Mining Bee Life Cycle Mining bees have a similar life-cycle to Masonry Bees, except they burrow into the ground to lay their eggs. They are a solitary bee. Title: How to Germinate Cannabis Seeds Introduction: Germinating cannabis seeds is the first step in growing your own marijuana plants. This process involves encouraging the seed to sprout by providing it with the right conditions. By germinating your own seeds, you have complete control over the strain and quality of your plants. The following steps will guide you through the process of germinating cannabis seeds using the paper towel method. Materials Needed: * Cannabis seeds * Two clean plates * Paper towels * Distilled water * Tweezers (optional) * A warm, dark place or a seedling heat mat Step 1: Soak the Seeds Soaking the seeds before placing them between damp paper towels can speed up the germination process. Fill a glass or small bowl with distilled water and drop your seeds in. Allow them to soak for at least six hours, but no longer than 24 hours. If left in the water too long, they may drown and fail to sprout. Remove any floating seeds as these are likely not viable. Step 2: Prepare the Plates and Paper Towels Take two clean plates and moisten several sheets of paper towel. Make sure they're wet but not dripping. Lay one sheet on each plate, then space out your cannabis seeds evenly across the surface of the moistened paper towel. Cover the seeds with another layer of slightly damp paper towel. Finally, cover both layers of paper towel with the second plate, creating a 'seed sandwich'. Step 3: Create Optimal Conditions Cannabis seeds require warmth and moisture to germinate successfully. Place the covered plates in a warm area away from direct sunlight. An ideal temperature range is around 70-90 F (21-32 C). You can also use a seedling heat mat set to this temperature range if you don't have access to a consistently warm location. Step 4: Monitor Daily Check on your seeds daily. Spray additional water onto the paper towels if necessary to keep them moist. Be careful not to let the towels dry out completely nor become oversaturated. It usually takes 24-48 hours for most seeds to open and reveal their taproots. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Use high-quality seeds - Lower-grade seeds often produce lower yields and might not sprout at all. * Don't force the shell off - Let the taproot emerge naturally; forcing it could damage the fragile new growth. * Maintain proper humidity - Keeping the environment suitably humid ensures successful germination. * Provide enough warmth - Without sufficient heat, germination may be delayed or unsuccessful. Step 5: Transferring Rooted Seeds Once the taproots appear (usually within 2-4 days), carefully transfer the rooted seeds into their final growing medium (soil, rockwool cubes, etc.). Handle the seeds gently by holding onto the hard casing rather than touching the delicate root. Plant the seeds about half an inch deep, ensuring that the taproot points downward. Firmly press the soil around the seed but do not compact it excessively. Conclusion: Patience and attentiveness during the germination stage ensure strong, healthy plant development. After completing these steps, your seedlings should grow steadily towards becoming mature plants ready for flowering. Remember, every strain has its unique characteristics and needs, so adapt these general principles accordingly for optimal results. Happy cultivating! [baby makes noise during first two words.] What a' ya going to put on your plate? Make the time for pretend play. Let your child be the "director." This helps him develop his own ideas. It also strengthens his thinking skills as he makes logical connections in his stories: The dog has to go back in his house because it's raining. You can help him develop his ideas by asking questions: What is the doggy feeling? What is the doggy trying to do? Why? What might happen next? Offer lots of props that help him act out the stories he's creating -hats, dress-up clothing, toy dishes, child-sized brooms, pads of paper, blocks, play food and household objects like big cardboard boxes, blankets, pillows, etc. Don't answer your child's questions right away. Ask first what he thinks the answer is. This gets his wheels turning. Listen carefully to and acknowledge his response and then you can offer the correct answer. For example, if he says he thinks it gets dark at night so people can sleep, you might respond: Yes, it is easier to sleep when it's dark, and then go on to explain as simply as possible about the sun setting and rising each day. Ask lots of questions during your everyday play and routines. As you go through your day together, ask your child "why" questions. Why do you think the leaves fall from the trees? Why does it snow? This gets your child's mind working and also lets him know that you are interested in and value his ideas. Watch your child and see what he is interested in. Ask questions about what you are seeing and experiencing together: What do you think we will find when we dig in the sand? Where do you think the butterfly is flying to? Wonder about things together: I wonder how many legs are on that spider? I wonder how many stairs there are to get up to the front door? I wonder where the rain goes when it lands on the ground? By noticing and building on your child's natural curiosity, you are nurturing her love of learning. Offer lots of chances to explore in creative ways. Take nature walks. Play with sand and water. Give your child objects he can take apart and investigate. By working with familiar (and not-so-familiar) objects, children figure out how things work. This type of problem-solving is critical for success in school. Make connections between past and present. Make the logical connections in your child's life clear to her: She has to wear mittens because her hands get cold if she doesn't. She needs to bring a towel to the pool so she can dry herself off. Use everyday routines to notice patterns. Using language to explain these patterns helps your child become a logical thinker and increases her vocabulary. "Do you notice that every time the dog whines he has to go out to do his business?" "When the buzzer goes off, the clothes are dry." Sort and categorize through the day. Do laundry together. Your child can separate colors from whites and make piles of socks, shirts, and pants. He can help set the table and organize the forks, plates and spoons. At clean-up time, have him put the cars on one shelf and books on another. Help him grasp a sense of time. Use an egg timer to help him put together the concept of time with the experience of time (to help him know what 5 or 10 minutes feels like.) This also gives him some sense of control over knowing when a change will happen. (He can look at the egg timer and see the arrow moving closer to the "0" which is when he has to stop playing and get in the car.) Talk about feelings. Help your child develop a feelings vocabulary. Put words to what you think she might be feeling. You are so mad that we have to leave the park. You feel sad when Grandma has to leave. This helps your child understand and cope with her feelings. Talk about what others might be feeling. That little girl is jumping up and down and smiling. Do you think she is happy? When reading books, ask what she thinks the characters might be feeling. Do you think he's afraid of the dark? Help your child test out different solutions to problems. When she is stuck, suggest other ways to approach the problem. For example, suggest she try different openings to fit the shapes into. If she needs a wand for pretend play, ask her what household object she might be able to use. Make up songs. Instead of Rain, Rain Go Away, suggest it can be Snow, Snow Go Away, or, Birthday, Birthday Almost Here. Ask your child what else he wants to make the song about. Change the words to the song to match his ideas. This helps your child learn to think logically and make connections between ideas. Bees are our friends, they help make food by pollinating flowers and they make honey for us to eat. 2. When bees are left alone, they are happy to do their job and are not dangerous. 3. When bees feel threatened by you or something around you, they will become upset and they can sting. 4. If bees start chasing you and buzz around your head, you need to leave that area as soon as you can. Run, don't hide! 5. Run to your class room, to your home, or to a store and go inside, whatever is closest to help get you away from the bees. Tell your teacher or the first adult you see that bees are chasing you and if you have been stung. They will know what to do. 6. If you see a large ball of bees hanging in a tree, leave them alone and tell an adult you saw them. 7. If you see bees flying in and out of a crack in a wall, a hole in a tree, or just about anywhere, leave them alone and leave the area quickly and tell an adult where they are. 8. Again, bees are our friends, but they can get very grumpy if they think someone is trying to hurt them and we do not want them to hurt you. Tell your friends about bee safety. When one develops this understanding they are able to recognize the letters and sounds in print and can read them. Beginning with short vowel sounds is the first step to teaching phonemes. This lesson will help children recognize a=/a/. "Lad and the Fat Cat" by Geri Murray (Powerpoint from Reading Genie site) http://www.auburn.edu/academic/education/reading_genie/Geniebooks/LadFatCat.ppt Tongue Twister: "Andrew and Alice asked if Annie's active animals were angry." Pictures of different things that have /a/ sound in (cat, bat, bag, crab, alligator) Picture of a snake - Begin the lesson by explaining why it is important to understand the letters in our language and what sounds the letters make. Explain that understanding letters and sounds helps us to read and how important reading is. Then explain that it is very important to focus on how our mouth moves when we make certain sounds. - Ask the students if they have ever seen a snake and said "Aaaaa!"? Ask, "What does your mouth do when you make that sound?" Explain that that is the mouth movement we are looking for in this lesson. Point at a picture of a snake and say, "Aaaa!" Have the students do the same. - Next try the tongue twister, "Andrew and Alice asked if Annie's active animals were angry." Have the students say it with you. Now let's try it again by stretching out the /a/ in the words. "Aaaandrew aaaaand Aaaaalice aaasked if Aaaanie's aaaacitve aaaanimals were aaaangry." Great job! Now let's say it by breaking off the /a/. "/a/ndrew /a/nd /a/lice /a/sked if /a/nnie's /a/ctive /a/nimals were /a/ngy." - Next get out the primary paper and pencils for the students to practice writing. We need to practice writing a. We need to start just below the fence, go up and touch the fence, down to the sidewalk, around and straight down. Now you practice. I am going to put a snake on your paper and I want you to write ten a's. - Now tell the students to signal that they saw a snake each time they see the letter a by itself in a word. Have them yell /a/. Ask the students if they hear /a/ in good or bad? Tell them that you hear /a/ in baaaad. Then ask the students which word they hear /a/ in. Bag or purse? Dog or cat? Bat or glove? - Begin with a book talk about "Lad and the Fat Cat." Lad is a dog and Scat is a cat. Lad is mad because Scat has his mat. Lad is mad because Scat can't get up. Why do you think Scat can't get up? To find out you'll have to read "Lad and the Fat Cat." Each time they hear /a/ tell them to act scared like when they see a snake. Have the students write a short message using a word with /a/ in it. Assess by using a running record as they read. - Assess again by giving them picture cards with things with /a/ in them and other cards that don't. Have them mark or color the ones that have /a/ Brittany Williams "Aaaaa! And I'm going to try to tell you about trig substitutions and trig integrals. And I'll be here tomorrow to do more of the same, as well. So, this is about trigonometry, and maybe first thing I'll do is remind you of some basic things about trigonometry. So, if I have a circle, trigonometry is all based on the circle of radius 1, and centered at the origin. And so if this is an angle of theta, up from the x-axis, then the coordinates of this point are cosine theta and sine theta. And so that leads right away to some trig identities, which you know very well. But I'm going to put them up here because we'll use them over and over again today. Remember the convention sin^2 theta secretly means (sin theta)^2. It would be more sensible to write a parenthesis around the sign of theta and then say you square that. But everybody in the world puts the 2 up there over the sin, and so I'll do that too. So that follows just because the circle has radius 1. But then there are some other identities too, which I think you remember. I'll write them down here. Cos 2 theta, there's this double angle formula that says cos 2 theta = cos ^2 theta - sin ^2 theta. And there's also the double angle formula for the sin 2 theta. Remember what that says? 2 sin theta cos theta. I'm going to use these trig identities and I'm going to use them in a slightly different way. In 2002 it moved to Milton Keynes and it 2004 it changed its name to Milton Keynes Dons F.C. Many football fans in Wimbledon did not agree with the move. [SIB goes over to play with airport] Title: How to Get Drinking Water from a Banana Tree Introduction: Banana trees are known for their delicious fruit, but they also have another useful feature - the ability to provide drinking water. This is especially helpful in survival situations or areas where clean water is scarce. Here's a detailed guide on how to extract potable water from a banana tree. Materials Needed: 1. A mature banana tree (at least 9 months old) 2. A sharp knife or machete 3. A container to collect the water 4. A piece of cloth or fine mesh to filter the water (optional) 5. Patience and caution Step 1: Identify the Right Tree Not all banana trees will yield drinkable water. The best candidates are those that are at least nine months old and show signs of maturity such as having large leaves and thick stems. Younger trees may not contain enough sap, while older ones might be too fibrous. Key Tip: Choose a healthy tree free from diseases or pests which could contaminate the extracted water. Step 2: Cut Down the Tree Using your sharp tool, cut down the entire tree as close to the base as possible. Be careful during this process; ensure you're standing away from the falling direction of the tree to avoid injury. Guideline: It's recommended to do this early morning when temperatures are cooler and the tree contains more liquid. Step 3: Let the Sap Flow After cutting, let the tree lay flat on the ground for about an hour. During this time, the sap will start flowing out from the top of the stem. You can make incisions around the stem if necessary to speed up the flow. Key Tip: Never try to pull or force the trunk after cutting. This could result in damage, reducing the amount of collected water. Step 4: Collect the Sap Place your collection vessel under the cut end of the stem. As gravity pulls the sap downward, it should drip into your container. Depending on the size of the tree, you could gather between one to four liters of sap. Guideline: If there isn't much flow initially, wait longer or consider making additional cuts near the base of the stem. Step 5: Filter the Water (Optional) While not always necessary, filtering the water through a clean cloth or fine mesh can remove any debris or impurities. Key Tip: Always err on the side of safety when deciding whether to filter. Impure water can lead to illnesses. Step 6: Consume Immediately OR Store Properly Fresh banana tree sap tastes sweet but turns bitter over time due to oxidation. Therefore, consume it immediately for best taste. However, if storage is required, keep it refrigerated and use within 24 hours. Guideline: Boiling the water before consumption kills bacteria, making it safer to drink. But remember, boiled sap loses its refreshing taste. Conclusion: Extracting drinking water from a banana tree is straightforward once you know what to do. With patience, care, and the right tools, you can turn this common plant into a valuable source of hydration. Remember though, nothing beats access to safe, regularly tested public water supplies! Title: How to Center Text in Microsoft Word Microsoft Word is a powerful word processing tool that offers various formatting options, including text alignment. Centering text can help you create visually appealing documents by balancing the content on the page. This tutorial will provide an in-depth guide on centering text in Microsoft Word, ensuring clarity and practicality for users of all levels. **Step 1: Open your document in Microsoft Word** To begin, launch Microsoft Word on your computer and open the document where you want to center text. Having the correct document open ensures that any changes made will be applied only to the intended file. **Step 2: Select the text you wish to center** Click anywhere within the paragraph or block of text that needs centering. If you want to select multiple lines or entire sections, click and drag your cursor across the desired area until everything is highlighted. Alternatively, press 'Ctrl + Shift + Right Arrow' (Windows) or 'Cmd + Shift + Right Arrow' (Mac) while positioned at the beginning of the text to highlight from your current position to the end of the line(s). Properly selecting the text allows you to make uniform adjustments efficiently without manually reformatting individual characters. ![Correct Selection]( Here here here or here or here there? Title: How to Get a Boyfriend in 7th Grade Introduction: Middle school can be an exciting time as you start to navigate new social situations and develop romantic interests. If you're in 7th grade and looking to get a boyfriend, this guide will provide you with practical steps, key tips, and guidelines to help you along the way. Remember, every individual is unique, so what works for one person might not work for another. The most important thing is to stay true to yourself and respect others throughout the process. Step 1: Build Self-Confidence * Explanation: Having self-confidence is attractive and increases your chances of finding a compatible partner. It also allows you to better handle rejection or setbacks. * Tips: Practice positive self-talk, engage in activities that make you feel good about yourself, maintain good hygiene, dress comfortably yet stylishly, and surround yourself with supportive friends. Step 2: Identify Your Ideal Partner * Explanation: Understanding what qualities you value in a potential boyfriend can help you focus on individuals who share similar interests and values. * Guidelines: Consider factors such as personality traits, hobbies, goals, and communication styles when thinking about your ideal partner. Avoid focusing solely on physical appearance. Step 3: Expand Your Social Circle * Explanation: Meeting new people increases your chances of encountering someone who could become your boyfriend. * Key Tips: Join clubs, participate in extracurricular activities, attend school events, and volunteer in community organizations to meet like-minded individuals. Step 4: Initiate Conversations * Explanation: Starting conversations shows genuine interest in getting to know someone and can lead to meaningful connections. * Tips: Ask open-ended questions, listen actively, show empathy, and find common ground to build rapport. Step 5: Spend Time Together * Explanation: Building a strong friendship foundation before pursuing a relationship can increase compatibility and strengthen bonds. * Guidelines: Engage in group activities, suggest one-on-one outings, and create shared experiences to deepen your connection. Step 6: Express Your Feelings * Explanation: Communicating your feelings honestly and respectfully is crucial when transitioning from friendship to romance. * Key Tips: Choose the right timing and place, be clear and concise, and prepare for different reactions. If rejected, remember that it's okay and move forward with grace. Step 7: Develop the Relationship * Explanation: Once a mutual understanding has been established, continue nurturing the relationship through quality time, effective communication, and emotional support. * Tips: Set boundaries, discuss expectations, practice forgiveness, and celebrate milestones together. Conclusion: Finding a boyfriend in 7th grade involves building self-confidence, expanding your social circle, initiating conversations, spending time together, expressing your feelings, and developing the relationship. By following these steps and remaining authentic, you'll increase your likelihood of attracting a compatible partner while fostering personal growth and resilience. You want to put peas on your head?
