[Nina hides the rattle in one of the two fists that she holds out in front of herself.] [a few of the bolts fall off the wand, some of which stick to Chi's construction] [walks toward the door and tries to reach the knob] Title: How to Access Archive Photos Accessing archive photos can be an exciting journey through time, as you rediscover memories that have been tucked away. This tutorial will guide you through accessing your archive photos with ease and efficiency. Whether you're looking for old family pictures or historical documents, this step-by-step process is designed to help you find what you need. **Step 1: Identify Your Photo Source** Before diving into the actual archival process, first determine where your photos are stored. Are they digital files saved on your computer or external hard drive? Or perhaps they exist only in physical form - albums, boxes, slides, etc. The location of these photos will dictate which method(s) you should use to access them. Key Tip: Keep track of all locations so nothing gets overlooked during the search. **Step 2: Digitize Physical Photos (Optional)** If your photos are primarily analog formats like prints or negatives, consider digitizing them for easier organization and sharing. You can do this yourself using various devices such as flatbed scanners, film scanners, or even smartphone apps specifically designed for photo scanning. Alternatively, many professional services offer high-quality digitization at affordable prices. Guideline: When choosing a digitization method, prioritize quality over speed. High-resolution scans may take longer but provide better results. **Step 3: Consolidate Digital Files** Gather all your digital photos from different sources (computers, phones, cloud storage, memory cards, etc.) onto one central device or platform. Having everything together simplifies searching and categorization. Key Tip: Use automatic backup systems like Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud to ensure no new memories get lost. **Step 4: Organize Your Collection** Create folders based on criteria meaningful to you - chronology, event type, people involved, etc. Within each folder, further sort items by date taken if possible. Most operating systems allow multiple levels of subfolders. Guideline: Develop a consistent naming convention for your files. It makes tracking down specific images much simpler. Example: YYYY_MM_DD_EventName_Location **Step 5: Utilize Metadata** Metadata refers to data about data - information embedded within image files detailing when and where they were captured, camera settings used, copyright details, etc. Some software allows editing of metadata fields; add keywords related to content to aid future searches. Key Tip: Don't rely solely on metadata. Always tag and title your files clearly too. **Step 6: Employ Image Recognition Software** Certain applications employ AI technology to recognize faces, places, objects, text, and more in your photos. Leveraging these features can significantly expedite your search process once initial organization has been completed. Guideline: Before uploading large collections to third-party servers, review privacy policies carefully. **Step 7: Regularly Review & Update** Periodically review your archive to fill gaps, remove duplicates, update titles/tags, and generally maintain order. Think of it as spring cleaning for your memories! Key Tip: Set aside dedicated time annually or semi-annually for this task. By following these steps meticulously, you'll create an accessible, well-organized photographic archive ready for exploration whenever curiosity strikes. Happy reminiscing! Later in large stars, heavier elements are made by fusion. Finally the star makes a supernova explosion. Most things happen in the universe so slowly we do not notice. But supernova explosions happen in only 100 seconds. When a supernova explodes its flash is as bright as a 100 billion stars. The dying star is so bright it can be seen during the day. Supernova means "new star" because people used to think it was the beginning of a new star. Today we know that a supernova is the death of an old star. The gas of the star is pushed away by the explosion. It forms a giant cloud of gas called a planetary nebula. The crab nebula is a good example. All that remains is a neutron star. If the star was very heavy, the star will make a black hole. Gravity in a black hole is extremely strong. It is so strong that even light cannot escape from a black hole. [removes something else from the container and shows it to Me] Atwood's machine is a device where two masses, M and m, are connected by a string passing over a pulley. Assume that M > m. What is the acceleration of the two masses? Start with a good free-body diagram. Two, in fact, one for each mass. Assume the pulley is frictionless and massless, which means the tension is the same everywhere in the string. We'll learn how to account for the pulley later in the course. T1 = T2 = T Think about what the system will do. If the system is released from rest, the heavy mass will accelerate down and the lighter one will accelerate up. Align the coordinate systems with the acceleration. Each mass has its own coordinate system, but they must be consistent. Take +y down for mass M. Take +y up for mass m. Recognize that the masses have the same acceleration, a. Apply Newton's second law for each mass. |For mass M:|||||For mass m:| |SFy = May|||||SFy = may| |Mg - T = Ma|||||T - mg = ma| Combine the equations to eliminate T. T = mg + ma, so: Mg - mg - ma = Ma a (M + m) = g (M - m) Check your answer by plugging in some limiting cases: Numerical example: M = 210 g and m = 200 g. Title: Convince Your Parents So They Can Trust You Introduction: Gaining your parents' trust is an essential aspect of growing up and becoming more independent. Building this trust requires time, effort, communication, consistency, and responsible behavior. This guide will provide you with steps and strategies to help convince your parents to trust you. Remember that every child-parent relationship is unique; therefore, some methods might work better than others depending on your specific situation. Step 1: Understand Their Perspective Parents often worry about their children because they care deeply for them. It would be best if you acknowledged their concerns and understood where they are coming from. By empathizing with them, you can begin addressing any issues or fears they may have. Key Tip: Listen actively when your parents express their worries and respond calmly without getting defensive. Showing maturity during these conversations will make a positive impression on them. Step 2: Evaluate Your Behavior Analyze your past actions and identify areas where you could improve. Consider whether there have been instances where you broke rules, lied, or failed to meet expectations. Recognize how those behaviors impacted your parents' trust in you. Guideline: Reflect on both minor and major transgressions, as even small infractions can accumulate over time and erode trust. Step 3: Set Clear Expectations Have open discussions with your parents about what they expect from you regarding responsibility, honesty, and communication. Make sure to ask questions and clarify anything you don't understand. Key Tip: Discuss mutual goals and values to create common ground between you and your parents. Shared objectives will encourage teamwork and collaboration towards rebuilding trust. Step 4: Create A Plan Together Collaborate with your parents to develop a plan outlining concrete steps towards regaining their trust. The strategy should include short-term and long-term goals along with measurable outcomes. Example: If your goal is to demonstrate greater reliability, suggest creating a chore chart or establishing regular check-ins to discuss progress. Over time, these consistent efforts will show your dedication to change. Step 5: Implement The Plan Put your plan into action by consistently following through on commitments and demonstrating improved behavior. Hold yourself accountable for meeting expectations outlined in your agreement with your parents. Guideline: Keep track of accomplishments and setbacks using a journal or calendar to monitor growth and maintain motivation. Step 6: Communicate Regularly Maintain ongoing dialogue with your parents about your progress and challenges. Share successes openly while also discussing moments of struggle honestly. Use these opportunities to learn from mistakes rather than hiding them. Key Tip: Schedule weekly meetings or informal check-ins to ensure both parties stay engaged in the process of building trust. Consistent communication fosters understanding and strengthens relationships. Step 7: Seek Feedback And Adjust Accordingly Ask your parents for feedback regularly so you can adjust your approach as needed. Welcoming constructive criticism shows humility and commitment to improvement. Guideline: Remain patient throughout this process, recognizing that restoring trust takes time and perseverance. Stay focused on making steady progress instead of expecting immediate results. Conclusion: Rebuilding parental trust involves self-reflection, setting clear expectations, collaborative planning, consistent implementation, open communication, seeking feedback, and patience. By taking proactive steps toward earning back your parents' confidence, you'll foster a stronger bond based on respect, understanding, and mutual support. Comparing prices on Frontline Plus is an excellent way to ensure that you're getting the best deal on this popular flea and tick prevention product for your pets. By taking the time to compare prices, you can save money while still providing your furry friends with the protection they need. Here's our comprehensive, step-by-step guide on how to do just that: ### Step 1: Research various retailers that carry Frontline Plus Before diving into comparing prices, first identify which stores or websites sell Frontline Plus. This may include pet supply stores like Petco and Petsmart, as well as online marketplaces such as Amazon, Chewy, and Walmart. Make a list of these retailers, so you have multiple options to explore when looking for the best price. Key tip: Keep in mind that some retailers might offer exclusive discount codes or promotions, making their prices even more competitive. Be sure to check if any available coupons could lower the cost further. ### Step 2: Determine which size and quantity of Frontline Plus you require Frontline Plus comes in different sizes based on your pet's weight. To effectively compare prices, determine the right size and quantity needed for your pet(s). Typically, one application (or vial) lasts about a month. You can purchase packs containing three, six, or twelve months' worth of applications at once. Guideline: Always double-check the packaging details before purchasing to make certain you are buying the appropriate size and dosage for your pet. ### Step 3: Visit the websites or physical locations of your selected retailers Now that you know where to look and what you need, visit the chosen retailer's website or store. If visiting brick-and-mortar shops, note down the prices listed on shelves or ask sales associates for assistance. When shopping online, navigate directly to the Frontline Plus product page. Important guideline: Remember that shipping costs may vary between online retailers; factor those charges into your final decision to get accurate pricing comparisons. Some sites may provide free shipping depending upon order value or membership status. ### Step 4: Record the prices of your preferred Frontline Plus products Create a table or spreadsheet listing all the retailers from your initial research along with the corresponding prices observed during step 3. Include columns for additional factors like shipping fees, tax calculations, and applicable promo codes. Key tip: Regularly update this chart since prices may change frequently due to sales, promotions, or stock availability fluctuations. ### Step 5: Analyze the data collected Carefully review the compiled information to pinpoint the most affordable option among your choices. Consider both the base price and total cost after accounting for taxes, shipping fees, and other potential expenses. Also, take into account whether bulk purchases result in significant savings per unit compared to smaller quantities. Guideline: Don't forget to consider customer service reputation and return policies as part of your evaluation process, especially if you plan to buy large quantities or become a repeat customer. ### Step 6: Complete your purchase Once you have identified the best deal, proceed to complete your transaction securely by entering required payment and delivery details. Double-check everything before submitting your order to avoid errors or misunderstandings. Important reminder: Save a copy of your receipt and track your shipment until it arrives safely. Store the package properly according to its storage requirements until ready for use. By following these steps diligently, you will successfully compare prices on Frontline Plus and ultimately find the best possible deal for your pet care needs. Happy shopping! Changing an RSC base light bulb to an E27 base (also known as converting a halogen bulb to an LED) is a simple process that can be completed in just a few steps. This conversion will not only save you money on your energy bills but also reduce your carbon footprint. Here's our comprehensive guide: **Things You'll Need:** 1. A new E27-based LED light bulb 2. Rubbing alcohol or cleaning spray 3. Cleaning cloth or paper towel 4. Screwdriver (if necessary) **Step 1: Turn Off the Power Supply** Before starting any electrical work, ensure that the power supply to the fixture is turned off at the circuit breaker or fuse box to avoid electric shock. It's essential for safety purposes. **Step 2: Remove the Old Halogen Bulb** Carefully remove the old halogen bulb by twisting it counterclockwise until it loosens from its socket. If it doesn't budge, try using gloves or tissue paper to get a better grip. Remember, these bulbs can get hot even when they've been switched off for some time, so exercise caution. **Step 3: Clean the Socket** Use rubbing alcohol or cleaning spray and a clean cloth or paper towel to wipe down the exposed contacts inside the socket. Dust and dirt buildup over time may hinder proper contact between the new LED bulb and the socket. Keeping this area clean ensures optimal performance and longevity of the new LED bulb. **Step 4: Understanding Compatibility** Make sure the voltage rating of the new LED bulb matches your home's electrical system. Most residential properties operate on either 110V or 220V systems. Also, check if there are any wattage restrictions listed on the lamp holder - many older fixtures have maximum wattages lower than modern lamps due to their construction materials. **Step 5: Inserting the New LED Bulb** Take your new E27-based LED bulb and insert it into the cleaned socket. Twist it clockwise until it feels snug but do not force it; excessive tightening could damage the internal components of the bulb or fixture. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** - Always read and follow manufacturer instructions provided with both your fixture and replacement bulb. - Never exceed the recommended wattage for your lighting fixture. Overloading can cause fires. - When dealing with electricity, always prioritize safety first. Don't hesitate to call a professional if you feel uncomfortable performing these tasks yourself. - Dispose of used CFL and halogen bulbs responsibly according to local regulations. These types of bulbs contain hazardous materials and should not be thrown away with regular household waste. By following these easy steps and guidelines, you're now well equipped to change your RSC base halogen bulb to an E27 base LED one! Enjoy your energy-efficient, cost-effective, and eco-friendly lighting solution. [Sarah seems unsure whether to answer yes or no] I'm goin' use it all up them. "Not me. When objects are in orbit around each other, there is a strong pull of gravity between them. For example, we commonly say that the Moon is in orbit around the Earth. However, the Moon pulls back on the Earth as well. This changes the Earth a little. One way we see this happening is the ocean tides. The amount of gravity between two objects depends on the masses of the objects, and also the distance between them. When you have two very large objects like the Moon and the Earth, different parts of them are pulled differently due to distance. The part of the Earth closest to the moon is pulled most. If there are oceans there, they will bulge out. The middle of the Earth is not pulled as strongly towards the moon, but it still is pulled more than the water on the far surface of the Earth. The far side of the Earth is pulled least, making another bulge on the far side of the Earth. As the Earth rotates underneath these bulges, we experience two high tides per day. Title: Solving Fun Trigonometry Puzzles with Your Calculator Hi there, young math enthusiasts! Today, we're going to learn how to solve a fun trigonometry equation puzzle using our calculators. This will help us understand how tangent (tan) and cotangent (cot) functions work together. Let's dive in! You might have seen equations like "solve for x: tan(x) - cot(x) = 0" before. The goal here is to find all the possible solutions for x within a specific interval, like [0, 360). Don't worry if those terms sound complicated; just think of them as different ways to describe angles. First, make sure your calculator is set to **DEGREES**, not radians. You can change this setting through its settings or modes menu. Now, let's see why this matters. When you calculate tan(225), you expect to get the same result as cot(225), because our given equation says that both should be equal. However, something strange happens when you try to compute cot(225): instead of giving you the same number, you get a completely different value! That's frustrating, but don't panic - this discrepancy occurs due to a common mistake when working with trigonometry functions. Here's the secret: *calculators treat cot(x) differently than tan^-1(x)*! To clarify, type $\cot(x) = \frac{1}{\tan(x)}$ on your calculator display, rather than $\tan^{-1}(x)$. When you input $\cot(225)$, your calculator computes the reciprocal of $\tan(225)$, resulting in the correct answer. Remembering this trick will save you time and headaches while solving similar problems in the future. Now that we know how to handle tangents and cotangents correctly, let's go back to solving our original puzzle. We need to find the values of $x$ between $[0, 360)$ satisfying the equation $\tan(x) - \cot(x) = 0$. By rewriting the equation as $\tan(x) = \cot(x)$, we realize that each side represents the reciprocal of another. Thus, $x$ must satisfy both $\tan(x)=\tan(\alpha)$ and $\cot(x)=\cot(\alpha)$ for some angle $\alpha$, making $x=\alpha$ or $x=180-\alpha$. Our textbook gives us four special angle values {45, 135, 225, 315}. Using the properties mentioned earlier, we can confirm that these indeed meet the conditions described above. So congratulations! With your newfound knowledge and skills, you too can tackle seemingly complex trigonometry problems confidently. Happy calculating! Recommended by Stacy DeVeau, Arizona NASA Educator Resource Center, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University This activity will help us to better understand the distances between the planets and their distances from the sun in our solar system. As students build their models, show planetary images from solar system lithographs in binder and read solar system trading card facts, or ask them what they recall about each body. Students may color their planets as they label them. 1. Start with a length of register tape the span of the letter "V" made with your arms (~1m). 2. Sun & Pluto (on the edges): 3. Uranus (1/2): 4. Saturn (1/4) and Neptune (3/4): 5. Jupiter (1/8): 6. Asteroid Belt (1/16): 7. Earth (inside 1/32), Mars (outside 1/32): 8. Mercury & Venus (between Earth & Sun): Be sure to have everyone put their names on their tapes and fold them up to put it in their pockets. But before you put them away, here are some questions you might ask to get them thinking about what they can get from building this model. 1. Are there any surprises? Look how empty the outer solar system is: there is a reason they call it space! And how crowded the inner solar system is (relatively speaking). Even though it looks crowded in this model, are our closest planets very close? What's the farthest distance humans have ever travelled away from Earth? The moon! 2. Do you know anything about the physical properties of the ones that are spread out versus the ones that are crowded in close to the Sun? All the inner ones are small and rocky and the outer ones are gassy giants (except small, icy Pluto). 3. Given this spacing, why do you think little, rocky Venus can outshine giant Jupiter in the night sky? Both are covered with highly reflective clouds, and although it is much smaller, Venus is also much, much closer. 4. Does anyone know where the Eris, the largest dwarf planet would go on this model? At 97 A.U., it would more than double the size of the model. Pluto is on average 40 A.U. [A.U. stands for Astronomical Unit, roughly the mean distance from the Earth to the Sun. 1 AU = 149,597,870.691 kilometers, or about 93 million miles.] 5. On this scale (1 m = 40 A.U.) where would the nearest star* be? After some guesses you could bring out your pocket calculator to use in getting how far away the star would be. This allows you to talk about how far is a light year and do the calculations to find that the next nearest star on this scale is about 7 km (4.2 miles) away. They could then take out a local map to see what is that far away from the class. You'd need a lot more register tape! 6. [Calculations: A light year, the distance light travels in one year, is about 63,240 A.U. (about 9,460,000,000,000 km). The nearest star is *Proxima Centauri (visible from the Southern Hemisphere), at 4.2 light years. So, 4.2 l.y. x 63,240 A.U./l.y. x 1 m/ 40 A.U. = 6640.2 m = about 7 km.] 7. In Roman times, Pliny the Elder said Trevi was a city of the old Umbrians, and an old stone with Umbrian writing was found in the comune, at Bovara, in the 1950s. ""Treviae"" is also listed in the 5th century Bordeaux Itinerary. We do not know the history of Trevi in very old times, although some walls in the center part of the town on the hill are as old as the 1st century BC. H1N1 Flu (Swine Flu) The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has reported cases of the swine flu in the United States. Cases have also been reported in other countries, including Mexico and Canada. The swine flu virus is mainly spread from person to person through coughing or sneezing. Here's what you can do to protect yourself: Avoid close contact with others who are sick. Wash your hands often with soap and water, especially after you cough or sneeze. Alcohol-based hands cleaners also work well. Avoid touching your eyes, nose, or mouth. Germs spread that way. Symptoms of the swine flu include a cough, sore throat, fever, body aches, chills, and extreme tiredness (fatigue). Some people have had vomiting and diarrhea. Most people have mild symptoms. But swine flu can lead to pneumonia in severe cases, and it may make other health problems worse. An infected person can infect others 1 day before symptoms start and for 7 days after getting sick. That means people can spread swine flu before they know they have it. If you do get sick with flu-like symptoms: - Talk to your doctor. You may need to be tested. - Stay home from school or work to avoid infecting other people. - Cover your nose and mouth with a tissue when you cough or sneeze. Throw the tissue in the trash after you use it. For more information, go to the CDC Web site at: http://www.cdc.gov/swineflu. She bothers me in the kitchen a lot haha when I cook dinner. You turned the light off to eat? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Peters/011125b.cha = = = How do we get oil and gas out of the ground? Oil and gas can get trapped in pockets underground such as where the rocks are folded into an umbrella shape. Oil and gas can move through the porous rocks (rocks with gaps between the grains). The oil and gas move upwards from the source rock where they were formed. When they met a layer of cap rock (a rock with no spaces between the grains) the oil and gas are trapped. A well is drilled so that the crude oil and other liquids travel up the bore hole. When it comes to the surface the crude oil has to be moved closer to where it is needed. Finding oil and gas trapped deep underground and drilling a well are very complicated and expensive. It costs millions of pound to drill a well and only a few are successful. The liquids found underground can be a complicated mixture of water, crude oil and gas. The crude oil and gas need to be separated before they can be transported safely. Which rocks are porous and could make good reservoir rock? Plan an investigation to find out which rocks are most porous. Can you find out how the people who search for oil discover oil traps deep beneath the land and sea bed? How are oil and gas transported? Oil and gas are often found far away or under the sea. They have to be transported to an oil refinery. This is often through a pipeline or in a tanker. The crude oil is often found in remote places such as deserts, jungle or the Arctic. Transport of the crude oil to the refinery is sometimes very complicated. The oil refinery turns crude oil into useful products and materials. These are transported all over Britain or abroad. The products can travel through pipelines, by road, rail or by boats around the coast or along rivers and canals. << Previous | Next >> In addition to controlling blood glucose levels, other treatments may be needed. Diabetics often have blood vessel diseases, so it is important to pay attention to other diseases that may affect blood vessels. In people with diabetes, treating high blood pressure (hypertension) and high cholesterol is more important than usual. Both of these diseases damage blood vessels. The treatment goals can change for diabetics. For instance, in people without diabetes, blood pressure should be 140/90 or less. In diabetics, it should be 130/80 or less. We All Live in a Watershed Land is very important to the water cycle. A WATERSHED is any area of land that water flows across or through. Where is the water going? Downhill of course. Water in a watershed trickles and flows toward a common body of water, such as a stream, river, lake or coast. Watersheds can be big or small, but they usually have high points (like ridges) as their upper boundaries. So watersheds capture water, store it and eventually release it farther downhill.What sort of land is your watershed made of? It's easy to find out. First you need to know if the land where you live is steep or flat. - Steep land makes water run off in fast-moving creeks and rushing rivers. - Flat land allows water to collect into lakes, ponds and swamps. Now think about what happens when you dig a small hole. Do you find solid rock, fine silt or dirt that is loose and sandy? - Loose ground (like gravel or sand) is POROUS, which means it has lots of little gaps that water can easily sink into. When water sinks into the ground, we call it GROUND WATER, and it fills an underground water supply called an AQUIFER. - Tightly packed ground, like clay or solid rock, isn't very porous at all, so it's hard for water to move through. Layers like that can trap water at the surface or below ground. Read on: Green and Leafy and Important His bread doesn't even look like this. She went right for those puzzles though. The man gave me a broken one. Title: How to Add Tags in WordPress Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of adding tags in WordPress. By following these steps, you'll be able to categorize your content more efficiently, improve SEO, and enable users to find related posts quickly. We have also included essential tips and guidelines to ensure clarity and ease of application. Step 1: Log into Your WordPress Admin Dashboard Visit <http://yourwebsite.com>/wp-admin (replace 'yourwebsite.com' with your actual domain name) using any web browser, then enter your username and password to log in. This action grants access to your WordPress admin dashboard, which is where all website management tasks take place. Step 2: Navigate to Posts > All Posts From the left sidebar within the WordPress admin dashboard, hover over the 'Posts' option and click on 'All Posts.' The page that appears displays an overview of every post published on your site. It allows easy navigation between existing articles while offering options for editing or deleting them as needed. Step 3: Select a Post to Edit or Create New One To add tags to an existing post, locate it from the list displayed under 'All Posts,' click on its title, and proceed directly to Step 4. To create a new post, simply select 'Add New' atop the 'All Posts' screen instead. Once you are inside the editor interface, continue to Step 4. Step 4: Locate the Tag Metabox Scroll down below the text editor area until you see the 'Tags' metabox located typically on the right side of the screen. If you don't see it initially, ensure that you haven't accidentally collapsed or hidden any sections by clicking on the small triangle icon near the top of the column. Step 5: Enter Relevant Tags Click inside the input field labeled 'Enter new tag here' and type one keyword or phrase per line representing what best describes the topic(s) discussed in your article. Separating keywords ensures they function independently; otherwise, they will remain connected via commas. As you begin typing, WordPress may suggest previously used tags - consider selecting those if applicable. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Use descriptive words or phrases relevant to your content. * Limit yourself to 10-15 tags per post. * Utilize low competition, high search volume keywords when possible. * Avoid repetition across multiple tags (e.g., singular vs plural forms). * Keep your tags consistent throughout the entire blog. Step 6: Save Changes Once satisfied with your chosen tags, there are two ways to save changes depending on whether you're working on an existing post or creating a new one: For Existing Posts: Click on 'Update' found towards the upper right corner above the text editor. A loading animation will appear momentarily followed by a success message confirming that updates were saved successfully. For New Posts: After entering your title and body content, click on 'Publish' situated next to the 'Update' button mentioned earlier. Similar to updating, a brief loading phase occurs before revealing the confirmation notice. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have now learned how to effectively add tags in WordPress. Properly implementing tags enhances user experience and contributes positively to search engine optimization efforts. Remember always to utilize descriptive yet limited tags relevant to each specific piece of content created. Happy blogging! That's dirty you made the spoon dirty. [places mouth to toothbrush on the page of the book] How to Keep the Romantic Spark Alive with Your Partner after Children Move Out Introduction: As children grow up and move out of the house, many couples find themselves facing an empty nest. This transition can be both exciting and challenging as you rediscover your relationship without the daily responsibilities of raising children. To keep the romantic spark alive during this new phase of life, consider implementing the following steps. Step 1: Communicate Openly Key Tip: Schedule regular check-ins to discuss feelings, thoughts, and goals related to your post-children lifestyle. Open communication is essential to maintaining a healthy relationship. With kids no longer living at home, it's crucial to establish open lines of dialogue about personal aspirations, shared interests, and any concerns that arise. Regular check-ins provide structure for these discussions while fostering emotional intimacy between partners. Step 2: Establish New Shared Interests Key Guideline: Identify hobbies or activities that both partners enjoy participating in together. Sharing experiences strengthens bonds between individuals by creating lasting memories and promoting mutual understanding. By exploring new hobbies or reviving old ones, couples can reignite their connection and develop deeper levels of appreciation for one another. Step 3: Prioritize Quality Time Together Key Tip: Set aside dedicated time each week for date nights or other enjoyable activities. Making quality time a priority ensures that couples continue nurturing their relationships amidst busy schedules. Whether attending concerts, cooking classes, or simply enjoying a quiet evening at home, consistent dates foster closeness and reinforce affectionate feelings towards each other. Step 4: Show Appreciation Daily Key Guideline: Express gratitude and admiration through words, gestures, or small gifts. Demonstrating appreciation keeps romance alive by reminding both partners why they fell in love in the first place. Simple acts like leaving loving notes, giving unexpected hugs, or bringing home favorite treats create moments of joy and affirm commitment to the relationship. Step 5: Rebuild Physical Intimacy Key Tip: Gradually reintegrate physical touch into your interactions, such as holding hands, cuddling, or massaging. Physical intimacy plays a significant role in keeping the romantic spark alive. As children leave home, couples may need to reestablish comfortable boundaries around physical contact. Slowly incorporating gentle touches into everyday routines can help ease this process and promote increased closeness over time. Step 6: Plan Future Adventures Together Key Guideline: Discuss joint goals and dreams, then make plans accordingly. Looking forward to shared experiences creates anticipation and excitement within a relationship. By setting future travel destinations, learning opportunities, or milestone celebrations, couples maintain enthusiasm and motivation to invest in their partnership. Step 7: Seek Professional Help if Needed Key Tip: Consider counseling or therapy sessions to address lingering issues or deepen connections further. If challenges persist despite efforts to improve communication, engage in shared activities, prioritize quality time, show appreciation, rebuild physical intimacy, and plan adventures together, seeking professional guidance might be beneficial. Therapists specializing in couple dynamics offer valuable insights and strategies tailored specifically to individual needs, ensuring continued growth and development throughout all stages of life. Conclusion: With dedication and effort, it is possible to preserve the romantic spark even after children have moved out of the house. Implementing these seven steps lays a strong foundation for ongoing happiness and fulfillment within your relationship, ultimately leading to greater satisfaction and contentment for both partners. Title: How to Perform the Finger Slap and Appear Card Trick The finger slip and appear card trick is an impressive magic trick that involves making a selected card disappear from the deck and reappearing under your fingers with just a snap. This trick requires some practice but can be mastered over time. Here's a detailed guide on how to do it. **Step 1: Choose Your Card** Ask a spectator to pick a card from the deck. Make sure they remember their choice as you will need them to identify it later. *Why this step matters:* Selecting a card creates personal involvement and anticipation for both the performer and audience member. It adds excitement because there's always curiosity about where the chosen card might end up. **Step 2: Control the Selection** After the spectator chooses a card, secretly control it so it becomes the top or bottom card of the deck without letting anyone notice. You can use various techniques like a classic force, riffle force, or simply ask them to place it back on top or bottom of the pack. *Why this step matters:* By controlling the selection, you ensure that you know which card has been picked. This way, when you make it magically reappear, it reinforces the illusion of real magic happening right before their eyes. **Step 3: Display the Deck** Hold the deck face down between your thumb and fingers, showing all sides to prove nothing unusual is going on. The controlled card should either be at the top or bottom depending on what comes next. *Why this step matters:* Showing all sides of the deck builds trust by demonstrating transparency and eliminating suspicion of hidden preparations. **Step 4: Prepare for the Snap Change** If your controlled card is at the bottom, flip the entire deck over so it's now facing upward. Use your dominant hand to hold the deck vertically, applying gentle pressure to bend the cards slightly. Position your index finger along the outer short edge near the break (where the two halves meet). Keep your middle finger close to your index finger ready to assist. *Why this step matters:* Proper positioning sets the stage for executing the actual finger snap change effectively. **Step 5: Execute the Finger Snap Change** Quickly pull away your index finger while simultaneously pushing forward with your thumb causing the top half of the deck to move backward momentarily. As soon as this happens, bring your index finger back into contact with the new top card while releasing the pressure applied earlier. With practice, timing and coordination create an instantaneous switch - giving the appearance that one card turned into another. *Key Tips*: - Practice the motion slowly first until you get comfortable with the movement. - Apply minimal force; too much could cause multiple cards to shift instead of just one. - Timing is crucial: pulling back your index finger must coincide exactly with the push from your thumb. **Step 6: Reveal the Selected Card** While maintaining eye contact with the spectator, turn your hand over revealing the original top card as if expecting something different. When surprised looks arise, smile confidently knowing you've successfully performed the trick! *Why this step matters:* Uncovering the supposed 'new' top card, followed by a convincing reaction, seals the deal, leaving everyone amazed. In conclusion, performing the finger slap and appear card trick takes patience, dexterity, and showmanship. Follow these steps closely, rehearse regularly, and enjoy amazing friends and family with your newly acquired magical skillset! The rails are supported by cross pieces set at regular intervals (called sleepers or ties), which spread the high pressure load imposed by the train wheels into the ground. They also maintain the rails at a fixed distance apart (called the gauge). Ties are usually made from either wood or concrete. These often rest on ballast, which is a name for very small pieces of broken up rock that are packed together and keep the railway tracks in place. Tracks are often made better by ballast tampers. Title: How to Have an Indoor Camp Out An indoor camp out is a fun and creative way to enjoy the camping experience without leaving the comfort of your home. This activity can be done alone or with family and friends, making it perfect for bonding time during rainy days or when you simply want to try something new. Here's our comprehensive guide on having an exciting and memorable indoor camp out. ### Step 1: Choose a Suitable Space Key Tip: Select a spacious area where you can set up your campsite. A living room or a large playroom would work great! Make sure there's enough space for all participants to move around comfortably. Choosing a suitable space creates a more enjoyable atmosphere as everyone will have ample room for activities. ### Step 2: Gather Necessary Materials To create an authentic camping feel, gather materials such as: - Blankets (to serve as makeshift tents) - Pillows (for seating and cushioning) - Flashlights (or battery-operated candles) - Sleeping bags (optional but adds to the camping ambiance) - Portable stove/grill (for cooking s'mores or other simple meals) - Snacks and drinks - Board games, books, or cards (for entertainment) Having these items readily available ensures that you have everything needed for a successful indoor camp out. ### Step 3: Set Up Your Campsite Create a cozy tent using blankets draped over furniture like couches, chairs, or tables. You may also build a fort using pillows and sheets if preferred. Arrange sleeping bags inside the tent for added realism. Setting up a comfortable and inviting campsite encourages relaxation and promotes engagement in camping activities. ### Step 4: Prepare Meals Plan simple yet delicious meals that can be cooked indoors. Grilled cheese sandwiches, hot dogs, or even s'mores made using a portable stove or microwave are excellent options. Cooking together fosters teamwork and allows everyone to participate in meal preparation. ### Step 5: Engage in Fun Activities Organize various camping-themed activities such as storytelling, singalongs, stargazing (using a planetarium app), board games, or craft projects. These engaging pastimes help replicate the traditional camping experience while providing hours of entertainment. ### Step 6: Establish Camp Rules Set ground rules to maintain order and ensure safety throughout the indoor camp out. Examples include no electronics usage, designated quiet times, and proper clean-up procedures after meals. Clear expectations promote harmony among participants and contribute to an overall positive atmosphere. ### Step 7: Enjoy Quality Time Together Take advantage of this unique opportunity to connect with loved ones by sharing stories, playing games, or simply enjoying each other's company within the confines of your homemade campsite. Strengthening relationships through shared experiences is one of the most rewarding aspects of any camping adventure - indoor or otherwise! By following these steps, you'll successfully host an unforgettable indoor camp out filled with laughter, learning, and lasting memories. Happy camping! Clear Memorized Answers on a Texas Instruments TI-30XA Calculator The Texas Instruments TI-30XA calculator is a popular choice among students and professionals due to its wide range of features and functions. One such feature is the ability to store frequently used values or calculations as memorized answers. However, there may come a time when you need to clear these memories to free up space or start anew. This tutorial will guide you through the process of clearing memorized answers on your TI-30XA calculator with clarity, detail, and practicality. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** 1. Familiarize yourself with the layout and buttons of your TI-30XA calculator before proceeding. 2. Follow the steps below in sequence to ensure successful completion of the task. 3. Pay attention to button sequences and capitalization since they are crucial in this process. 4. Remember that clearing all memories also resets settings like conversion factors and display mode. 5. Perform this action carefully to avoid accidentally deleting important data or settings. **Step 1: Access Memory Menu** To begin, press the [2nd] (labeled as 'alpha' on some models) and then the [MEM] keys simultaneously. The screen should now read "Mem" indicating that you have entered the memory menu. *Why this works:* Pressing the [2nd] key along with another function key allows access to secondary functions associated with those keys. Here, we're using it to open the memory menu. **Step 2: Select Clear All Memories Option** In the memory menu, scroll down until you see "ClrAllMem". To do so, repeatedly press the arrow keys (located just above the number pad). Once highlighted, press ENTER. *Why this works:* Clearing individual memories one by one could be tedious, especially if you have many stored values. By choosing to clear all memories at once, you save time and effort. **Step 3: Confirm Action** After pressing ENTER in Step 2, the calculator will prompt you to confirm whether you want to delete everything in the memory. If you wish to proceed, press 1 for YES; otherwise, press 2 for NO. *Why this confirmation exists:* Deleting all memories irreversibly erases any information previously saved in them. Therefore, the calculator requires explicit consent before executing such an operation. Upon successfully completing these three steps, you would've cleared all memorized answers from your Texas Instruments TI-30XA calculator. Always double-check after performing operations like these to make sure nothing vital was unintentionally removed. Orbits: The long and short of comets Back to the Beginning... Since comets were created from the same spinning cloud of gas and dust as the planets, they continue that motion, revolving around the Sun like the planets and everything else in the solar system. Like the planets, each comet travels on a regular path, called an orbit. The planets' orbits are very nearly circular, but not quite. Each orbit has the shape of a slightly stretched-out circle, called an ellipse. Earth's orbit is so close to a circle that if you could look at it from space, you couldn't really tell the difference. But many comets revolve along more stretched-out ellipses with the Sun near one end instead of in the center. It's as if the Sun were twirling each comet on a stretchy rubber band, that gets longer and then shorter again, each time the comet comes back around the Sun. Coming and Going Having an elliptical orbit means there is a point for each comet where it is closest to the Sun. At this point we say that it is at perihelion; "peri" means close, "helio" is the root word for Sun. There is also a point where the comet is farthest away from the Sun. At this point, we say that it is at aphelion. ? In perihelion, " peri" means close. What do you think the "ap" in aphelion means? Here's one more new term: the time it takes a planet or a comet to travel once around the Sun in its orbit is called the orbital period. ? Suppose the "planet" in our definition of period is the earth. Do you know another name for the earth's orbital period? (Hint: think of the calendar.) Stretching the Point In the Oort Cloud, a comet's orbit can be changed over many years by gravity, until it is long and thin, with the Sun very close to one end. These comets travel all the way from the Oort Cloud to a point inside the Kuiper Belt and back out again. If the orbit's perihelion is close enough to the Sun, say, less than 5 AU, it then receives enough solar energy to become bright and be seen by the naked eye. This picture shows a long-period comet orbit. Imagine the other Oort Cloud comets near its aphelion. The picture is not drawn to scale. The actual distance to the nearest star is about twice the size of the whole Oort Cloud. Since these comets still travel from the Oort Cloud all the way around the Sun and back, they can take from hundreds of years, to over a hundred thousand (100,000) years to revolve once around the Sun. What would you call these comets? Right! They are known as long period comets. Comet Hale-Bopp, which appeared in 1997, is a long period comet. It wont return to its perihelion near the Sun again for almost 2500 years: a long time to us, but a short time for a comet. About five out of every six comets that have been observed are long period comets. The comets that led Oort to develop his theory of the comet cloud were all long-period comets. Short and Sweet There are also comets whose entire orbit lies within the region of the Kuiper Belt, the "waistband" of comets that is just beyond the planet Neptune. These comets have periods of about 200 years or less. Because of this, they are known as short period comets. Comet Halley, which last appeared in 1986, is a short period comet. It takes 76 years for Comet Halley to complete one trip around the Sun. But as you saw, in the Origins section, many objects in the Kuiper Belt have nearly round orbits. Only a few have long flat orbits that come close to the Sun at one end. This picture shows that Halley's orbit doesn't even go beyond that of Pluto. You can just see the comet and its tail inside the orbit of Venus near perihelion. The planets are NOT drawn to scale. They are shown bigger so the viewer can recognize them. How fast are comets? The important difference between pens and pencils is that the tip of a pencil is made of solid graphite (or other material) which is rubbed off onto the paper. A pen has a tip, usually made of metal, with liquid ink coming out and onto the paper. Writing with a pen can smudge when it is still wet. Writing from a pencil can be erased, but writing from a pen usually cannot, unless it uses a special type of ink and eraser. Well move and let Urs'la sit down. Well thank you for writing that joke. How to use a compass (Lesson 1) Using the compass alone This is a very easy lesson, and I would say, not sufficient for those who would like to travel safely in unfamiliar terrain. The first thing you need to learn, are the directions. North, South, East and West. Look at the figure and learn how they are. North is the most important. There are several kinds of compasses, one kind to attach to the map, one kind to attach to your thumb. The thumb-compass is used mostly by orienteers who just want to run fast, and this is the kind of compass I normally use. But not in this tutorial. I would recommend the third kind of compass. Let's take a look at it: You see this red and black arrow? We call it the . Well, on some compasses it might be red and white for instance, but the point is, the red part of it is always pointing towards the earth's magnetic north pole. Got that? That's basically what you need to know. It's as simple as that. But if you don't want to go north, but a different direction? Hang on and I'll tell you. You've got this turnable thing on your compass. We call it the Compass housing. On the edge of the compass housing, you will probably have a scale. From 0 to 360 or from 0 to 400. Those are the degrees or the azimuth (or you may also call it the bearing in some contexts). And you should have the letters N, S, W and E for North, South, West and East. If you want to go in a direction between two of these, you would combine them. If you would like to go in a direction just between North and West, you simply say: "I would like to go Northwest ". Let's use that as an example: You want to go northwest. What you do, is that you find out where on the compass housing northwest is. Then you turn the compass housing so that northwest on the housing comes exactly there where the large direction of travel-arrow meets the housing. Hold the compass in your hand. And you'll have to hold it quite flat, so that the compass needle can turn. Then turn yourself, your hand, the entire compass, just make sure the compass housing doesn't turn, and turn it until the compass needle is aligned with the lines inside the compass housing. Now, time to be careful!. It is extremely important that the red, north part of the compass needle points at north in the compass housing. If south points at north, you would walk off in the exact opposite direction of what you want! And it's a very common mistake among beginners. So always take a second look to make sure you did it right! A second problem might be local magnetic attractions. If you are carrying something of iron or something like that, it might disturb the arrow. Even a staple in your map might be a problem. Make sure there is nothing of the sort around. There is a possibility for magnetic attractions in the soil as well, "magnetic deviation", but they are rarely seen. Might occur if you're in a mining district. When you are sure you've got it right, walk off in the direction the direction of travel-arrow is pointing. To avoid getting off the course, make sure to look at the compass quite frequently, say every hundred meters at least. But you shouldn't stare down on the compass. Once you have the direction, aim on some point in the distance, and go there. But this gets more important when you use a map. There is something you should look for to avoid going in the opposite direction: The Sun. At noon, the sun is roughly in South (or in the north on the southern hemisphere), so if you are heading north and have the sun in your face, it should ring a bell. When do you need this technique? If you are out there without a map, and you don't know where you are, but you know that there is a road, trail, stream, river or something long and big you can't miss if you go in the right direction. And you know in what direction you must go to get there, at least approximately what direction. Then all you need to do, is to turn the compass housing, so that the direction you want to go in, is where the direction of travel-arrow meets the housing. And follow the above steps. But why isn't this sufficient? It is not very accurate. You are going in the right direction, and you won't go around in circles, but you're very lucky if you hit a small spot this way. And that's why I'm not talking about declination here. And because that is something connected with the use of maps. But if you have a mental image of the map and know what it is, do think about it. But I think you won't be able to be so accurate so the declination won't make a difference. If you are taking a long hike in unfamiliar terrain, you should always carry a good map that covers the terrain. Especially if you are leaving the trail. It is in this interaction between the map and a compass, that the compass becomes really valuable. " Who's behind the teddy bear? The triangle inequality theorem states that the sum of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the length of the third side. To find a range of values for the third side when given two lengths, write two inequalities: one inequality that assumes the larger value given is the longest side in the triangle and one inequality that assumes that the third side is the longest side in the triangle. Combine the two inequalities for the final answer. Let's say I gave you 3 pieces of spaghetti and you try to make a triangle out of them, so I'm going to say that this piece of spaghetti is 10 inches long, this piece right here is 3 and this piece right here is 6. Does it look like we're going to be able to make a triangle out of this? It looks like we don't quite have enough to close that arch, let's say however instead of 3 and 6 let's say I gave you pieces that were 3 and 7, then we would just have a straight line. Because there's no way for us to form an angle there. What we're talking about here are triangle inequalities which means if you look at a triangle there's a special relationship between those 3 sides and it kind of make sense if you think about it. If you started at point a what's going to be the fastest way to go to point c? Is it going to be to walk to point b and then point c? or is it just going to be faster to go from point a to point c? Well pretty clearly you're just going to walk straight to point c. But how does this look in terms of relationships? Well we can say that line segment ab or side ab plus side bc must be greater than your third side ac, so what's that saying is that going straight from one point to another is going to be shorter or less than the sum of the other 2 sides. You can write 2 other inequalities, we could start with ab and go with our side ac and say ac has to be greater than your third side which is bc. At last you could write your last inequality which we'd say side ac plus side bc has to be greater than your side ab. So in essence you always have to make sure that two of your sides summed will be more than the third side which is why we couldn't make a triangle because 3 plus 6 is 9 which is less than 10. 3+7=10 but notice you do not have an equal to part to your inequality all you have is this greater than symbol. In the beginning, operating systems on personal computers (or PC) did not have the concept of access control. There were no "users", everyone could do everything. Modern operating systems have the concept of access control. There can be more than one user and some users may have more "privileges" than others. Certain users may only have access to certain files and are unable to access others. Other users may be able to modify or delete certain files. These privileges can be specified for each file. Counting money quickly and accurately is an important skill that can be useful in many situations, such as managing personal finances, handling cash registers at work, or even running a small business. With practice and patience, you can improve your money counting speed while maintaining accuracy. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to count money fast: **Step 1: Familiarize yourself with different denominations** To begin, make sure you are comfortable recognizing all paper currency denominations ($, $5, $10, $20, $50, and $100) used in your country. This knowledge will help prevent mistakes when counting large amounts of mixed bills. You should also understand the difference between genuine and counterfeit currency to avoid potential issues. **Step 2: Organize the bills by denomination** Before starting to count, separate the bills into their respective piles based on their denominations. Sorting bills first makes counting faster because you won't have to stop frequently to check which bill you are dealing with. It also reduces errors since you focus only on one type of bill at a time. Key tip: Place the largest denomination pile(s) nearest to you to minimize distraction from other piles. **Step 3: Estimate the total amount before counting** Quickly glance over the sorted piles and estimate the approximate value of each stack. Add up these estimates mentally or using rough calculations to get a general idea of the total amount. This initial assessment gives you a target number to aim for during actual counting, helping maintain focus and motivation throughout the process. **Step 4: Start counting the largest denomination** Begin counting with the highest-value bills, usually $100 notes if available. Pick up one bill and fan its edges gently so that you see all four corners simultaneously. As you do this, say aloud the denomination name or value, like "$100." Repeat this action until you reach the last bill in the pile, ensuring proper alignment to facilitate easy counting. Guideline: Aim to establish a rhythmic pattern while saying the denomination out loud - doing so often results in increased speed without compromising accuracy. **Step 5: Straighten and restack counted bills** As you finish counting each pile, straighten the bills neatly and place them side-by-side face upward in groups of five or ten depending on convenience. Restacking after every completed pile allows you to keep track of progress and prevents confusion caused by scattered uncounted bills. **Step 6: Move onto smaller denominations** After completing the highest-denomination pile(s), proceed to count lower-value stacks following steps 4 and 5. Maintain consistency in your counting method, continuing to state the denomination aloud as you move through each pile. **Step 7: Verify your counts periodically** It's essential to double-check your work occasionally to catch any possible errors early. Choose random samples from various stages of your counting process and recount them independently. If discrepancies arise, reevaluate both sets of numbers to identify where the mistake occurred and correct it accordingly. **Step 8: Sum up individual totals** Once you've finished counting all the denominations, add together the subtotals obtained from each pile. Perform this final calculation carefully to ensure accuracy, preferably multiple times or using different methods to cross-verify results. By consistently practicing these techniques, you'll become more proficient at counting money rapidly while minimizing errors. Remember to stay focused, organized, and diligent during the entire process to maximize efficiency and effectiveness. Title: How to Hack Any Wi-Fi Network on an Android Device Disclaimer: This article is intended for educational purposes only. It is illegal to attempt to hack into a wireless network without permission from its owner. The author and publisher are not responsible for any misuse or damage caused by this information. Introduction: In today's digital age, securing our wireless networks has become increasingly important as more devices connect to them daily. However, understanding how to test your own network security can be beneficial in identifying potential vulnerabilities before they are exploited. This guide will walk you through the process of attempting to crack a Wi-Fi password using an Android device. Please note that this method may not work with all routers and encryption types. Materials Needed: 1. An Android device running a custom recovery image (such as TWRP) and root access. 2. A USB OTG cable. 3. A compatible Wi-Fi adapter supporting monitor mode (e.g., TL-WN722N). 4. The latest version of Aircrack-ng suite downloaded onto your computer. 5. Basic knowledge of command line interfaces. Step 1: Preparation Before beginning, ensure that your Android device meets the requirements mentioned above. Rooting your phone and installing a custom recovery image can void your warranty and potentially brick your device if done incorrectly. Proceed at your own risk. Step 2: Installing BusyBox BusyBox provides Unix tools commonly used in embedded systems. To install it, search for "BusyBox" in the Google Play Store and download the app developed by Stephen (Stericson). Open the app and grant root permissions when prompted. Select "Install" followed by "Yes" to confirm the installation directory. Once installed, press "Open" to launch the application. Step 3: Configuring Wireless Interface Connect your compatible Wi-Fi adapter to your Android device via the USB OTG cable. Launch the previously installed BusyBox app and type "ifconfig" without quotes in the terminal window. Take note of the wireless interface name (usually starts with "wlan"). We will refer to this as <interface> throughout the remaining steps. Step 4: Enabling Monitor Mode To capture packets in the air, we need to put our wireless interface into monitor mode. Type the following commands one by one in the terminal window: ```bash su airmon-ng stop <interface> airmon-ng start <interface> ``` Replace `<interface>` with the actual name obtained earlier (e.g., wlan0). After executing these commands, you should see a new interface named "<interface>mon". Step 5: Scanning for Access Points Now let's scan for nearby Wi-Fi networks by typing: ``` airodump-ng --bssid <target_AP> -c <channel> --write cap <filename> <interface>mon I don't know if it'll fit on with the socks. Under the relentless measures of Ferdinand and Isabella, with whose story all American children, at least, should be familiar, the last Moorish stronghold had fallen, in the very year in which Columbus discovered America, and Spain, from the Pyrenees to the Straits of Gibraltar, acknowledged the mastership of its Christian sovereigns. But the centuries of warfare that had made the Spaniards a fierce and warlike race, had also filled Spain with frowning castles and embattled towns. And such an embattled town was this same city of Avila, in which, in 1525, lived the stern and pious old grandee, Don Alphonso Sanchez de Cepeda, his sentimental and romance-loving wife, the Donna Beatrix, and their twelve sturdy and healthy children. Religious warfare, as it is the most bitter and relentless of strifes, is also the most brutal. It turns the natures of men and women into quite a different channel from the one in which the truths they are fighting for would seek to lead them; and of all relentless and brutal religious wars, few have been more bitter than the one that for fully five hundred years had wasted the land of Spain. So he just watched, and the skin slowly sank to the bottom. But then something miraculous happened! After a few weeks, the filthy skin disappeared! The old man couldn't believe it, and he wondered what had happened. He thought and he thought, until he realized the answer: The skin had rotted away! John Warnock Hinckley, Jr. (born May 29, 1955) is the man who attempted to assassinate President Ronald Reagan. On March 30, 1981, shortly after Reagan was elected, Hinckley shot at Reagan in Washington, D.C. Reagan was injured, but recovered quickly. Hinckley was tried, but was found not guilty, due to his insanity. He said the reason he shot the President was to get the attention of actress Jodie Foster, of whom he was a fan. The four different types of clubs used in golf are woods, irons, putters and wedges. Woods are used to hit the ball very far, usually off the tee (but also on the fairway). The name of woods is different every number, for example, No.1 wood is called "driver", No.2 is "brassey", No.3 is "spoon" and so on. There are many types of irons, which can be used to hit varying distances. They are numbered 1-9 with a 1 iron hitting the ball farther than a 9. The 1 iron is no longer common in the game. Even the 2 iron is getting uncommon. Putters are used when on the green. The rules do not let the golfer use over 14 clubs in a game. Some oral rehydration solutions come in packets and just need to be mixed with clean water. Oral rehydration solution can also be made at home. If the water in the area is not safe, it can be boiled to make it safe. (Boiling the water will kill any bacteria, viruses, or parasites in the water.) Salt and sugar are then mixed into the water. Drinking this mixture, after the water cools, will re-hydrate the child, if he drinks enough. Adding a banana or orange juice can add potassium to the mixture. And only mommas and daddies drink coffee? Title: Understanding Customer Arrivals with a Special Twist Imagine you are running a lemonade stand near a park where kids play. You want to know how many kids will come by during recess to buy your delicious lemonade. But here's the twist - some kids might prefer ice cream instead! Let's learn about how we can predict these fun events using something called a "Poisson Process." a. Predicting Kid Visits Between Specific Times You observe that kids come by every few minutes, and on average, you have 30 visits per hour. Now, let's say between 9:30 AM and 10:30 AM, you counted exactly 32 kid visits (some may have wanted both lemonade and ice cream). How likely is it that no kids came by during the last 5 minutes (between 10:25 AM and 10:30 AM)? To figure this out, imagine the number of kid arrivals follows a pattern where visits tend to spread out evenly over time. We call this a Poisson Process. To calculate the chance that zero kids showed up during those last 5 minutes, follow these steps: 1. Find the difference in the total number of observed visits within half-hour intervals before and after 10:25 AM (in our example, it's 32 - 30 = 2). 2. Calculate the probabilities separately: one when 2 kids show up during the entire 30-minute interval without any visits in the final 5 minutes; another when 30 kids visit throughout the full hour but still no visits occur in the last 5 minutes. 3. Divide the joint probability (step 2) by the overall likelihood of observing 32 visits within an hour. 4. Finally, simplify the expression by substituting the Poisson formula ($P(N(t)=n) = (\lambda t)^ne^{-\lambda t}/n!$) into the equation wherever needed. b. Guessing Which Treat They Want Now, suppose you wish to find the chance that the very first two kids asking for treats after 9:00 AM both ask for ice cream. Since kids choose either lemonade or ice cream randomly, we can think of their choices like two separate coin flips happening back-to-back - except instead of heads or tails, we get "lemonade" or "ice cream." In other words, treat this scenario as two independent processes: one for lemonade requests (with rate $1/3 \times 30$, because 1 out of 3 kids prefers it), and another for ice cream requests (rate $2/3 \times 30$). Then, multiply the individual probabilities together to compute the combined odds for both ice cream picks. And just like that, armed with the power of understanding patterns and randomness, you're well equipped to make smart predictions about all sorts of real-life situations! Whether it's guessing which flavor friends would pick at an ice cream party or anticipating busy times at your future business-these skills are bound to serve you well. Happy learning! Reece always went in last for the School, and the School in consequence always felt that there were possibilities to the very end of the innings. The lot of a last-wicket man is somewhat trying. As at any moment his best innings may be nipped in the bud by the other man getting out, he generally feels that it is hardly worth while to play himself in before endeavouring to make runs. Every day, Cindy would go to the store to get milk for her family. She loved the store and was always greeted by the friendly shopkeeper. One day when Cindy was at the store, she noticed something strange: the shelves were all empty! She asked the shopkeeper why the shelves were empty. "It's too hard for you to understand," the shopkeeper said. Cindy wasn't happy about this. But she had to trust the shopkeeper. So, she said goodbye and went home. When Cindy told her family the news they didn't recognize the problem. But then they noticed something even more strange: the milk was gone too! The family soon realized what had happened: the shopkeeper had been preparing for a snowstorm and had run out of milk. They were relieved that the shopkeeper had already taken care of the problem. Sing and Spell (Tune: "Row, Row, Row Your Boat") Sing and spell with me; It's easy as can be. Just sing a song And learn along - It's so much fun, you'll see. Z-e-r-o spells zero, O-n-e spells one, T-w-o spells two, Now we've just begun. Now let's spell some more. S-i-x spells six, You're spelling like a pro. Three more numbers to go. T-e-n spells ten, Let's spell them one more time. Zero - z - e - r - o One - o-n-e Etc. Write words on sentence strips and hold up as you sing. Make puzzles by writing number words on sentence strips. Cut between letters and place in an envelope. Write the number word on the envelope as well. Children remove pieces and put the puzzle together to spell words. Line Up - Tap children as you say their position when they line up. For example, "first," "second," "third," "fourth," etc. Toy Talk - Line up classroom toys and then orally say their position. Next, ask children to bring you toys by calling the ordinal position. For example, "Who can bring me the fourth toy?" Piggy Sticks - Make piggy sticks by gluing pigs similar to the one shown on craft sticks. Retell "This Little Pig" using ordinals. The first little pig went to the market. The second little pig stayed home. The third little pig had roast beef. The fourth little pig had none. The fifth little pig cried, "Wee wee wee" all the way home. The sixth little pig ate some pizza. The seventh little pig ate a pear. The eighth little pig had spaghetti. The ninth little pig's plate was bare. The tenth little pig cried, "Wee wee wee, I will share!" Guess and Check - Give each child a zip bag to take home and fill with objects. (Give them parameters for this, such as 1-10 or 1-25.) Number each of their bags. Classmates write down the number of the bag and then write down their estimation ("guess") of how many objects are in the bag. Next, they open the bags, count, and write down the correct amount ("check"). Estimation Center - Fill a plastic bottle or jar with different objects each day. Place small pieces of paper and a pencil by the "estimation jar." Sometime during the day ask students to write their name and estimation on a piece of paper. At the end of the day, empty the container and count. Who guessed the closest amount? Who guessed more? Who guessed less? And now I'll eat some up. I've got half a pizza I've got of a pizza I've got 1/8 of a pizza I've got no pizza in my hands It's just an empty pan. Cut a red sheet of paper into a circle and then cut into fractions. Hold up the pieces as you sing the song. *Click here to download a book that goes with the song. Eating Fractions - Order a large pizza for your class and sing the song as you cut it into enough pieces to share. (You might need two pizzas if you have a large class!) *Give children a large graham cracker. Can they break it in half? Can they eat half? Can they break it in fourths? Can they eat one fourth? Can they break it in eighths? Can they eat one eighth? Fractions Puzzles - Have children bring in food boxes from home. Cut the fronts off the boxes and use them to make fraction puzzles. Problem Solving - You can use blocks, crayons, or other classroom objects for problem solving. For example, invite four children to come to the front of the room. Take twelve blocks and ask the other students how you could share the blocks with those four friends. Bring in 12 packages of crackers. Count together. "What can we do? There are 24 people in our classroom and only 12 packs of crackers." Found a Penny (Tune: "Found a Peanut") (Hold up real coins or cut outs as you sing.) One dime is worth ten cents. Found a quarter, found a quarter, found a quarter just now. It's the largest of all the coins, Found a quarter just now. Rubbings - Have children do rubbings of coins. Place each coin under a sheet of paper and rub with the side of a crayon. Who do they see? What's it worth? Let children examine coins with a magnifying glass. Encourage them to discuss details. How old is the coin? Money Song (Tune: "Shortnin' Bread") Chorus: I like money to buy things at the store. (Point to self.) Money, money, money, I always want more! (Palms up and shake.) A penny's worth one cent. (Hold up 1 finger.) A nickel's worth five. (Hold up 5 fingers.) A dime's worth ten cents. (Hold up 10 fingers.) A quarter's twenty-five. (Open and shut hands for 25.) Lincoln's on one cent. Jefferson's on five. Roosevelt's on ten cents. Washington's on twenty-five. A building's on one cent. A building's on five. A torch is on ten cents. An eagle's on twenty-five. Hint! Feel free to change the words of the song to "Let's learn some more!" instead of "I always want more." Penny Time Line - Let children make a penny time line. First, have them write the year they were born and subsequent years on a sentence strip similar to the one shown. Let them take this home and look for a penny with each date. Tape the pennies to the time line. Change Please - Draw four square on a file folder. Label with "penny," "nickel," "dime," and "quarter." Give children a coin purse with change and ask them to sort the coins. Can they count the total amount? Coupon Clippers - Bring in coupons and have children cut them out. Ask children to sort the coupons. Can they sort them another way? Wants and Needs - Have children use a T-chart to write or cut out pictures of things they "want" and things they "need." Money Tree - Does money really grow on a tree? Where does money come from? Have children go home and interview their parents to find out how they make money for their family. Pay Day - Brainstorm how you pay for things when you go to a store? Do your parents use dollar bills, checks, or credit cards? Run off pretend checks for the children to fill in. Let children make play credit cards by cutting 2" x 3 " rectangles out of Styrofoam plates. Addition Pokey (Tune: "Hokey Pokey") Put 1 finger in. (Hold up finger on right hand.) Put 1 finger more. (Hold up 1 finger on left hand.) Shake them altogether (Roll around.) And then lay them on the floor. (Place on floor or table.) Add them both together, (Bring hands together.) And you don't want to stall. Now you have 2 in all. 2 fingers 3 fingers 4 fingers 5 fingers *Do "Addition Pokey" with other facts. Signs for Math - Introduce sign language for equal (fingers straight and bring tips together), addition (open fingers and then bring tips together), and subtraction (pretend to take something out of palm and throw it down). Put fingertips together for "more," and pretend to push down with one palm on the other for "less." Incredible Equations - Each day let children brainstorm equations that would equal the date. Start doing this as a large group activity, and then have children do it independently or with a partner. Domino Addition - Let children add up dots on dominoes. "Egg"cellent Math - Write math problems on plastic eggs with a permanent marker. rite the answer on a small sheet of paper and place it inside so children can self check. Math Flash - You can make this simple game from a spiral ring of index cards. Write math facts on the front of the index cards with a marker. Write the answer on the reverse side with a pencil.
