I was rescued just when I thought I had come to the end of my limit, taken to Santa F  , more dead than alive, and there Neal found me. Have you ever wondered what makes a paper plane fly? Some paper planes clearly fly better than others. But why is this? One factor is the kind of design used to build the plane. In this activity you'll get to build a paper plane and change its basic design to see how this affects its flight. There's a lot of cool science in this activity, such as how forces act on a plane so it can fly. So get ready to start folding! The forces that allow a paper plane to fly are the same ones that apply to real airplanes. A force is something that pushes or pulls on something else. When you throw a paper plane in the air, you are giving the plane a push to move forward. That push is a type of force called thrust. While the plane is flying forward, air moving over and under the wings is providing an upward lift force on the plane. At the same time, air pushing back against the plane is slowing it down, creating a drag force. The weight of the paper plane also affects its flight, as gravity pulls it down toward Earth. All of these forces (thrust, lift, drag and gravity) affect how well a given paper plane's voyage goes. In this activity you will increase how much drag a paper plane experiences and see if this changes how far the plane flies. Sheet of paper Large open area in which to fly a paper plane, such as a long hallway, school gym or basketball court. If you're flying your paper plane outside, such as in a field, try to do it when there isn't any wind. Something to make at least a one-foot-long line, such as a long string, another ruler, masking tape, rocks or sticks. Paper clips (optional) Make a standard, "dart" design paper airplane (for instructions, go to the Amazing Paper Airplanes Web page ). Fold your paper into the basic dart paper plane. Fold carefully and make your folds as sharp as possible, such as by running a thumbnail or a ruler along each fold to crease it. Do not bend up the tailing edge of the wings (step 6 of the online folding instructions). Go to a large open area and, using string, a ruler, masking tape, rocks or sticks, make a line in front of you that's at least one foot long, going from left to right. This will be the starting line from which you'll fly the paper plane. Place your toe on the line you prepared and throw the paper plane. Did it fly very far? Throw the plane at least four more times. Each time before you throw the plane, make sure it is still in good condition (that the folds and points are still sharp). When you toss it, place your toe on the line and try to launch the plane with a similar amount of force, including gripping it at the same spot. Did it go about the same distance each time? Once you have a good idea of about how far your plane typically flies, change the plane's shape to increase how much drag it experiences. To do this, cut slits that are about one inch long right where either wing meets the middle ridge. Fold up the cut section on both wings so that each now has a one-inch-wide section at the end of the wing that is folded up, at about a 90-degree angle from the rest of the wing. Throw your modified paper plane at least five more times, just as you did before. How far does the paper plane fly now compared with before? Why do you think this is, and what does it have to do with drag? Extra: Make paper planes that are different sizes and compare how well they fly. Do bigger planes fly farther? Extra: Try making paper planes out of different types of paper, such as printer paper, construction paper and newspaper. Use the same design for each. Does one type of paper seem to work best for making paper planes? Does one type work the worst? Extra: Some people like to add paper clips to their paper planes to make them fly better. Try adding a paper clip (or multiple paper clips) to different parts of your paper plane (such as the front, back, middle or wings) and then flying it. How does this affect the plane's flight? Does adding paper clips somewhere make its flight better or much worse? And then we get to see what we height play with here. Once upon a time, there was a chubby bunny. Title: How to Check the Status of Your Tax Return Introduction: Tax season can be a stressful time for many people, but one way to alleviate some of that stress is by regularly checking the status of your tax return. This will give you an idea of when to expect your refund or any correspondence from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Here's a step-by-step guide on how to do just that. Step 1: Gather Necessary Information Before you start, make sure you have all necessary information handy. You'll need your Social Security number or Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN), filing status, and exact amount of your refund as shown on your tax return. Key Tip: Keep your tax documents organized throughout the year so they are easy to find during tax season. Step 2: Visit IRS Website Navigate to the official website of the IRS at www.irs.gov. Avoid clicking links in emails claiming to be from the IRS; these could lead to phishing scams designed to steal personal information. Step 3: Choose "Where's My Refund?" Tool On the homepage, look for the tab labeled "Tools" near the top menu bar. Hover over this tab and select "Refund Status" from the dropdown list. Alternatively, scroll down until you see the section titled "Quick Links" and click on "Where's My Refund?" under that heading. Guideline: The "Where's My Refund?" tool is available in English and Spanish and can be accessed 24 hours a day, seven days a week. However, there may be delays after certain peak times due to system updates. Step 4: Enter Required Details You will now arrive at the main page for the "Where's My Refund?" tool. Enter your SSN or ITIN, filing status, and the whole dollar amount of your expected refund without commas or decimal points. Then hit "Submit." Step 5: Interpret Results The next screen should display the current status of your tax return. Possible statuses include: * Received: The IRS has received your tax return and is processing it. * Approved: The IRS has processed your return and approved your refund. It should provide an estimated date when you can expect to receive your refund. * Sent: The IRS has sent your refund to your financial institution. Key Tips: If your refund was sent via direct deposit, it usually takes about five days for it to show up in your account. Paper checks might take longer, depending on mail delivery times. Also, remember that a refund date is an estimate - sometimes actual refund dates vary slightly from what was initially projected. Step 6: Bookmark Page & Set Alerts (Optional) If you want to keep tabs on your refund more closely, bookmark the "Where's My Refund?" results page in your web browser for quick access later. Additionally, consider signing up for email alerts if offered. These alerts notify you whenever the status changes. Conclusion: Regularly checking the status of your tax return can help reduce anxiety around tax season. By following these steps carefully, you'll know exactly where your return stands every step of the way. He likes me to read to him a lot. And when they got there their mother ordered a salad. Title: Understanding the Difference Between Christened and Baptized Introduction: The terms "christening" and "baptism" are often used interchangeably; however, they have different meanings within various Christian denominations. While both rituals involve water and signify entrance into a religious community, there are nuances that set them apart. This comprehensive guide will help you understand the distinction between christening and baptizing and provide insight into their significance within Christianity. Step 1: Defining Christening Christening is an act that primarily takes place among Anglican and Episcopalian communities. It refers to the naming ceremony for infants or young children where they receive their first name(s) and become members of the church. Historically, godparents played a crucial role as sponsors who would support the spiritual growth of the child if necessary. Key Tips: * Christening does not necessarily imply full membership in the Church until confirmation later in life. * The term can also be applied more generally to any formal naming ceremony across various cultures and religions. Step 2: Exploring Baptism Baptism, on the other hand, is widely recognized throughout most branches of Christianity. It symbolizes purification from sin, death to self, and rebirth in Jesus Christ through immersion (full body submersion), pouring, or sprinkling with water. This sacrament confers full membership in the particular Christian denomination performing the baptism. Guidelines: * Most Protestant sects practice believer's baptism, which occurs after a person has made a conscious decision to accept Jesus Christ as Savior. * Some traditions perform infant baptism, emphasizing God's grace given at birth rather than personal choice. * Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Oriental Orthodox, and some Lutheran churches uphold paedobaptism, meaning they baptize infants. Step 3: Distinguishing Key Elements While superficially similar, christening and baptism differ significantly in several ways: Age: Traditionally, christenings occur during infancy while baptisms may happen anytime from infancy to adulthood depending on the tradition. Membership: A christening marks entry into the local congregation but doesn't automatically confer complete church membership until confirmation. Conversely, baptism signifies immediate incorporation into the entire faith community. Spiritual Significance: Both acts denote spiritual transformation, yet their interpretations vary. For instance, baptism might represent public profession of faith, cleansing from original sin, or initiation into discipleship. Meanwhile, christening could indicate recognition of divine election, consecration for service, or simply welcome into the earthly fellowship. Step 4: Appreciating Historical Context Understanding historical developments provides further clarification. Originally, early Christians practiced adult baptism following conversion. Over time, infant baptism became prevalent, particularly amongst Catholics and certain Protestant groups. However, Anabaptists and others maintained the importance of believer's baptism. As these divergent practices evolved, distinct terminologies emerged-hence 'christening' versus 'baptizing'. Conclusion: Although frequently confused, christening and baptizing carry unique implications within diverse Christian contexts. By grasping their distinctions, one deepens appreciation for the rich tapestry of beliefs and practices characterizing global Christianity. Whether celebrating a cherished family event or exploring deeper theological questions, this knowledge serves as a valuable starting point. And then you can put it back together. Although you can't get inside your students' heads and shake up their brains, there are some activities that you can do to make their brains more alert and to help improve learning. Vigorous physical activity gets the blood flowing and releases stress. According to Eric Jensen (BRAIN-BASED LEARNING), children need to "stand up and stretch" every 20 minutes. I think most teachers could use "brain breaks" as well! It's also important to build cross-lateral exercises into your day. Cross-lateral movements are those in which arms and legs cross over from one side of the body to the other. The left side of the brain controls the right side of the body, and the right side of the brain controls the left side. Both sides are forced to communicate when arms and legs cross over. This "unsticks" the brain and energizes learning. You can use these activities to start your day, between activities, or whenever your students appear bored or restless. And it certainly wouldn't hurt to try them before, during, or after testing! They are simple, inexpensive, and might even create some smiles! Try out one at a time and see how your class responds. Write those they like on a poster or put them on an index card and store them in a "brain break" can. After several weeks, children can choose their favorite exercises and lead the class. Hint! Most of these are a lot more fun if you do them to some catchy music with a strong beat. Cross the Midline Stand with arms at sides. Touch right hand to left knee. Stand with arms at sides and touch left hand to right knee. Count or sing as you do this. Hint! Put a piece of painter's tape down the middle of children's bodies so they can be aware of crossing the midline. Stand with arms at sides. Bend and touch right elbow to left knee as you raise your leg. Stand and then touch left elbow to right knee. Feet spread apart and arms extended. Bend over at waist and tap right hand to left foot. Back up and then bend and tap left hand to right foot. Bend left knee and put foot behind right leg. Reach back around with right hand and touch left foot. Reverse and put right foot behind your left leg as you touch it with your left hand. The old man cuffed him twice. "Is it your mother you are speaking of?" Title: Get Smooth Kissable Lips Introduction: Having smooth, soft, and kissable lips is something many people strive for. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to achieve just that. From exfoliating to hydrating, we'll cover all the necessary steps to help you get the perfect pout. Step 1: Exfoliate Your Lips Exfoliation removes dead skin cells from your lip surface, revealing softer and smoother skin underneath. You can make a simple homemade lip scrub using sugar and honey or olive oil. Mix equal parts sugar and honey (or olive oil) until you create a paste-like consistency. Gently rub this mixture onto your lips in circular motions for about 30 seconds before rinsing off with warm water. Remember not to overdo it as excessive exfoliation could lead to irritation. Key Tip: Exfoliate once or twice a week at most. Step 2: Cleanse Your Lips After exfoliating, cleanse your lips gently to remove any residue left behind by the scrub. Use a damp washcloth or a mild facial cleanser. Avoid using harsh soaps as they may dry out your lips. Pat them dry afterwards; don't rub because this might cause further dryness or chapping. Step 3: Hydrate Your Lips Hydrated lips are plump, soft, and less prone to cracking. Apply a lip balm containing ingredients like shea butter, beeswax, vitamin E, or petroleum jelly after cleansing. These components lock in moisture and protect your lips throughout the day. Reapply whenever needed, especially during cold weather or when spending time in air-conditioned environments. Step 4: Remove Lipstick Stains If you wear matte lipsticks frequently, you might notice some stubborn staining on your lips over time. To eliminate these, mix baking soda with lemon juice to form a paste, then lightly rub it over your lips before wiping away with a wet cloth. The acidic nature of lemon juice lifts pigment while baking soda acts as a gentle abrasive. However, do not use this method more than once a month as both lemon juice and baking soda can be drying if used excessively. Step 5: Protect Your Lips Sun damage isn't only harmful to your skin but also to your lips. Always apply a lip product with SPF before going outside to shield your lips from UVA and UVB rays. Prolonged sun exposure can lead to premature aging and increased risk of skin cancer. Step 6: Maintain Good Habits Avoid licking your lips repeatedly as saliva evaporates quickly, leaving your lips drier than before. Also, try to quit smoking if you haven't already. Not only does smoking stain your teeth and harm your lungs, but it also dehydrates your lips causing wrinkles around the mouth area. Conclusion: With regular practice of these steps, you should see an improvement in the texture and appearance of your lips. Just remember, everyone's body reacts differently to different products and routines, so what works best for one person might not work as well for another. Listen to your lips and adjust accordingly. Title: How to Block Explicit Music on an iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of blocking explicit music on your iPhone. By following these steps, you can ensure that your listening experience remains family-friendly and appropriate for all ages. This is especially useful if children have access to your device or if you simply prefer to avoid explicit content. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Make sure you're using the latest version of iOS for optimal functionality. * Familiarize yourself with the Settings app and its features before starting this process. * Be aware that some songs may still slip through the filters due to varying definitions of explicitness across different platforms and cultures. Step 1: Open the 'Settings' App Find the gray gear icon labeled 'Settings' on your home screen or in the app library. Tap on it to open the settings menu where various options related to phone customization are available. Step 2: Navigate to 'Screen Time' Scroll down until you find 'Screen Time'. It's located between 'Sounds & Haptics' and 'Control Center'. Clicking on 'Screen Time' allows you to monitor and control your usage time and set restrictions accordingly. Step 3: Select 'Content & Privacy Restrictions' Underneath Downtime and App Limits sections within Screen Time, there should be an option called 'Content & Privacy Restrictions'. Tapping this activates additional settings for controlling what type of content appears on your iPhone. Step 4: Enable Content Restrictions Toggle the switch next to 'Content Restrictions' so that it turns green. Once enabled, further options become accessible under this section. Step 5: Choose 'Music, Podcasts & News' Swipe up from 'Allowed Store Content' till you reach 'Music, Podcasts & News', then tap on it. Here, you can manage the settings specific to Apple Music, one of the default apps for playing media files on iPhones. Step 6: Change Ratings For Tap on 'Change Ratings For' right below the 'Explicit' label. A popup window will appear prompting you to choose which age rating system to use; select either 'Clean' or 'Explicit'. Clean restricts any explicit tracks while Explicit enables them. Step 7: Confirm Your Selection You'll be asked to confirm your selection by entering your passcode (if applicable) or Touch ID/Face ID depending upon your security measures setup earlier. After confirmation, the changes take effect immediately. Conclusion: By completing these seven steps, you have successfully blocked explicit music on your iPhone. Remember that this only applies to Apple Music and similar third-party streaming services might require separate configurations. Always remain vigilant about checking song ratings as certain exceptions could occur despite these precautionary measures. And stayed inside for more than two weeks. On 1 August 1774, British clergyman Joseph Priestley focused sunlight on mercuric oxide in a glass tube. From this experiment he got a gas that he called "dephlogisticated air". He found that candles burned more brightly in the gas and a mouse lived longer while breathing it. After breathing the gas, Priestley said that it felt like normal air, but his lungs felt lighter and easy afterwards. His findings were published in 1775. It is because his findings were published first that he is often said to have discovered oxygen. Because atoms of different elements react with one another in very specific proportions but atoms of different elements have different weights, chemists often describe the amount of different elements and compounds in terms of the number of "moles". A "mole" of any element contains the same number of atoms: 602,214,150,000,000,000,000,000 atoms. The atomic mass of an element can be used to see how much of the element makes a mole. For example, the atomic mass of copper is about 63.55. That means about 63.55 grams of copper metal has a mole of atoms. The atomic mass of chlorine is about 35.45. That means 35.45 grams of chlorine has a mole of atoms in it. " I'll be back in a moment." Toad the Wet Sprocket is an American alternative rock band from California. Comparatives and Superlatives Comparatives and superlatives are ways to compare different items. Comparatives are when there is a comparison of two items while superlatives compare three or more items. Alright, now we are talking comparatives and superlatives and I think it may sound like a pretty basic idea here. Generally, what you're going to do is, sometimes you're going to put adjectives or modifiers into a comparative form, sometimes you're going to put them into a superlative form. You're going to use the comparative form when you're comparing just two things and the regular form of that comparative is to add -er to the end, like the word 'smarter', alright? Some words that require less or more rather than the -er like, 'less intelligent'. So those are some of the regular forms. And then sometimes we have some irregular forms of the comparative; least, most, better, worse. So you've got to work those in where the words are necessitated. Now, if you were comparing three or more things, you've got to move to the superlative form. And the regular form of the superlative is to add -est to the end, so instead of smarter, smartest; instead of less intelligent, least intelligent. And those irregular forms shift as well, to least, most, worst and best. So that's really basic and sometimes it seems a little bit intuitive but there can be times where we get caught up on these, so let's take a look at some examples. Here in this sentence we've got 'Rachel is a better student than her brother; she goofs off less and studies more'. Now, everything looks good here. The first thing I'm going to do to make sure I'm okay is to check how many things are compared. So I've got Rachel and I've got her brother; I've got two things which means this should be in the comparative form. So, we have better with that -er on the end, we're good there and then we've got less and more, we are good there. The next example, if we wanted to take this to the superlative form we'd say 'Rachel is the best student in her family; she goofs off the least and studies the most'. So now we're looking at how many people are being compared; we've Rachel, but when we compare it to a group, her family; we're no longer at two, we're at more than two. So we've got to go to the superlative form and we're good here because we have 'best' and then we've moved this to least and most. So that's how it works in the basics. Let's take a look at some filling the blank spaces; 'Of all the people in the classroom, the teacher is by far the older or the oldest?' Let's take a look, so we've got the things being compared; the teacher and that teacher is being compared to all the people in the classroom. We know that that's more than two people so we've got to fill this in with the superlative. It should be, by far the 'oldest'. This one; and I think this is the easiest one to make a mistake on; it's the 'tallest' or 'taller' of the three buildings. I'm kind of inclined to say 'taller' but when I look at this I see that I've got three buildings that are being compared, now three is definitely more than two which means I've got to go superlative and I've got to say; 'It's the tallest building out of the three'. So, basically to remember your comparative and superlatives, the big trick is to check your numbers. Always go back, figure out how many things are being compared and then from there you can make the best decision. Title: Help Your Child Stay Awake in School: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: As a parent, you want your child to succeed academically. One crucial aspect of academic success is staying awake during school hours. This may seem like an obvious requirement, but many children struggle with sleepiness throughout the day due to various reasons such as irregular sleep schedules, poor sleep hygiene, or medical conditions. Here's our comprehensive guide on helping your child stay awake in school. Step 1: Establish a Consistent Sleep Schedule Regularizing your child's sleep schedule can significantly improve their alertness during school hours. Our bodies have internal clocks known as circadian rhythms that regulate feelings of sleepiness and wakefulness. By maintaining consistent bedtimes and wake-up times, even on weekends, you help reinforce these natural patterns, making it easier for your child to fall asleep at night and wake up feeling refreshed in the morning. Key Tip: Aim for 8-10 hours of sleep per night for elementary school kids, 9-11 hours for middle schoolers, and 8-10 hours for high school students (National Sleep Foundation). Step 2: Create a Restful Environment A calm, dark, cool room encourages better sleep quality. Consider using blackout curtains, earplugs, or white noise machines if necessary. Also, keep electronics out of the bedroom since screens emit blue light which suppresses melatonin production, disrupting the sleep cycle. Guideline: The ideal sleeping environment should be between 60 and 67 degrees Fahrenheit (National Sleep Foundation). Step 3: Encourage Regular Physical Activity Physical activity promotes healthy sleep habits by reducing stress levels and tiring out the body. However, try not to let your child engage in vigorous exercise right before bedtime because this could interfere with their ability to fall asleep. Key Tip: Thirty minutes of moderate physical activity daily can make a substantial difference (American Heart Association). Step 4: Limit Caffeine Intake Caffeine found in soda, energy drinks, coffee, tea, and certain medications can cause insomnia and restlessness. Therefore, limiting caffeine intake, especially close to bedtime, is essential. Guideline: Children aged 12 and under should avoid caffeine completely; adolescents aged 13-18 years old should consume less than 100mg per day (American Academy of Pediatrics). Step 5: Implement a Pre-Bed Routine Creating a calming pre-bed routine signals to your child's brain that it's time to wind down and prepare for sleep. Activities like reading a book, taking a warm bath, listening to soothing music, or practicing relaxation techniques can all contribute to improved sleep quality. Step 6: Watch Out for Sleep Disorders If despite following all these steps, your child still struggles with excessive daytime sleepiness, they might be dealing with a sleep disorder like narcolepsy, sleep apnea, or periodic limb movement disorder. Consult a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment. Conclusion: Helping your child establish good sleep habits early on sets them up for lifelong success. While getting enough sleep won't eliminate every instance of daytime drowsiness, regular sleep schedules, conducive environments, active lifestyles, limited caffeine consumption, and established routines will certainly minimize its impact on their academic performance. Title: How to Be a Good English Teacher Introduction: Teaching English can be an incredibly rewarding experience, both personally and professionally. However, being a good English teacher requires more than just knowing the language. It involves effective communication, creativity, patience, and a deep understanding of your students' needs and abilities. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the steps needed to become a successful English teacher. Step 1: Master the English Language Before you can teach English, you need to have a strong command over the language yourself. This includes grammar, vocabulary, pronunciation, and comprehension skills. You should be able to construct grammatically correct sentences, explain complex linguistic concepts clearly, and provide accurate feedback on students' work. Regularly reading English literature, watching English movies or TV shows, and engaging in conversations with native speakers are excellent ways to improve your proficiency. Step 2: Obtain Necessary Qualifications In most countries, teaching English requires specific qualifications such as a bachelor's degree in English or Education and certification in Teaching English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESOL), Teachers of English to Speakers of Other Languages (TESL) or a Teaching English as a Foreign Language (TEFL). These certifications typically involve completing coursework and practicum hours. Always check local regulations regarding teaching credentials. oh, he's kinda big for that bike, isn't he? This is important for surgeries and certain medical procedures. Anaesthesiologists also provide pre-operative assessments, ensuring the patient is safe during the operation and successfully awakens from anaesthesia after the operation. They assess for medical conditions and suitability for anaesthesia. They screen for risk factors prior to surgery and try to optimize the operative environment for the patient and the surgeon. They are the doctors who give epidurals during labor and delivery, provide spinal blocks, local nerve blocks, and general anaesthesia for procedures. They are the doctors who are especially trained in intubation (putting a tube into the lungs to help a person artificially breathe when the person is paralyzed and asleep during surgery). Hence, due to their skill in intubation, they are often the first line responders for emergencies. They help people who are in distress with their breathing, who have lost their airway or when their airway has become obstructed. (5) Finding the magnitude or length of a vector This drawing shows you how we set about doing this. Notice the right-hand coordinate system but with the z-axis vertical The formula for the length OP or |p| is a kind of 3 dimensional form of Pythagoras' theorem. Here's how the working would go if we had a = 4 and b = 3 and c = 12. Using Pythagoras' theorem in the yellow right-angled triangle we get OQ = the square root of (16 + 9) = 5 and then, using Pythagoras' theorem in the vertical green right-angled triangle, we get OP = the square root of (25 + 144) = 13. Alternatively, in just one step of working, we have OP = the square root of (16 + 9 + 144) = 13. A reminder on unit vectors Unit vectors are vectors which have unit length. They are important in many applications. In three dimensions, the vectors i, j and k which run along the x, y and z axes respectively, all have unit length. Sometimes, for example when working with planes, it is necessary to find a unit vector in the same direction as a given vector. Suppose you need the unit vector in the same direction as u = 2i - j + 2k or u = (2, -1, 2) written as a row vector. The length or magnitude of u is given by the square root of (4 + 1 + 4) = 3. Also, the required vector must be parallel to u. So we can get the vector we want by just scaling down u by a factor of 3. It is 1/3(2i - j + 2k) = 1/3(2, -1, 2) = (2/3, -1/3, 2/3). Unit vectors are often written with a little hat on top, so here we would have found . Here's a practical application which uses components and magnitude. If a body is acted on by three forces P, Q and S whose lines of action all pass through the same point, find the resultant force R, and its magnitude |R| if P = 3i - 5j + 2k Q = - 2i + 4j + 4k S = 3i - 3j - 4k. P + Q + S = R = (3 - 2 + 3)i + (- 5 + 4 - 3)j + ( 2 + 4 - 4)k = 4i - 4j + 2k and |R| = the square root of (16 + 16 + 4) = 6. We might also need to know what angles this resultant makes with the x, y and z axes. Finding these angles will come as a spin-off from discovering how we can multiply two vectors together. The World's Only Flying Mammals Did you know that there is only one mammal in the world that can fly? Many people think that flying squirrels, flying possums and colugos (flying lemurs, which actually aren't even lemurs) can fly. But, the can't. They can only glide for limited distances. The only mammal that is actually capable of powered flight is the bat. All bats belong to the order Chiroptera. The word Chiroptera comes from the Greek words cheir meaning "hand" and pteron, which means "wing" because the structure of the open wing is very similar to an outspread human hand. But, the bones of the arm and four of the fingers are light, slender and lengthened as an adaptation for flight. They are used to support, spread and manipulate the membrane. The only digit not attached to the wing is the thumb. It remains free for clinging to various surfaces. The knees on the hind limbs bend backward, but the feet face forward. The bat's wing is an extension of the skin on its body. It is two tightly stretched layers of skin membranes connected together by connective tissue without any flesh between the layers. This is why a bat's wings are translucent, which means you can see through them. This formation of skin membranes is called the patagium. Bat wings usually run from the shoulder region to the ankle, or in some cases, to the toes themselves. The wing membrane joins the body along the sides, except in a few cases in which it arises near the middle of the back. When the wing is not extended the membrane folds up along countless creases more efficiently than an umbrella. Bat biologists use different names to refer to different parts of the patagium. Here is a simplified illustration of a bat's wing. Roll your mouse over the image below to see the different parts of the membrane with descriptions. Wing and tail membranes appear naked on most bats. However, when you look closely you'll see that they are covered with tiny little hairs. Some species have distinctive tufts and fringes of hairs. A few species have a thick layer of fur on their tail membranes and some have thick hair on parts of their wings. While flapping their wings, bats move upward and downward through the air by moving the membrane between the body and fifth finger. This is called "lift." Bats move forward (called "thrust") by changing the shape of the membrane between the second and fifth fingers. With their flexible wings, bats can change the degree and direction of lift and thrust very quickly by positioning their wings into different shapes. They can also fold one of their wings to steer and brake. This is how they are able to weave and dive in the air like no other animal can. The rigid bird wing is more efficient at providing lift, but the flexible bat wing allows for greater maneuverability. Bats fly by using their hands and wings in a "rowing motion" through the air. Many bats may fold their wings on the upstroke. This helps to reduce air resistance. Both thrust and lift occur during the down stroke. Another way bat's wings are different than bird's wings is the fact that most of the flight muscles controlling the wing beat are attached to the shoulder blades. With birds, the muscles are braced against the rib cage, and they typically have a prominent breast keel where heavy muscles are attached. Bats only have one muscle attached to the breatbone, the ribs are flattened, and there is some fusing of the vertebral joints. These additional adaptations help make the bat incredibly agile in the air. The picture below gives you an inside view of how bats extend and fold their wings. You'll also see the heavy muscles attached to the shoulder blades. Click the left arrow to fold the wing and the right one to extend it. Can bats take off from the ground? Bats with longer, narrower wings cannot take off from the ground. They'll take off by dropping from a hanging position. But some bats, like some species of horseshoe bat and the common vampire bat, can take off from the ground. Most bats land by slowing down until they stall and grabbing hold of a branch or other surface. Common vampire bats land on the ground, so they can sneak up on their prey. Many species of flower-visiting bats and some species that take animal prey from the ground can hover. Bats can also move around on the ground. The first digit of the bat wing (similar to our thumb) is small and clawed. Bats use it to climb or to walk on the ground. Several species of bat are known to use their strong forelimbs and weak hind legs to crawl along the ground. Some insectivorous species feed on ground-dwelling insects as much or more than they feed on insects captured in flight. Common vampire bats also get around quite well on the ground. Cornell University biologist Daniel Riskin dropped a common vampire bat onto a treadmill. He was quite stunned when it broke into a bounding run. Common vampire bats of Central and South America can run up to about 2.5 miles per hour. New Zealand common vampire bats can get around pretty well on the ground, but they can't run. Some bats can also swim. There have been photos of the flying fox actually swimming, using its wings and feet to reach land rather than floating or paddling. Do you have an apron at home that you use? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV1/MT/tamtp1.cha = = = Activity source: Construction Specifications Institute Experiment 1: Loads and Forces, Buckling - Uncooked spaghetti Push on the ends of a piece of uncooked spaghetti. The sideways bending is called buckling. The compression force that you apply causes complex internal forces that bend the spaghetti sideways. If you push hard enough, it will snap. The snapping starts on the edge where the tension force within the spaghetti is great enough to pull it apart. Brittle materials like spaghetti, stone or glass break rather easily this way. Stone columns must be made so they won't buckle-because once they start to buckle, they will collapse.Experiment 2: Beams - Flat rubber eraser - Pencil, pen or marker Make a clapper bridge out of a flat rubber eraser. First mark the eraser with some parallel lines. Push on it. It is hard to break, but you will see something else too. The lines spread apart at the bottom edge. When you increase the load on the bridge, you are producing tension on the bottom edge. (You can also see compression on the top edge.) Long, heavy stone beams don't work because stone cannot take much tension without cracking.Experiment 3: Arches - 10 books - 2 small books Try making a thin, flexible arch of cardboard. Bend it carefully-a crease will make a weak spot and spoil the arch. Support the ends first with two small books. Push down to see how the arch pushes out on these abutments. Then using a pile of books push down to see how the arch pushes out on these abutments. Next use a pile of books at each end. When you push down, you will see the arch buckle at the sides.Experiment 4: Triangles and Trusses - Coffee stirrers - Pipe cleaners - Drinking straws - Paper clips - Strips of wood - Small finishing nails - Miniature marshamallows Here's the basic connection with coffee stirrers: Using four stirrers and four joiners, make a square frame. Notice that you can change its shape easily into a new form (a parallelogram). Now attach one more stirrer with two joiners to make the parallelogram into two triangles. A triangle tends to retain its shape when you push or pull on it. When you push on one corner, for instance, the other two corners try to spread apart, but the opposite sides holds them together. When you load this truss frame by pushing on it, it will keep its shape much better. Now build a structure using triangles as the basic building unit.Experiment 5: Suspension Bridge - 2 books Tie a string between two books. Push down on the string. The "cable" pulls down and inward, toppling the books. Now set up the books on a board. Pass the string over the books to thumbtack-anchors. Push down slowly, harder and harder. You'll be able to put a lot of load on the string-cable; you may even be able to push hard enough to lift the thumbtacks out of the board. yeah, but look I want to show you something watch if you hold it close enough look! The dragon smiled and said, " I just used a bit of magic and I yawned". Then it flew away in the clouds. The little girl was so happy that everything was ok. Once upon a time there was a very fragile bird. She lived in a lovely tree in the woods. Every day she went out and searched for tasty bugs. But one day a big scary cat leapt out of the bushes and tried to grab her. She flew away as fast as she could and wasn't caught, but she dropped some of the bugs she was carrying. The fallen bugs became waste that was spread around the forest. The poor fragile bird was very sad that the yummy bugs were now waste. So she worked hard to pick up all of the waste and put it back in the tree. Now every day the bird can eat all of the tasty bugs she can find, without having to worry about the big scary cat! This will stop you from turning pages. But the boy said I don't know. How to Be a Successful Singer Becoming a successful singer requires more than just having a good voice. It involves dedication, hard work, and a strategic approach to building your career. Here's an extensive guide to help you navigate this journey: Step 1: Develop Your Vocal Skills Before anything else, focus on honing your vocal abilities. This includes understanding breath control, pitch, rhythm, and dynamics. Consider taking singing lessons from a professional vocal coach who can provide personalized feedback and guidance. Practice regularly to build muscle memory and strengthen your vocals. Key Tip: Record yourself during practice sessions to track progress and identify areas needing improvement. Step 2: Find Your Unique Style Every great artist has their unique sound or style that sets them apart. Experiment with different genres, artists, and techniques until you find what feels most authentic to you. Remember, being original is crucial; don't try to mimic someone else's style entirely. Guideline: Listen to various types of music and note down which ones resonate with you emotionally. Try incorporating elements of these into your own performances. Step 3: Build a Repertoire Choose songs that showcase your strengths as a singer while also appealing to your target audience. Having a diverse repertoire will make you more versatile and marketable. Always keep learning new songs to stay fresh and relevant. Key Tip: Select songs within your vocal range to avoid strain and potential damage to your voice. Step 4: Perform Live Gain experience by performing live at local venues such as bars, cafes, open mic nights, or community events. These opportunities allow you to engage directly with audiences, receive immediate feedback, and grow comfortable performing under pressure. Guideline: Start small and gradually move towards larger platforms. Networking at smaller gigs could lead to bigger opportunities. Step 5: Create High-Quality Demos Title: How to Charge a Black & Decker Pivot Plus Battery The Black & Decker Pivot Plus is a versatile and powerful handheld vacuum cleaner that can help you keep your home tidy with ease. To ensure optimal performance, it's essential to charge its battery correctly. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of charging your Black & Decker Pivot Plus battery step-by-step, providing clear explanations, helpful tips, and best practices along the way. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Black & Decker Pivot Plus charger (model number CHV1410L) 2. Removable battery pack from the Pivot Plus vacuum 3. A wall outlet or power strip with available electrical socket(s) ### **Step 1: Unplug the Vacuum and Remove the Battery Pack** Before charging the battery, make sure the vacuum is turned off and unplugged from any power source. Next, remove the battery pack by pressing the release button located at the back of the handle near the bottom. The battery should slide out smoothly once the button is depressed. It's crucial to always remove the battery before charging as this prevents potential damage to the appliance due to overheating during the charging process. ### **Step 2: Connect the Charger to the Battery Pack** Locate the charging port on the top side of the battery pack; align it with the prongs on the charger. Insert the charger into the battery pack securely until you hear a click indicating proper connection. Make certain that both contacts are touching their respective metal plates on the battery pack to avoid damaging either the charger or the battery. ### **Step 3: Plug the Charger into an Electrical Outlet** Plug one end of the AC adapter cord into the back of the charger unit while ensuring the other end is connected to a functioning wall outlet or surge protector. Always double-check that the connections are firm and secure to prevent accidents caused by loose wires. ### **Step 4: Observe the Indicator Lights** Once plugged in, observe the indicator lights on the charger. When initially connecting the charger, the red light should illuminate, signifying that the battery is currently receiving a charge. After approximately four hours, when fully charged, the green light will turn on, indicating that the battery is ready for usage. Never disconnect the charger until the green light appears to ensure the battery reaches full capacity. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Only use the official Black & Decker charger specifically designed for the Pivot Plus model to guarantee safety and maintain warranty coverage. * Avoid leaving the charger connected after the battery has reached full capacity, as doing so may reduce overall battery life. * Store the battery in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight or heat sources when not in use. * If the battery doesn't seem to hold a charge efficiently anymore, consider purchasing a replacement battery rather than attempting to recharge a damaged one multiple times. * For maximum longevity, allow the battery to run down completely every few charges before recharging it again. By following these steps carefully and adhering to the provided tips and guidelines, you can effectively charge your Black & Decker Pivot Plus battery, ensuring peak performance and extending its lifespan. Happy cleaning! First I found a rocket then I put on my space helmet, and then I flew to the moon! Skilled readers rely on the spellings of words to the words found in text. It is important for students who are learning to rely on phoneme-grapheme correspondences. The goal for this lesson students to learn to spell and read words using the correspondence o_e = /O/. Students will have the opportunity to spell words with the o_e = /O/ correspondence and apply the spelling strategies to reading. *Elkonin letterboxes for each student The following letters in a plastic bag for each student: h, o, m, e, r, s, c, a, n, d, t, t, i, p, l, c, b, k (two t's are needed for this lesson) Large Elkonin letterboxes with the center of each box for the teacher The following letters with Velcro/tape on the back for the teacher (letters should be large enough for all students h, o, m, e, r, s, c, a, n, d, t, t, i, p, l, c, b, k (two t's are needed for this lesson) Markers/chalk for white List of words separated by the phonemes for the teacher rose, can, dome, tip flop, slope, spine, List of words spelled for each should be in a different order than when they were spelled) Flashcards of the following words: flat, smoke, dive, sneak, mope, crush, plop, Lists of words that are on the assessment (one for each student) 1. Before beginning the lesson, it would be a great idea to review some of the long vowel sounds that were previously learned such as a_e = /A/. To review this correspondence, the teacher may say "Do you remember when we learned about the /A/ sound and how there was something special about it? Does anyone remember what was so special about this sound, /A/?" Give students time to respond. If no one responds, take the opportunity to remind them that "some words with the /A/ sound have what we call a silent e. Let's see if we hear the /A/ sound in the word cane? /c/ /A/ /n/. I hear the /A/ sound, but let's see if we can spell this word on the board. The first sound that really sticks out in my mind in the word cane is the /A/ sound (teacher writes an a on the board). If we leave this letter (pointing to the a) here by itself, it makes the /a/ sound. Is there anything that we can do to make it say /A/?" Hopefully students the right response; if not, inform them that we can add a silent e at the very end. Help them further understand by telling them that the silent e pinches the a to make it say its name. The next sound that I hear the /k/ sound. (teacher writes a c) The last sound we hear in the word /c/ /A/ /n/ is the /n sound (teacher writes an n on the board). "It seems that we have all the sounds, and we see that this words says /c/ /A/ /n/, cane." *Elkonin letterboxes may be made for each student by cutting cardstock paper into 4" x 4" squares and taping them together so that easily fold. For teacher letterboxes, of a poster board may be used for each letterbox, using the same taping method. - After reviewing, ask the students "what do you say when you've dropped something or you've done something that you didn't mean to do?" Allow students to answer, hoping that one says 'uh oh'. If not, tell them "When I drop something or I do something that I didn't mean to do, I sometimes say 'uh oh'. Well today, we are going to learn about the /O/ sound and how and where we find it in words. - "Just as we just spelled words with /A/ sound in them, we can spell words with the /O/ sound the same way by adding a silent e. If I wanted to spell the word bone, I would say the word to my self and stretch it out so that I could hear each sound - /b/ /O/ /n/. In this word, the first sound that really sticks out is the /O/ sound." Write the letter o on the board. If I leave this letter (pointing to the o) here by itself, it makes the /o/ sound, but I want it to make the /O/ sound. To make it say the /O/ sound, I want to place a silent e at the end of my word. Remember that the silent e pinches the o and makes it say its name. Now that I have the /O/ sound, I want to get the rest of the sounds in the word /b/ /O/ /n/. The next sound that I hear is /b/." Write a b on the board. The last sound that I hear in the word /b/ /O/ /n/ is /n/." Explain to the students that sometimes we place the letter n between the o and the e because the e is so silent, it's almost as if it isn't there. - Allow the students to practice finding the /O/ sound by giving them example words such as joke, club, and cone. During this time, ask students for their thought processes in finding the /O/ sound. With each word that has the /O/ sound, go to the board and allow the students to instruct you on the spelling. - "Now that we've learned about the /O/ sound and we've figured out how to find it in words that we say, we're going to see if we can spell words with the /O/ sound in them." At this time, give students their individual Elkonin boxes and plastic bags. Ask students to remove their letters from the bag and fold their Elkonin boxes so that three of them are showing. Also take this time to set up your letterboxes on the board. - "Remember when I said that I wanted to spell the word bone and I said each sound to myself and then wrote it on the board? Well, that's what I want you to do as we begin spelling these words. Each individual sound, NOT letter, goes into each letterbox." Repeat if necessary. "Since we're using the letterboxes, the silent e that we sometimes find at the end of words goes outside of the letterboxes because we don't hear it." For example, if we use the word joke that we spelled earlier, we would say the word to ourselves so that we hear each individual sound. When we found the sounds, we would put the /j/ sound in the first letterbox, the /O/ sound in the second letterbox, and the /k/ sound in the third letterbox." Teacher places sounds in the appropriate letterboxes as he/she instructs. "If we read this word, it would say /j/ /o/ /k/. We want it to say /j/ /O/ /k/, so we would need to place the silent e outside the letterboxes to let us know that the o is going to say its name. - "Since everyone has their letterboxes folded to where only three are showing, I think we're ready to begin spelling words! Remember to think about each sound that you hear and place each sound into a different letterbox. Using three letterboxes, the first word I want you to spell is home. After I left school, I went straight home." Repeat the word and same/different sentence, allowing students to spell the word and place each sound in the correct box. Take this time to walk around and observe each student as they begin spelling words. If you notice any problems students are having, take this time to model for them how to spell the word as you did with bone, and joke. After students are done spelling the word, ask them to clear all the letters from the letterboxes. Continue this procedure by finishing all the three phoneme words (rose, can, dome, tip), then the four phoneme words (slope, spine, close, state, flop) and then the five phoneme words (stroke). Ask students to change the number of letterboxes at the end of each list. - Once all the words have been spelled, give each student a list of words. "Now that we've finished spelling our words, we are going to read this word list from top to bottom. As I clap, I want you to read each word." - After reading the list, gather the students together and give a brief book talk about the book Is Jo Home? "This is a story about a little dog who really wants to play with Jo. He searches and searches for Jo so they can play together. In order to find out what Jo and the dog do, we're going to read the story. While you're reading, I want you to pay attention to words with the /O/ sound and see how they are spelled." - For assessment, allow each student to read Is Jo Home? and take a running record. Then show each student flashcards with words including o_e = /O/ and previous correspondences learned. Allow the student to read each word while you check the words on a list, making note of any miscues. The word list may include words such as flat, smoke, dive, sneak, mope, crush, plop, stone, and crop. Words with the /o/ sound will be useful in order for students to be able to distinguish between the short and long o. Barton, Sarah. Oooohh My! Cushman, Shelia. Is Jo Home? Carson, CA. [picks up the yellow block, gives it to GRF] Title: How to Determine If an Answer to a Math Problem is Even or Odd Introduction: In mathematics, numbers are classified as either even or odd. An even number is any integer that can be divided by 2 without leaving a remainder. On the other hand, an odd number cannot be divided evenly by 2; division will always leave a remainder of 1. This article aims at teaching you how to determine whether the answer to a math problem is even or odd. Step 1: Understand What Constitutes an Even Number The first step towards determining if a math solution is even or odd involves understanding what constitutes an even number. As mentioned before, even numbers are those divisible by two with no leftover. Examples include -4, 0, 2, 6, etc. They all end in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8 when expressed as integers. Key Tip: Memorize these ending digits (0, 2, 4, 6, 8) because they're crucial in identifying even numbers quickly. Step 2: Recognize Odd Numbers Just like recognizing even numbers, being able to identify odd numbers plays a significant role here. By definition, odd numbers have remainders when divided by two-that remaining unit makes them 'odd.' For instance, the number 3 gives a remainder of 1 upon division by 2. Other examples include 1, -3, 5, 7, etc., which don't end in any of the even-number digits. Guideline: Remember, odd numbers never end in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8. Instead, look out for numbers that terminate in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9. Step 3: Perform Calculations Now let's get into some actual arithmetic! Begin solving your mathematical problem just as you normally would-addition, subtraction, multiplication, exponentiation, roots, logarithms...whatever operation(s) necessary until you arrive at your final answer. Step 4: Identify Remainder After Division by Two After obtaining your result from Step 3, check if there's a remainder after dividing it by two. You can do this mentally, using long division, or employing a calculator's modulus function (%). Key Insight: The presence or absence of a remainder determines whether the result is even or odd respectively. Step 5: Interpret Your Findings If there was no remainder in Step 4, congratulations! Your answer is an even number. However, if there was indeed a remainder, then sorry but your answer turns out to be odd. Common Mistake Avoidance: Don't forget about negative numbers! Despite their appearance, negative integers (-1,-2,-3...) fall under the same even/odd categorization explained above. Conclusion: By following these steps, you should now feel comfortable deciding whether the response to a math question is even or odd. Practice regularly to reinforce this skillset since familiarity breeds speediness and accuracy. Happy calculating! Title: How to Cut a Hoodie Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of cutting a hoodie to create a new fashion statement or repurpose an old one. This project requires basic sewing skills and tools. Be sure to carefully follow the steps to ensure a successful result. Materials Needed: * A hoodie * Fabric scissors * Measuring tape * Straight pins * Sewing machine (optional) * Thread (optional) Step 1: Choose Your Design Determine what kind of cut you want to make. You can opt for a simple trim around the hem, sleeves, or collar; fringe accents; or even turn your hoodie into a vest by removing the bottom half. Decide which look suits you best before proceeding. Step 2: Prepare Your Hoodie Wash and dry your hoodie according to its care label to prevent shrinkage after alteration. Iron the garment if necessary for easier cutting. Lay the hoodie flat on a stable surface with all seams smooth and aligned. Step 3: Mark Your Cut Line Using the measuring tape, measure and mark the desired length along the area you wish to cut. For example, if creating sleeve cuffs, measure from the current cuff's edge towards the elbow where you want the new cuff to end. Make small marks using straight pins at regular intervals (e.g., every 5 inches). Key Tip: Always err on the side of caution when deciding upon cut lengths - you can always shorten further but lengthening is much more challenging! Step 4: Connect the Dots Connect the marked points using chalk or another fabric marker. Draw a continuous line following the contour of the hoodie. If making multiple cuts, repeat this process as needed. Guideline: When drawing curved lines, keep the shape natural-looking rather than trying to make perfect circles or arcs. The human body has curves, so should your clothing. Step 5: Double Check Measurements Before cutting, double-check that your measurements are accurate and symmetrical. Compare both sides of the hoodie to ensure consistency. It may help to have someone else take a glance too since fresh eyes often spot discrepancies missed otherwise. Step 6: Start Cutting Carefully begin cutting along your marked lines using sharp fabric scissors. Apply steady pressure while keeping your movements slow and controlled. Avoid jagged edges by maintaining consistent blade direction and avoiding sudden changes in angle. Key Tips: * Use dedicated fabric scissors instead of paper or craft ones. Their sharper blades provide cleaner cuts. * Keep hands clear of the path of the scissor blades to avoid accidents. Step 7: Finish Raw Edges (Optional) For a polished appearance, finish raw edges using a serger or zigzag stitch on a sewing machine. Alternatively, fold the edge under twice and securely stitch down with a matching thread color. Step 8: Try On Your New Look Slip into your newly altered hoodie and admire your handiwork. Adjust any areas needing improvement now that you see how they lay on your body. Remember, practice makes perfect - don't be discouraged if your first attempt isn't flawless. With patience and persistence, you'll master cutting hoodies like a pro! After you have a nap then you can go outside. Nicole Kidman was born in Honolulu, Hawaii while her Australian parents were temporarily in the United States on educational visas. Kidman can therefore claim citizenship in Australia and the United States. Her father, Antony David Kidman (1938-2014) was a biochemist, clinical psychologist, and author. Her mother, Janelle Ann (n e Glenny), is a nursing instructor who edits her husband's books and was a member of the Women's Electoral Lobby. Kidman is of Scottish and Irish ancestry. At the time of Kidman's birth, her father was a graduate student at the University of Hawaii at Manoa. He soon became a visiting fellow at the National Institute of Mental Health of the United States. Opposed to the war in Vietnam, which was causing social unrest in both Australia and the United States, Kidman's parents participated in anti-war protests while they were living in Washington, D.C. The family returned to Australia when Kidman was four and her parents now live on Sydney's North Shore. Kidman has a younger sister, Antonia Kidman, a journalist and TV presenter. As these youths flourished in the early days of liberty, equality, and fraternity, and were near neighbors, they naturally hated each other. Their enmity commenced at school, where the delicate and refined De Chaulieu, being the only gentilhomme among the scholars, was the favorite of the master, (who was a bit of an aristocrat in his heart,) although he was about the worst dressed boy in the establishment, and never had a sou to spend; while Jacques Rollet, sturdy and rough, with smart clothes and plenty of money, got flogged six days in the week, ostensibly for being stupid and not learning his lessons,--which, indeed, he did not,--but, in reality, for constantly quarrelling with and insulting De Chaulieu, who had not strength to cope with him. Their goal is to try to pull together material they learned in earlier modules to draw some conclusions about drug use. - Have the students watch the DVD. Stop the DVD at the break. Make sure that each student has all the Instruction Sheets, Fact Sheets, and notes from previous modules, as well as the scrapbooks from Module 1. - Tell students that their challenge is to add a page to their scrapbooks from Module 1 to reflect what they have learned during the program. These pages will serve as a before and after summary and will assess their growth and understanding over time about the brain and drugs. Based on what they know now, how would they depict people drinking and smoking in ads? How would they describe drug use to friends? - Give the students class time to modify their scrapbooks. They may choose to find new pictures to put in, or they may simply change what they say about the images. - Finish watching the DVD. When the DVD is over, bring the class back together to share their scrapbooks. What conclusions did the students come to? Divide the class again into two groups (A and B) and instruct each group to come up with four to six trivia questions about drugs and addiction to ask the other group. Give the students time to come up with questions and then play the game. If students have difficulty coming up with questions on their own, have them use these examples: - What is it called when a person needs more of a drug to produce the same effect? Answer: tolerance - What is it called when someone who is addicted to drugs feels bad when not using drugs? Answer: withdrawal - One member from Group A should read the question to be answered by Group B. Group B should discuss among themselves before agreeing on an answer. Group B has two options at this point: (1) Group B members can agree on an answer that a group member will announce, or (2) they can ask for a clue from Group A. The point system is explained in the chart below. |How Question is Asked||Right or Wrong||Points Awarded| |Group B with no help||Right Answer||2 points for Group B| |Group B with a clue from Group A||Right Answer||2 points for Group B, and 1 point for Group A| |Group B with no help||Wrong Answer||No points awarded| |Group B with a clue from Group A||Wrong Answer||No points awarded| - After all the questions have been asked to both groups, declare the group with the higher number of points the winner. Take the time now to emphasize how collaboration results in more points for both groups in this activity. Explain how scientists benefit from collaboration as well. When researchers work together, they make better progress. - Discuss what addiction means. Ask whether that piece of information changes their opinions about drug use. - Discuss what collaboration means. Ask whether scientists benefit more from competition or collaboration. It does this by folding the sea floor in towards the centre of the Earth - this is called subduction. This bumping and grinding is hard so there are many earthquakes and volcanoes when the pressure builds up and is quickly released as large explosions of hot rocks and dust. When an earthquake happens under the sea, the quick jerk causes a tsunami. This is why tsunamis are more common around the edge of the Pacific than anywhere else. Title: How to Block Someone on TikTok on Your iPhone or iPad TikTok is a popular social media platform that allows users to create and share short videos. However, there may be instances where you want to block someone due to various reasons such as harassment, spamming, or discomfort. This tutorial will guide you through the process of blocking someone on TikTok using your iPhone or iPad with clear, concise, and easy-to-follow steps. Step 1: Open the TikTok app Start by locating the TikTok icon (a white music note inside a black square) on your device's home screen or app library and tap on it to open the application. Log in if necessary. Step 2: Navigate to the user's profile There are two ways to access the user's profile: * Method A: If you have received a video from the person you wish to block, simply tap their username displayed above the video. * Method B: Alternatively, go to your account page by tapping the 'Me' button at the bottom right corner of the main interface, then select 'Following'. Find the target individual in your follower list and tap on their name to proceed. This action directs you to the specific user's profile page, which contains information about them including their number of followers, likes, videos posted etc. Key tip: Remember, only those who follow each other can see private accounts' content; otherwise, they can only view limited details like the account holder's username, profile picture, and bio. Step 3: Access the three-dot menu Once you're on the intended user's profile page, locate the three horizontal dots ('...') situated near the top right corner of the screen. These represent further options related to this particular user. Guideline: Be aware that these settings allow you to manage interactions between yourself and others, promoting safety and positivity within the community. Step 4: Select 'Block' from the dropdown menu After pressing the three-dot icon, a popup window should appear listing several actions you can take regarding this user. Scroll down until you find 'Block', then tap on it. Important note: Blocking prevents the blocked user from interacting with your content, commenting on your posts, sending messages, or following you again once unfollowed. It does not notify the blocked user about being blocked unless they attempt to perform one of the restricted actions mentioned earlier. Step 5: Confirm blocking action A confirmation box will now appear asking if you're sure you want to block the selected user. Choose 'Confirm' to finalize the blocking process. Congratulations! You have successfully blocked another user on TikTok via your iPhone or iPad. Remember, everyone has the right to feel safe and comfortable online, so don't hesitate to utilize this feature whenever needed. You need a rubber stick to play that xylophone, don't. By the way, what I started to tell you was that the Towners had decided to kidnap you, Sammy. It may be wrong to give them away, but there are some things I just can't stand for!" [moves the wand, with objects attached, back and forth through the air] I know you can cry about it, but I can't help you. Music is most commonly notated using the Staff (and tablature.) The staff consists of five horizontal lines on which musical notes lie. The lines and the spaces between the lines represent different pitches. Lower pitches are lower on the staff and higher pitches are higher on the staff.With the blank staff we can't yet tell what notes to play. We use Clefs to tell us which notes correspond to which lines or spaces. The most common clefs are the Treble Clef (also known as the G Clef) and the Bass Clef (or F Clef). The Treble Clef spirals around the second line from the bottom. This spiral tells us that notes on this line are G. From here we can figure out the other note names simply by going forward or backward through the musical alphabet: A,B,C,D,E,F,G. The note names in Bass Clef are: oh, and look he's in this cute little basket. No I'll help you walk over here though. Title: How to Get a Rotary Dialer on YourAndroid Device Introduction: While rotary phones have become obsolete, many people still find them charming and nostalgic. This tutorial will guide you through the process of getting a rotary dialer on your Android device, allowing you to enjoy that classic experience on a modern platform. Although there isn't an actual physical rotary dialer available for Android devices, we can simulate the look and feel with third-party apps. The app we will be using is called "Rotary Phone Dialer." Step 1: Check Compatibility Before proceeding, ensure your Android device meets the following requirements: Android version 4.1 (Jelly Bean) or higher At least 20 MB of free storage space These are standard requirements for most Android applications, so if your device runs smoothly with other apps, you should have no issues installing and running the Rotary Phone Dialer app. Step 2: Allow App Installation from Unknown Sources By default, Google Play Protect scans all apps and prevents installation from unknown sources. However, since the Rotary Phone Dialer app is not available on the Google Play Store, you need to allow app installations from outside sources temporarily. Here's how: 1. Open the Settings app on your Android device. 2. Scroll down and select 'Security.' If you don't see this option directly under settings, try searching for Security within the search bar at the top of the screen. 3. Find 'Unknown sources' and toggle it ON. A warning message may appear; read it carefully before confirming by tapping OK. Key tip: After completing the installation, remember to turn off the 'Unknown sources' setting to maintain security on your device. Step 3: Download the Rotary Phone Dialer APK File Since the app is unavailable on the Google Play Store, download its APK file from a trusted source like APKMirror or APKPure. Follow these steps once you have downloaded the APK file successfully: 1. Navigate to where the APK was saved (usually in the 'Downloads' folder). 2. Tap the Rotary Phone Dialer APK file to start the installation process. 3. Grant necessary permissions when prompted during the installation. Step 4: Set Up and Use the Rotary Phone Dialer App Once installed, open the Rotary Phone Dialer app and familiarize yourself with its interface. It mimics the design of old rotary phones, complete with a spinning wheel to input numbers and realistic sound effects. To make calls using the app, simply spin the wheel to enter phone numbers manually. Additional Tips & Guidelines: * For better accuracy while entering numbers, place two fingers on the wheel instead of one. * You can customize various aspects of the app, such as changing background images, adjusting call volume, etc., via the settings menu inside the app. * Remember that emergency services cannot always be reached through VoIP apps like this one; please verify local regulations and consider keeping another means of calling nearby. Conclusion: With these simple steps, you now know how to bring back memories of yesteryears right onto your Android smartphone! Enjoy making calls using the retro-style rotary dialer without sacrificing convenience or functionality. He almost can save the um something... Now first, you and I ide- identify what parent graph is being transformed and here it's the function f of x equals the absolute value of x. And so it helps to remember what the shape of that graph is. Absolute value looks like this, it's got a little corner at the bottom. And there are three key points that I usually like to start with. There's the point -1 1, the point 0 0 and the point 1 1, and my technique here is basically to take points of my parent graph and transform those points first and then plot the transformed points. The turning point of the graph and so I want to see where the vertex ends up because that'll be my new vertex. Now, when you look at this function, the function's basically saying, multiply the absolute value of x by negative one half and then add three. Now this means two things. First of all, all the transformations are going to happen on this side of the column and secondly there what are the transformations? This multiplication by negative one half what does that do? Well, first of all, multiplying by a negative number is going to flip the graph across the x axis. Multiplying by a half is a vertical compression of the graph, and adding three will shift the graph up. So we have x, negative one half absolute value of x plus three. But you'll see all that when you do the Arithmetic on these numbers. Now first of all we'll just carry the x values over cause nothing's happening inside the absolute value. So those are the x values. And then for each of these absolute value of x values, I'm going to multiply them by negative one half and add three. So one times negative one half is negative one half plus 3 is 2.5, 0 times negative one half is 0 plus 3 is 3. And 1 times negative one half is negative one half plus 3 is 2.5. That's not a bad start. Let's plot these points. We've got 0 3, this is 2, 3. We've got -1 2.5, so that's -1 is here 2.5 is here and one 2.5. Those points are kind of close to my y intercept. Let's just plot some points that are further out. So let's say oh -6 and 6. -6 the absolute value is 6, 6 the absolute value is 6. Those x values will translate right over oops positive 6 but what happens to the absolute value? Well we multiply by negative one half and add 3. So this times negative one half is -3 plus 3, 0. Again 6 times negative one half is -3 plus 3 is 0. So we get we get -6 0 and 6 0, and those are these points here and here and that's going to give us a much better graph if we use points that are further away from the y intercept. Title: How to Enter the Drawing for a Free Gas Card on GasBuddy Introduction: GasBuddy is a popular mobile app and website that helps users find the best gas prices near them. They also run promotions and giveaways, including a weekly drawing for a free gas card. This tutorial will guide you through entering the GasBuddy drawing for a chance to win free gas. By following these steps, you'll increase your chances of winning and learn about potential benefits along the way. Step 1: Create or Log into Your GasBuddy Account Visit the GasBuddy homepage (gasbuddy.com) or open their mobile app. If you don't have an account yet, click "Sign Up" at the top right corner of the page or sign up within the app. You can create an account using email, Google, or Facebook. Having an account allows GasBuddy to track your entries and notify you if you win. Key tip: Make sure to remember your login credentials as you'll need them every time you want to enter the drawing. Step 2: Navigate to the Giveaway Page Once logged in, look for the "Win Free Gas" banner or button, usually located at the top or bottom of the page. On the mobile app, this option might be found under the menu icon. Clicking on it should take you directly to the sweepstakes entry form. Guideline: The location of the "Win Free Gas" link may change depending on updates from GasBuddy. Look around carefully until you find it. Step 3: Fill Out the Entry Form The form typically requires basic information such as name, email address, zip code, and sometimes phone number. Be honest when filling out the form; providing accurate details ensures proper communication with GasBuddy regarding any winnings. Remember, they respect user privacy and won't share personal data without permission. Key Tip: Double-check all fields before submitting your entry. Errors could lead to disqualification or delayed notification if you win. Step 4: Confirm Submission After hitting 'Submit', wait for a confirmation message indicating successful entry. Sometimes, GasBuddy sends a confirmation email to verify your submission. Opening and clicking the verification link confirms your participation in the drawing. Guideline: Keep an eye on both your inbox and spam folder for the confirmation email. It might take several minutes to arrive due to server delays. Step 5: Increase Entries by Finding Local Stations Some weeks offer bonus entries based on finding and reporting local station prices via the GasBuddy platform. Using the search feature, locate nearby stations and report current fuel prices. Each verified price update adds more entries into the drawing. Key Tip: Always ensure reported prices are correct to avoid penalties like temporary bans from future drawings. Step 6: Check Regularly for Winning Notifications Drawings occur once per week, so check back frequently for results. Typically, winners receive an email or see a congratulatory notice upon logging into their GasBuddy accounts. Respond promptly if contacted, as failing to do so may result in forfeiting the prize. Guideline: Set reminders or notifications if possible to stay updated about new drawings and potential wins. Conclusion: By consistently participating in GasBuddy's free gas card draw, you maximize your opportunities to save money on fuel costs. Plus, staying engaged with the community encourages better driving habits while promoting competition among fellow drivers. Happy saving! This was a traditional paper size used in Europe and the British Commonwealth, before the adoption of the international standard A4 paper, the most common standard size in the world.
