Mr. Shiner, according to the interesting rule laid down, deserted his own partner, and made off down the middle with this fair one of Dick's-the pair appearing from the top of the room like two persons tripping down a lane to be married. Dick trotted behind with what was intended to be a look of composure, but which was, in fact, a rather silly expression of feature-implying, with too much earnestness, that such an elopement could not be tolerated. Then they turned and came back, when Dick grew more rigid around his mouth, and blushed with ingenuous ardour as he joined hands with the rival and formed the arch over his lady's head; which presumably gave the figure its name; relinquishing her again at setting to partners, when Mr. Shiner's new chain quivered in every link, and all the loose flesh upon the tranter-who here came into action again-shook like jelly. The purple or blue stripe down each arm distinguish this ten armed sea star. It can be very pale yellow to completely blue on it's smooth surface. The arms are moderately long (R/r = 2.3 to 4.4)*. This animal is usually found on cobbled beaches but can be found on sand. It is a carnivore which feeds primarily on sea cucumbers. The animal on the right is not in distress but takes on this shape so that it can tumble across the bottom in the along-shore currents. The author assumes that it moves from one feeding area to another by this method. * Two measurements need to be made in order to determine the relative length of the arms of a sea star. Simply stated, a sea star is made up of arms and a central area to which they are attached called the disk. Since a sea star has radial symmetry, their dimensions are referred to radii. "R" is the length of the arm as measured from the center of the animal to the tip of the arm. "r" is the radius of the disk and is measured from the center to a notch between the arms. R divided by r (R/r) is the relative length of the arm. For instance: Distance from center of the disk to the tip of the are is 10" and the distance from the center to the notch between the arms is 2 1/2", then: R/r = 10/2.5 = 4 Well um after that I can't find my mom. A kind of a simple function. Both of these are going to be equal to 1, for all other x'es other than 1. But - at x=1, it becomes undefined. This is undefined and this one's undefined. At 1, f(x) is undefined, so - I'm gonna put a little bit of a gap right over here, this circle, to signify that this function - is not defined - we don't know what this function equals at 1, we never defined it. - This definition of the function doesn't tell us what to do at 1 - it's literally undefined when x=1. - So this is the function right over here, and so once again, if someone were to ask you what is f(1), you'd go... - and let's say, well this was a function definition, you would go x=1. Oh wait, there is a gap in my function - over here, it is undefined. So let me write it again... well, it's kind of redundant but I'll rewrite it. - f(1) is undefined. But what if I were to ask you, what is the function approaching - as x=1? And now, this is starting to touch on the idea of a limit. So as x gets closer and closer to 1... - what is the function approaching? Well this entire time, what is it getting closer and closer to? - On the left hand side, no matter how close you get to 1, as long as you're not at 1, f(x)=1. - Over here from the right hand side, you get the same thing. So you could say - and you'll get - more and more familiar with this idea as we do more examples - that the limit as - x (and lim, short for limit) - as x approaches 1 of f(x) is equal to... - As we get closer we can get unbelievably, infinitely close to 1 as long as we're not at 1... - And our function is going to be equal to 1, it's getting closer and closer to 1, - it's actually at 1 the entire time. So in this case, we can say the limit as x approaches 1 of f(x) - is 1. So once again, has very fancy notation, we're just saying, "Look, what is the function approaching - as x gets closer and closer to 1?" - Let me do another example where we're dealing with a curve, just so that you have the general idea. - So let's say that I have the function f(x) - let me, just for the sake of variety, let me call it g(x). - Let's say that we have g(x) is equal to - I can define it this way, we can define it as x - when x does not equal 2, and let's say that when x=2, it is equal to 1. So once again, kind of an interesting - function that - as you'll see - is not fully continuous. It has a discontinuity. Let me graph it. - So this my y=f(x) axis, this is my x-axis right over here. Let's say this is x=1, this is x=2, - this is -1, this is -2... So everywhere except x=2, it's equal to x . So let me draw it like this, - this is gonna be a parabola, it looks something like this... It's gonna look something... - Let me draw a better version of the parabola. Alright. - Now, this should be the graph of just x , but it's not x when x=2. On the graph of f(x)=x this would be 4, this would be 2, - this would be 1, this would be 3. So, x=2, our function is equal to 1. - So this is a bit of a bizarre function, but you can define it this way, you can define a function however - you like to define it! And so, notice, it's just like the graph of f(x)=x except when you get to 2, - it has this gap, because you don't use the "g(x)=x when x=2", you use "g(x)=1". - If I've been saying f(x), I apologize for that. - You use g(x)=1, so then just exactly at 2, it drops down to 1, and then it keeps going along x . - So there is a couple of things. If I were to just evaluate the function - g(2), - well you look at this definition. Okay, when x=2, I use this situation right over here, - and it tells me it's going to be equal to 1. Several fossils have been found in the Hell Creek Formation about 40,000 years later than the K/T extinction event. Many scientists dismiss the "Paleocene dinosaurs" as re-worked, that is, washed out of their original places and then re-buried in much later sediments. An associated skeleton (e.g. more than one bone from the same individual) found above the K/T boundary would be convincing, but no such finds have been reported. One day, Max found a big banana but it was too big for him to eat. Max asked his friend, a rabbit named Rosie, "Can you help me split this banana?" This CDC Kidtastics podcast discusses things kids and parents can do to help prevent illness by keeping food safe. Created: 5/27/2009 by National Center for Zoonotic, Vector-Borne, and Enteric Diseases (NCZVED). Date Released: 5/27/2009. Series Name: CDC Kidtastics. [Announcer] This podcast is brought to you by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC - safer, healthier people. [Kaya] Hi kids! Welcome to CDC Kidtastics Radio! I'm Kaya Kidtastic! Today, we're going to talk about food safety. [Chris] Food safety? You mean food has to look both ways before crossing the street? [Kaya] No. Food safety means keeping food safe - before, during, and after it's served. If we don't, bad germs can grow. [Karmen] So how do we keep food safe? [Kaya] To start with, some foods need to be stored in the refrigerator or freezer and some foods need to be cooked so germs don't grow. [Caydan] What happens if a bad germ stays on the food and I eat it? [Kaya] You could get very sick. [Caydan] Oh no! [Kaya] I'm going to teach you some great food safety tips so you can help your parents and other adults be 'food safe' in the kitchen! [Kaya] Kids, you should always have an adult with you when you're making something in the kitchen. OK. There are four words you need to remember: CLEAN, SEPARATE, COOK, CHILL. [Kids] CLEAN, SEPARATE, COOK, CHILL [Kaya] So, what's the first word? [Kaya] Right! Make sure everyone cleans their hands with warm, soapy water. Then, have an adult clean all the surfaces that foods touch, like counters and cutting boards. [Kaya] What's the next word? [Kaya] Good! Make sure you and your parents keep cooked foods separate from uncooked foods. Don't use the same fork, knife, or spoon on cooked foods that was used on uncooked foods, unless it's washed first with hot, soapy water. Do the same thing with plates. A cooked hamburger should never be put on the same plate it was on before it was cooked. [Kaya] Who knows the third word? [Caydan] I do! I do! COOK! [Kaya] Make sure you and your parents cook all foods to the proper temperature. You can find temperature information at www.usda.gov. Use a meat thermometer when cooking any type of meat. Never, ever eat raw foods that are supposed to be cooked, like cookie dough. Cookie dough has raw eggs in it, so wait until the cookies are totally baked before eating them. [Chris] Aww man, I love cookie dough! [Kaya] I know. But you can get really sick if you eat the dough before it's cooked. [Chris] OK, I won't eat cookie dough. [Kaya] What's the last important word? [Kaya] Right. Chill foods right away after using them. Never leave food out of the refrigerator for more than two hours. When you bring your lunch to school, use an ice pack to keep the food cold. [Karmen] Ok, I've got it. We need to tell everyone the four food safety words they need to remember: CLEAN, [Chris] COOK, and [Kaya] Right! It sounds like you know all about food safety! Now go spread the words, not the germs! [Kaya] Thanks for listening to CDC Kidtastics Radio. We'll talk to you again soon. Until then, be a safer, healthier kid! [Announcer] For the most accurate health information, visit www.cdc.gov or call 1-800-CDC-INFO, 24/7. And Lisa said that she would have a happy meal um with coke and a chocolate ice cream cone. Title: How to Burn an ISO File on Windows ISO files are disk image formats that contain everything present on a disc, including the file system and data. They can be burned onto CDs, DVDs, or USB drives using various tools available on Windows operating systems. This tutorial will guide you through burning an ISO file on Windows using its built-in tool - Windows Disc Image Burner. **Step 1: Downloading and Obtaining Your ISO File** Before you start burning your ISO file, ensure you have downloaded it from a reliable source. You may need administrative rights to save and burn the ISO file. **Step 2: Locating Your ISO File** Once downloaded, locate the ISO file on your computer. It might be in your 'Downloads' folder or wherever you choose to save downloaded files. Remember the location as you'll need to navigate there later. **Step 3: Inserting a Blank Disc** Insert a blank writable CD/DVD into your optical drive (Blu-ray disks won't work with this method). The disc should have enough space for all contents of the ISO file. A prompt may appear asking what action to take with the inserted media; close it if it does. **Step 4: Opening Windows Explorer** Press `Win + E` simultaneously to open Windows Explorer. Alternatively, search for 'File Explorer' in the Start menu and click on it. **Step 5: Navigating to the ISO File** In the left pane under 'Quick access', find and click on the drive where you saved the ISO file earlier. Then, locate and right-click on the ISO file. From the context menu, select 'Burn disc image'. **Step 6: Using Windows Disc Image Burner** The Windows Disc Image Burner window will pop up. Here, verify that the correct drive is selected under 'Disc burner'. Also, make sure the box next to 'Verify disc after burning' is checked if you want to check for errors once the process finishes. **Key Tip**: Verifying the disc ensures the integrity of the burnt disc but extends the overall time required for the operation. Uncheck this option if speed is more important than error checking. **Step 7: Starting the Burn Process** Click on the 'Burn' button at the bottom right corner of the Window Disc Image Burner window. Depending upon the size of the ISO file, the burning process could take several minutes. During this period, avoid removing the disc or shutting down your computer. **Step 8: Completion Notification** Upon completion, a notification sound plays, and a message appears stating, "The disc was successfully burned." If you chose to verify the disc, wait until this procedure completes before ejecting the disc. **Guideline**: Always test your newly created disc by attempting to access its content immediately after creation. Issues encountered post-burning could indicate potential problems with either the original ISO file or the burning process itself. By following these steps meticulously, you've now learned how to burn an ISO file onto a disc using only built-in Windows tools! You can tell me what we drew after we're done. ADDING LIKE TERMS Here is a sum of like terms: 4x - 5x + x Each term has the same literal factor, x . Only the coefficients are different. The coefficient of x in the first term is 4. The coefficient in Here, on the other hand, is a sum of unlike terms: x - 2xy + y What number is the coefficient of x ? To see the answer, pass your mouse over the colored area. What number is the coefficient of xy? -2 What number is the coefficient of y ? 1 Actually, the coefficient of any factor is all the remaining factors. Thus in the term 4ab, the coefficient of a is 4b; the coefficient of 4a is b; and so on. In this term -- Adding like terms In this sum -- 2x + 3y + 4x - 5y -- the like terms are 2x and 4x, 3y and -5y. What do we do with like terms? We add, or combine, them: 2x + 3y + 4x - 5y = 6x - 2y. Problem 1 . 6x - 4y - z a) What number is the coefficient of x ? 6 b) What number is the coefficient of y ? -4 Problem 2. What number is the coefficient of x? Problem 3. How do we add like terms? Add their coefficients; make that sum the coefficient of the common letter or letters. Problem 4. Add like terms. i) -3x - 4 + 2x + 6 = -x + 2 j) x - 2 - 4x - 5 = -3x - 7 k) 4x + y - 2x + y = 2x + 2y l) 3x - y - 8x + 2y = -5x + y m) 4x - 5x + x = 0 Problem 5. Add like terms. a) 2a + 3b These are not like terms. The literals are different. b) 2a + 3b + 4a - 5ab = 6a + 3b - 5ab. Problem 6. Remove parentheses and add like terms. Problem 7. 5abc + 2cba. Are these like terms? Yes. The order of factors does not matter. Problem 8. Add like terms. c) 9xyz + 3yzx + 5zxy = 17xyz d) 3xy - 4xyz + 3x - 8yx + 5yzx - 9x = -5xy + xyz - 6x Problem 9. Add like terms. a) 2n + 2 - n = n + 2 b) n - 2 - 3n + 1 = -2n - 1 c) 2n + 4 - 2n - 2 = 2 The rule for subtraction "Subtract a from b." Is that a - b or b - a ? It is b - a. a is the number being subtracted. It is called the subtrahend. The subtrahend appears to the right of the minus sign -- before the word "from." Example. Subtract 2x - 3 from 5x - 4 Solution. 2x - 3 is the subtrahend. Notice: The signs of the subtrahend change. 2x - 3 changes to -2x + 3. We can therefore state the following rule for subtraction. Change the signs of all the terms in the subtrahend. Problem 10. Subtract 4a - 2b from a + 3b. Change the signs of the subtrahend, and add: a + 3b - 4a + 2b = -3a + 5b. Problem 11. Subtract x - 5x + 7 from 3x - 8x - 2. Please make a donation to keep TheMathPage online. Copyright 2013 Lawrence Spector Questions or comments? Numbers written in scientific notation are the products of a digit term and an exponential term and are written in the general form a x 10^n. For example, 0.0000234 is written 2.34 x 10^n and 456,000 is written as 4.56 x 10^5. So we're going to talk about Scientific notation. Scientific notation is basically a way to take very big numbers or very small numbers and simplify them in a way that s easier to write and keep track of. So let's look at how we do that trying to determine proper Scientific notation we need to write a number a times 10 to the b. Now a is going to be a number between 1 and 10, to get that number between 1 and 10, we're going to usually have to move our decimal point to the right or to the left. If we move the decimal point to the right, that s going to be a negative exponent. If we move it to the left it's going to be a positive exponent. Okay let's look at how we do that with some problems. Over here I've got a number 1,100 not a terribly big number not a lot of zeros but to put it in proper Scientific notation we need to move the decimal point, the decimal point is right here, we need to move 1, 2, 3 spots now we have 1.1 times 10 to the third. Sorry 1.1 times 10 to the third okay that was pretty straight forward. Now we've got a very small number with a lot of zeros to the right of the decimal point. So we need to make this number larger by multiplying it by a negative exponent. So if we start with a decimal point we go 1, 2, 3, 4 units to get 5.4 times 10 in this case to the negative 4 since we're moving to the right of a decimal point. Okay now let's look at a really big number with a lot of zeros and again who wants to write all those zeros? I don't want to write them so let's simplify that okay, we're going to go 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 units to the left of the decimal point. And so we're going to take 7.12 times 10 to the ninth. And we've simplified this very big number with a lot of zeros into a number that s much more manageable. Okay and that s how we do Scientific notation. What do you think he turned into? Title: How to Achieve Effortless, Messy Bedhead Curls ===================================================== Introduction ------------ Messy bedhead curls are a popular hairstyle that gives off an effortlessly chic vibe. This look is perfect for casual outings or dressing up an outfit for a night out. The beauty of this style lies in its imperfection - there's no need for perfectly uniform curls! With just a few simple steps and some basic hair tools, you can create your own messy bedhead curls at home. The strikingly beautiful Haleakala silversword has always aroused the curiosity of human visitors to Haleakala Volcano. In pre-park days, plants were often removed by travelers to Haleakala Volcano as proof that the party had reached the summit, a practice that eventually seriously affected the silversword population. Browsing by feral goats and domestic cattle was also a significant factor in the silversword decline, but it was not a species preferred by these animals. By the 1920s, silversword numbers were so depleted that the Maui Chamber of Commerce sent a petition to Washington, DC, requesting that a serious effort be made to save the species (Loope and Crivellone 1986). let's see if you can open it. "Maybe we can take some rocks and make a necklace!" They climbed up the hill, but it was very hot and hard. They heard a loud noise and saw fire and lava coming out of the volcano. They were scared and tried to run away, but it was too late. "Help, Lila, help!" Tom shouted. "The volcano is angry and wants to hurt us!" "I'm sorry, Tom, I'm sorry!" Lila cried. "We should not have come here, we should have listened to our moms!" But no one heard them or came to help them. The fire and lava caught them and burned them. They were not fine. They were gone. Title: How to Care for Grown Ducks Introduction: Ducks are delightful additions to any backyard farm or pet collection. They require specific care and attention to ensure they remain healthy and happy. This guide will focus on providing comprehensive information about caring for grown ducks (ducks that are no longer ducklings). Follow these steps closely to give your feathered friends an enriching life! Step 1: Provide Adequate Housing Grown ducks need secure housing at night to protect them from predators such as foxes, raccoons, and coyotes. The coop should be well-ventilated but draft-free with dry bedding like straw or wood shavings. It's crucial that the floor is waterproof because ducks produce a lot of wet droppings. Key Tips: * The coop must have enough space; allow around 4 square feet per duck indoor and 10-25 square feet outdoor. * Elevate the coop off the ground if possible to avoid dampness and reduce parasite issues. * Clean the coop regularly to prevent disease buildup. Step 2: Create a Suitable Outdoor Environment Ducks love swimming and foraging, so providing access to a pond or pool is ideal. If this isn't feasible, create a shallow digging pit filled with water or buy a children's plastic pool. Make sure there's ample room for exercise and exploration. Key Guidelines: * Fill the water source daily to keep it clean. * Change the water frequently to prevent mosquito larvae growth. * Supervise their time outside to guard against predator attacks. Step 3: Feed Your Ducks Properly A balanced diet is essential for optimal health. Commercially available waterfowl feed typically contains grains, proteins, vitamins, and minerals necessary for adult ducks. Supplement their diet with fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. Avoid bread, avocado, chocolate, raw beans, and uncooked rice which can harm ducks. Key Facts: * Adult ducks generally eat 4-6 oz of food per day. * Limit treats to less than 10% of their total intake. * Always provide fresh drinking water. Step 4: Encourage Natural Foraging Behavior While commercial feed provides essential nutrients, letting ducks forage adds variety and mental stimulation. Scatter chopped veggies, fruit scraps, or duck pellets around the yard. Remember, uneaten food attracts vermin, so remove leftovers after mealtime. Step 5: Monitor Health Regularly Check your ducks daily for signs of illness, injury, or stress. Symptoms include lethargy, loss of appetite, unusual breathing patterns, and abnormal droppings. Consult a vet if you notice anything concerning. Prevention strategies include regular deworming and vaccination schedules according to local regulations. Step 6: Handle Your Ducks Correctly Handling ducks gently reduces stress and strengthens bonds between humans and birds. Use both hands to support their body weight evenly when picking up a duck. Hold them close to your chest to make them feel safe and secure. Never grab a duck by its neck or wings. Conclusion: By following these steps, you'll ensure your grown ducks live a comfortable, engaging lifestyle while minimizing potential health risks. Happy duck keeping! oh, look this is just like what your Lola got you. oh, look here's a pan there's a big pan you can use. Title: Understanding Matrices through Patterns and Symmetry Grade school students often enjoy playing with pattern blocks or shape sorters because they love finding different ways to fit shapes together. In this activity, we will learn about something similar called "matrices," which are arrays of numbers that can represent various things like patterns, pictures, or even symmetries! We won't dive into complicated math concepts but instead explore some basic ideas using everyday examples. Imagine having four special boxes labeled 0, 1, 2, and 3. Now let's think of each box as holding unique magical symbols ($\gamma^0$, $\gamma^1$, $\gamma^2$, and $\gamma^3$) that follow specific rules when combined. These symbols have interesting properties - one property being that swapping any pair of them twice brings us back to our original arrangement. This concept is known as symmetry. Now, imagine another set of these magical symbols ($\gamma^{'*0}$, $\gamma^{'*1}$, $\gamma^{'*2}$, and $\gamma^{'*3}$) following identical rules. Interestingly, both sets share the same kind of symmetry due to a fascinating relationship between them. It turns out that there exists a single guiding rule (or a magic wand!) that transforms all elements of one set into those of another by rearranging the positions of the symbols while preserving their magical properties. Mathematically speaking, this transformation can be represented as a product of multiplying and combining the symbols in a certain way using a "unitary matrix" ($U$). A unitary matrix helps maintain the balance of these symbols' properties just like how a scale keeps weights balanced on its pans. The symbol $U$ itself has a neat quality - when squared and multiplied element-wise with its own conjugate transpose ($U*U^T$), it results in a multiple of the identity matrix ($cI$) which signifies equal contributions across every dimension. Next up, consider taking photographs of our magical friends. Flipping the photograph horizontally should still give us recognizable images since nothing drastic changes except directionality. Similarly, mathematically, flipping the superscript (*) of the magical symbols corresponds to a new set of relationships among themselves, governed by yet another unitary matrix ($B$). This matrix holds true under several conditions involving the interplay between primes, unprimed symbols, and their respective counterparts. Though quite abstract, understanding matrices provides valuable insights into connections and patterns present around us. By exploring matrices and their properties, young minds develop analytical thinking skills essential for solving intricate problems in life and beyond! Here are 5 activities you can do indoors and out-even if you don't get snow. They'll help your child learn about colors, numbers, science, nature and more. With your child: Listen to the sounds of winter. Take a walk and talk about what you hear. Perhaps boots crunching on snow. Rain splashing. Wind whistling. Make a paper snowman. Cut the shape of a snowman from construction paper. Glue on cotton for the body. Add buttons for the coat. Fabric for the scarf. Yarn for arms and legs. Beans for eyes. Count pairs. Find five pairs of mittens or gloves in your house. Mix them up. Have your child sort them back into pairs. Be winter detectives. Help your child identify the signs of winter. Cold weather. Less daylight. Bare trees. People wearing more clothing on. Smoke from chimneys. Make a "No Snow Man." For each one, collect a set of children's clothes. Stuff pants and shirt with wadded newspaper. Use safety pins to hold the two together. Make a head from a stuffed paper bag. Use markers to add facial features. Add a hat and scarf, and put outside. It is sometimes called code, but this is not really a correct name. It is the science used to try to keep information secret and safe. Modern cryptography is a mix of mathematics, computer science, and electrical engineering. Cryptography is used in ATM (bank) cards, computer passwords, and shopping on the internet. Students read two online stories that introduce them to the elements of a budget and show that a successful budget balances money coming in (income) with money going out (expenses and savings). Follow-up activities point out the value of a budget. Students learn that a budget can help us make sure we buy the things we really need and want. A budget can also help us save for things that we can't afford to buy right now. In a culminating activity, students create a budget they could use to achieve a savings goal. - Identify the components of a budget. - Design a budget for achieving a savings goal. - Understand that saving for the future requires giving up something today. Write the word "budget" on the board. Ask students to tell you what they think a budget is. Perhaps they have heard their parents or the principal say that something is not in the budget. If they have no idea, suggest that they look the word up in the dictionary. (budget, n.: a plan adjusting expenses to income). Summarize by saying a budget is a plan that shows how much money comes in (income) and how much money goes out (expenses and savings). Tim's budget is available online as well. Tim's Online Budget - Heather Learns About Earning: A story about a girl who needs money for a birthday party. What is Tim's problem? [He spends too much and can't to save.] 2. What is the first thing Money Mouse advises Tim to do? [Make a list of everything he buys so he knows where his money goes.] 3. How does spending money today affect what Tim spends tomorrow? [He has less money to spend tomorrow.] 4. What did Money Mouse do to save money for the new Monster Mouse video game? [He spent less on cheese popcorn and movies.] 5. What were Tim's biggest expenses? [$7.50 for school lunches.] 6. What is the total amount Tim spent on movies and video games? Show your math. [$3.00 playing video games + $3.00 renting video games + 3.00 movie rental + $5.00 movie and popcorn at theater = $14.00.] 7. What other things did Tim have on his list? Name the items and tell how much he spent. [$2.50 for snacks and $2.00 for baseball cards.] 8. What three things did Money Mouse do to spend less and save more? [He took cheese sandwiches for lunch, rented only one Monster Mouse movie each week, and made his own cheese popcorn.] 9. What did Tim do to spend less and save more? [NOTE: Tim's budget is available online ] Below are further questions that can be used for review: - Sometimes we can reduce spending without giving up something entirely. [He made it at home instead of buying it at the movie theater]. - How did Tim reduce his spending without giving up playing video games? [He played video games at the homes of friends]. - What else might Tim do to cut expenses without giving up something entirely? [Tim may not always have a specific video game when he wants to play it. The friends have to depend on each other to buy the videos they want to play most.] - What is the cost of renting a video versus going to the movie theater? [Tim has to wait until the movie is available on video.] - What might be the cost of Tim carrying his lunch versus buying it? Reducing spending should start with reducing spending on wants versus needs]. [NOTE: If your students are not familiar with the concepts of economic needs and wants, explain that economic needs are things we must have to survive such as food, water and shelter. Wants are things that are nice to have--but not necessary for survival. Movie tickets, bubble gum, and jewelry are examples of wants.] Conclude the activity by pointing out that people can reduce their spending in many ways. Their spending plans will be influenced by their needs, wants, and personal preferences. Have the students read the story Heather Learns About Earning to find out how Heather increased the money coming in (income). Then discuss the THINK ABOUT IT questions: 1. What was Heather's problem? [She needed more money for a party.] 2. How did she earn the money she needed? [She baked and sold a cake.] 3. What else might Heather have done to earn the money she needed? [Potential answers include providing a service such as raking leaves for a neighbor, or selling something that she has made for friends, or doing extra chores at home.] [NOTE: In this story, only money that is earned is labeled "income". For budget purposes, an allowance is also considered income. While some students earn their allowances, others receive an allowance as a gift. Explain to students that an allowance is an amount they can count on and as such, can be included as income. It is difficult to include other gifts in a budget because the size and timing of gifts cannot be counted on. One way to deal with sporadic gifts is to determine in advance how gift money will be divided between saving and spending. For example, a child may decide to save half of any gift money and spend the rest. Some parents and guardians insist that their children follow such a plan.] Have students use the sample budget to answer the questions in the budget activity. Title: How to Be the First to Arrive at a Party or Event Introduction: Arriving early to a party or event can have many benefits. It allows you to be one of the first people to greet the host, helping them with any last-minute preparations. You'll also have ample time to mingle, claim your favorite spot, and get comfortable before the crowd starts pouring in. This guide will provide detailed steps on how to ensure punctuality and make a great impression as an early bird. Step 1: RSVP Promptly As soon as you receive the invitation, respond accordingly. This gives the host an accurate headcount and shows respect for their planning efforts. Additionally, noting the RSVP deadline helps you prioritize this event in your schedule. * Key Tip: If possible, include any dietary restrictions or accessibility needs when responding to the invite. Step 2: Study the Invitation Details Carefully review all relevant information provided by the host, such as date, start time, location, dress code, and parking details. Make note of these particulars to avoid confusion closer to the event. * Guideline: Double-check transportation methods if needed (e.g., public transit schedules, designated drivers). Step 3: Plan Your Outfit Ahead of Time Selecting what to wear ahead of time reduces day-of stress and decision fatigue. Try on your outfit to check for fit and cleanliness, making adjustments or alterations if necessary. * Key Tip: Consider layering clothing items to accommodate potential changes in temperature throughout the evening. Step 4: Set Reminders Input the event into your digital calendar or set reminders on your phone to prompt you about the upcoming occasion. This proactive measure ensures that other commitments don't interfere with your plans. * Guideline: Give yourself extra buffer time for unforeseen circumstances like traffic jams or wardrobe malfunctions. Step 5: Leave Early To arrive early, leave earlier than usual. Factor in additional travel time due to factors beyond your control, such as roadwork or heavy traffic. * Key Tip: Use GPS navigation apps to find optimal routes and real-time updates on traffic conditions. Step 6: Bring a Hostess Gift Show gratitude towards the host by bringing a small gift, such as flowers, wine, chocolates, or a scented candle. Presenting this upon arrival adds a thoughtful touch to your early appearance. * Guideline: When gifting perishable items, confirm whether the host has refrigerator space available. Step 7: Offer Assistance Upon Arrival Greet the host warmly and offer assistance with any remaining tasks. They may appreciate help setting up food stations, arranging seating, or welcoming guests. * Key Tip: Keep conversations brief during setup; allow the host to focus on finishing touches without distraction. Step 8: Network During the Initial Phase Take advantage of the quieter atmosphere to introduce yourself to other attendees. Engaging in meaningful conversations early on fosters stronger connections throughout the night. * Guideline: Remember names and basic details shared by others to maintain fluid conversation flow. Conclusion: By following these steps and being mindful of key tips and guidelines, you are well on your way to mastering the art of arriving early to parties and events. Not only does this demonstrate respect for the host, but it also provides opportunities for quality social interactions and increased visibility among fellow attendees. He felt a knot around his leg. He could not move. He was scared. Lily counted to ten and opened her eyes. She looked for Ben. She did not see him. She called his name. "Ben, where are you? I'm coming to find you!" She walked around the park. She saw the big tree. She heard a whisper. "Lily, help me!" She went to the tree and saw Ben. He was hanging from the rope. He looked sad. She ran to him. "Ben, what happened? Are you okay?" She asked. Ben told her what happened. He said he was sorry. He said he was silly. He asked her to help him. Lily saw the knot. She tried to untie it, but it was hard. She pulled and twisted the rope, but it did not work. She said, "Don't worry, Ben. I will help you. I will get someone to help us." She looked around. She saw a man walking his dog. She waved and shouted. "Mister, mister, please help us! My friend is stuck in the rope!" The man heard her and came to the tree. He saw Ben. He said, "Oh dear, how did this happen? Don't worry, I will get you down." He climbed the tree and untied the knot. He lifted Ben and brought him down. He checked his leg. He said, "You are lucky. Tokelau is among the most remote areas in the Pacific: There are no airports; the islands can only be reached by ship: there are also no sea ports, which makes travelling difficult, even between the atolls. New Zealand pays for a ship, which connects Tokelau with the next bigger seaport (Apia, on Samoa). The trip from Apia to Fakaofo takes about a day, reaching the northernmost atoll Atafu will take another three to four hours. There is no large pier on any of the islands, which means that smaller boats (called barges) are needed to reach the shore. The ship reaches Tokelau about once every two weeks. Ew not in the mouth not in the mouth! By law of inertia, when no force is applied to an object, the object holds its velocity, which is speed "and" direction. An object either in "uniform motion", which is constant speed in an unchanging direction, or staying at rest, which is zero velocity, experiences inertia. This exhibits Galilean invariance-its mechanical interactions proceeding without variation-also called Galilean relativity since one cannot perceive whether one is at rest or in uniform motion. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Jane who had a kitten. Jane loved her kitten more than anything else. One day she wanted to take her kitten outside, but her mom told her it was a foolish thing to do and that she should stay away from the ground. Jane wasn't happy about this but she surrendered to her mother's wishes. She stayed away from the ground and made sure her kitten stayed safe too. That night, Jane had trouble sleeping. She really wanted to explore the ground with her kitten. So, she decided to sneak outside and take her kitten with her. She got to the ground and was amazed to see what was there! She saw lots of bugs, leaves and animals that she'd never seen before. Then, all of a sudden, she heard her mom calling from the house. Jane reluctantly said: "I surrender!" Copernicus and Galileo, for example, thought so. But when people began to study the motions of planets carefully, they saw that the planets were not moving in perfect circles. Title: How to Cope with Being Alone at Night Introduction: Being alone at night can be difficult for some people due to various reasons such as fear, anxiety, or loneliness. However, there are several ways to cope with this situation and make your nights more peaceful and enjoyable. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to deal with being alone at night effectively. Step 1: Create a Safe Environment Before anything else, ensure that your living space is safe and secure. Lock all doors and windows, check security systems if any, and keep emergency numbers handy. A sense of safety contributes significantly to feeling comfortable when spending time alone during the night. Key Tip: Install motion sensor lights outside your house to deter potential intruders and illuminate dark areas around your property. Step 2: Establish a Routine Having a regular routine before bedtime can help alleviate feelings of unease or apprehension about being alone at night. This could include activities like reading, meditating, listening to music, or doing household chores. Consistency provides a familiar structure which makes it easier to relax and fall asleep. Guideline: Try to maintain the same bedtime every day, even on weekends. Irregular sleep patterns disrupt your body's internal clock leading to restless nights. Step 3: Limit Exposure to Stimulating Activities Before Bedtime Refrain from watching TV shows, playing video games, or engaging in other stimulating activities an hour prior to sleeping. These tasks increase brain activity making it harder for you to unwind and prepare for sleep. Key Tip: Opt for calming practices instead, such as yoga, deep breathing exercises, or progressive muscle relaxation techniques. Step 4: Control Lighting Bright lighting might interfere with your ability to sleep soundly. Dim the lights half an hour before going to bed. Use blackout curtains if necessary to block out external light sources. Guideline: Consider using soft-glowing lamps rather than harsh overhead lights. The former creates a soothing atmosphere conducive to relaxation. Step 5: Listen to Soothing Sounds Listening to white noise, nature sounds, or gentle music can mask disturbing noises and promote better sleep. There are numerous apps and devices available that offer these features. Key Tip: Experiment with different types of sounds until you find what works best for you. Some people prefer rainfall sounds while others may like ocean waves or rustling leaves. Step 6: Practice Mindfulness Meditation Mindfulness involves focusing on the present moment without judgment. It reduces stress, enhances mental wellbeing, and promotes better sleep quality. You can practice mindfulness through guided meditation sessions available online or via smartphone applications. Guideline: Start with short five-minute sessions daily and gradually increase the duration as you get more comfortable with the technique. Step 7: Engage in Relaxation Techniques Implementing relaxation strategies can ease tension and facilitate falling asleep faster. For instance, visualize a serene place, perform diaphragmatic breathing, or stretch tight muscles gently. Key Tip: Make sure to adopt a position that feels natural and comfortable for relaxing. Avoid forcing yourself into positions that cause discomfort. Conclusion: Remember, coping with being alone at night takes time and patience. Gradually incorporating these steps into your lifestyle should lead to noticeable improvements over time. If despite trying these methods, you continue experiencing distress, consider seeking professional advice from therapists specializing in treating anxiety or insomnia. The first reference to the game of Go occurs in Chinese records from around 2,400 [two thousand four hundred] years ago. Originally the game Go was used by political leaders to develop skill in strategy and mental skill. Knowing how to play Go was required by a Chinese gentleman, along with the skills of artistic writing or calligraphy, painting and the ability to play a musical instrument. These were regarded as the four most important skills. In ancient China, a gentleman had to pass a test in these four key skills in order to get a good paying job in the government. The Chinese brought Go to Korea, and it entered Japan around 1,500 [one thousand five hundred] years ago, and it has been popular ever since. Don't you play in your diaper an' your shirt? [moves wand slowly closer to Chi's construction] Lunchtime with your preschooler is an ideal time to serve up some learning along with their meal. Fun games and activities will help the child learn in a casual, no-pressure atmosphere. Start with the basics: counting, singing the alphabet, and identifying colors - then add more fun with easy games that will sharpen their skills! - Serve a whole sandwich, then cut it in half while explaning to the child that two halves equal one whole. Cut the halves again. Show the child that there are now four pieces that make a whole, so each piece is 1/4. You can demonstrate this easy concept every time you slice an apple or other food - and your preschooler is learning about fractions! - Practice counting. How many forks do you see? Have the child count the number of green items in the room - it will be exciting for them to discover colors in unexpected places, like the green in mom's salad! - Put a small amount of two different types of dry pasta or cereal on the table and have them sort it by type - then have them count the number of pieces of each. They'll learn sizes and shapes, have fun counting and be improving their small motor skills along with practicing counting. Ready for higher numbers to count? Add more pasta! - Give them a deck of cards (minus face cards) and ask them to find all of the hearts. Next, let them sort by black and red cards, and help them learn to recognize the numbers. Ask them to find the red five, and show them how easy it is to count the number of hearts on the card to identify the correct one. Write the number down for them, and let them copy it. Letters and spelling - How many things on the table start with the letter P? Plates, pepper, potato, pasta! Sound out the letter to help them along. It's fun to choose a letter, write it down and tape it to your shirt. Now let them write their letter on several post-its and after lunch they can tag any items in the room that start with that letter. It's especially fun if you start them off with the first letter of their own name. Most kids won't tag themselves without a little hint! When they do, show them how to write their name. And show them how to spell "mom" and ask them to write it. Preschoolers love finding out that "mom" and "dad" are the same forward and backwards! - Play Bingo. They'll learn to recognize letters and numbers while marking their card with pieces of cereal, nuts, or gumdrops. Printable Bingo cards are available online. 5 Math games for kids 5 Games to play at the dinner table 3 Video games to play with your preschooler Important Esperanto writers are for example: Trevor Steele (Australia), Istv n Nemere (Hungary) and Mao Zifu (China). Sight word games should be geared to self-improvement rather than deciding a winner. Words can be chosen from papers your child brings home, word lists provided by the teacher or favorite books. Place these words on index cards and develop a file of sight vocabulary to use and review. For variety, try a game using words from "Make Way for Ducklings." Draw fish shapes on cardboard or index cards. Cut them out and write a word from the list on each fish. Attach a paper clip to the mouth of each fish. Tie a small magnet to the end of a string and tie the other end to a stick that can be used to "fish" words out of a bowl. Your child must say the word correctly to keep it. If incorrect, tell her the word and let her throw it back in and fish again. Count how many words she can "catch." What are those [Chi is holding zebras]. Bauxite is the most important aluminium ore. It is largely made up of the minerals Gibbsite Al(OH), Boehmite -AlO(OH), and Diaspore -AlO(OH), together with the iron oxides Goethite and Hematite, the clay mineral Kaolinite and small amounts of Anatase TiO. It was named after the village Les Baux-de-Provence in southern France, where it was first discovered in 1821 by geologist Pierre Berthier. Today the four biggest producers are Australia, Brazil, China and India. Okay let's get one piece of paper first. Some studies have looked at the health risks of coffee. In February 2003, there was a study in Denmark of 18,478 women to find out if coffee had an effect on pregnancy and birth. It was found that if a woman drank between four and seven cups of coffee a day, it did not seem to make a big change to the number of babies that were born dead, (stillborn). But the women in the study who drank eight or more cups of coffee a day had three times as much chance of having a stillborn baby. Do you want to look at something else besides... Reflection, practice and evaluation - Why do you ask questions in your classroom? - What kinds of questions do you ask? - In your classroom, who is doing most of the talking? Who is doing most of the thinking? Is this how things should be? - What use do you make of student responses to questions that you or students ask in your classroom? - Consider the range of cultures present in your classroom. Are there cultural considerations that you need to take into account when questioning your students or encouraging them to ask questions? - Thinking about gender issues: in your classroom which gender is more likely to be asked questions? What kinds of questions are asked of boys? What kinds of questions are asked of girls? Ask a colleague to use the audit sheet in order to identify what questioning practices occur in your classroom. Use the results to answer the following questions: - What kind of questions do you ask most frequently - open or closed? - Do you ask questions that demand higher-order thinking? - Are you employing wait time and hands down when appropriate? - Are you prompting students to further responses? - Do you ask students to build on each other's answers? - Are you distributing questions around the class (and equally between the genders, if relevant)? - Are you acknowledging student responses in a positive manner? - Do students ask questions? What kinds? Do you think there is a need to change your questioning practices? What might you focus on and why? Well to put the ponytail we height brush your hair. [CHI holds out waffle block to MOT] Disabling Touch Accommodations on an iPhone =========================================== Touch accommodations are accessibility features designed to help individuals with motor impairments or other disabilities interact more easily with their iPhones. However, if you find that these settings are causing issues or interfering with your normal usage, you can disable them. This tutorial will guide you through the process of turning off touch accommodations on your iPhone. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Make sure you have the latest version of iOS installed before proceeding. * Be aware that this process may require several steps depending on which specific touch accommodation features you have enabled. 1. **Access Settings** * Begin by unlocking your iPhone and locating the 'Settings' app on your home screen. Tap on the icon to open it. 2. **Navigate to Accessibility** * Within the 'Settings' menu, scroll down until you see the 'Accessibility' option. It should be located near the bottom of the page under the 'General' category. Tapping on 'Accessibility' will take you to a new menu where you can adjust various settings related to your device's accessibility features. 3. **Select Touch** * From the 'Accessibility' menu, look for the 'Physical & Motor' section and tap on 'Touch'. Here, you'll find options related to how your iPhone responds to taps and gestures. 4. **Toggle Off Required Features** * The first setting you'll encounter is 'Touch Accommodations'. If this feature is currently turned on (green switch), toggle it off (gray switch) to disable all associated touch accommodations at once. * If you prefer to customize individual components of touch accommodations, continue reading: 5. **Adjust Hold Duration** * Scroll down to 'Hold Duration', which determines how long you must hold down on an item before it registers as a press-and-hold gesture instead of a single tap. By default, this value is set to 0.5 seconds. You can increase or decrease this duration based on your preference. A longer duration might prevent accidental activations while a shorter one could speed up common actions like copying text. 6. **Configure Swipe Gestures** * Under 'Swipe Gestures', you can choose whether swiping left, right, up, or down performs a specific action, such as going back to the previous page or opening Notification Center. If you don't need these functions, consider turning them off here. 7. **Manage Tap Assistance** * Further down, you'll find 'Tap Assistance'. Enable this feature if you want additional guidance when performing complex multi-finger gestures like pinching to zoom. When activated, a visual overlay appears on the screen to assist with proper finger placement. 8. **Customize Additional Options** * Depending on your needs, there might be other settings within the 'Touch' menu worth exploring, such as '3D Touch' or 'Voice Control'. Adjust any relevant options according to your preferences. 9. **Exit Accessibility Menu** * Once you've made all necessary changes, return to the main 'Settings' screen by pressing the 'Back' button in the top left corner of the display. By following these steps, you should now have successfully disabled unwanted touch accommodations on your iPhone. Remember that these modifications can greatly improve usability, so feel free to revisit the 'Accessibility' menu whenever needed to fine-tune your experience further. why do you want to chew on that? Once upon a time, there lived a brave little bunny named George. Every day George would run around his favourite meadow, hopping in and out of bushes. One day while George was out exploring, he came across a patch of mint. As George neared the mint, he spotted a surprise in the middle of it - a pot of gold coins! He could hardly believe it. George had never seen anything so wonderful. He carefully stepped closer, when all of a sudden he heard a loud growling sound. To his surprise, a big, angry bear was standing in front of the pot. The bear roared, "This pot of gold is mine! You must leave now!" George was scared, but he felt brave and he was determined to keep the pot of gold. So he stood his ground, torn between fear and bravery. Eventually, George stood up tall and found his courage. He walked right up to the bear and said, "This mint patch is big enough for both of us. We can share the pot of gold and I'll take these coins - but this pot will always be ours." The bear was impressed by George's bravery and agreed to share. They shared the mint patch and the pot of gold, becoming friends. Moral of the story: Sharing is better than taking all for yourself. With a brave and kind heart, we can all find peace and harmony. [low volume makes this difficult to transcribe] Volume 3, Number 12, November 30, 1998 Good or Well? It's not that complicated. Good is an adjective. Well is an adverb. If you are modifying, or describing, a noun or pronoun, use good. Example: You are a good writer. If you are modifying a verb, use well. Example: You write well. Write good or well in the space. Write the modified word after the sentence. The first four sentences have been done for you. 1. How well do you know him? know 2. Sam did a good job on her assignment. job 3. Sam did well on her assignment. did 4. Driving with your eyes open is a good idea. idea 5. The idea was expressed __________ in the article. 6. I'll feel better if I get a __________ night's sleep. 7. Your snoring made it impossible for me to sleep __________. 8. I really believe that tuna fish is a __________ breakfast food. 9. I'm still afraid of your __________ trained grizzly bear. 10. How __________ do you run on a hot day? 11. Dig deep to have a __________ well. 12. Have you been a __________ or a bad example to your younger friends? 13. You think that Desmond has behaved badly, but I think he has behaved __________. 14. I hope you have a __________ excuse! 15. Please chew your food __________. 16. The doctor took a __________ deserved vacation. 17. Why would you quit when you're doing so __________. 18. Melody is a __________ singing partner. 19. You get to stay or does this pasta stay put... = = = children/CHIDES_NA/HSLLD/HV2/MT/brnmt2.cha = = = Do You Like to Spend or Save? Main Subject Area: Mathematics Additional Subject Area Standard(s): Coins Used in Lesson: Grade Level(s): K-2 2. As a class, discuss coins and the different places people keep their money - wallets, coin purses, piggy banks, jars, banks, etc. 3. Give each student a paper square and crayons. Have them draw a piggy bank to represent their own money. 4. Ask the students, Do you like to spend or save? To answer the question, have each student come to the front of the room and glue his or her bank under Spend or Save on the graph. 5. As a class, analyze the results. Ask questions such as: How many students in our class like to save their money? How many like to spend their money? How many more people in our class like to spend their money instead of save it? (or vice versa.) What else can you tell about the students in our room from this graph? VIEW THIS PAGE Fog and mist are essentially the same thing - cloud banks made of suspended water droplets - so why do we have different words for them? The only difference is the density, but the distinction is highly subjective. In aeronautical terms, if a pilot cannot see further than 1km, it is defined as fog. For motorists, visibility must fall below 200m to be classed as fog, otherwise it is known as mist. Whatever you choose to call it, fog forms when air rapidly cools, causing the water vapor to condense. Fog often forms in coastal areas when warm air moves in from the sea. The temperature of the land is colder than the sea, which causes the air to cool. In today's experiment you will recreate this scenario to make your very own fog. What you will need: some hot water, two bottles and two packs of ice cubes. Step 1: Fill one bottle with hot water. Tap water will suffice - it does not need to be boiling. Let the bottle stand for ten minutes to heat it up. Step 2: Fill the other bottle with cold water and allow it to stand. Step 3: Empty 80 percent of the water out of both of the bottles, so that just a little bit remains at the bottom. Step 4: Turn both bottles on their side, then take the packs of ice cubes and press them on the bottles. You should notice fog appearing inside the bottle that contained hot water. Hold the ice cubes in place until the bottle is full of fog. No fog will appear in the cold water bottle no matter how long you hold the ice. 1. fog n. 2. mist n. 3. droplet n. (xiao3 di1) 4. subjective adj. (zhu3 guan1 de5) 5. coastal adj. (yan2 hai3 de5) 6. appear v.i./v.t. (xian3 lu4) WHAT HAPPENED? This simple experiment recreated the conditions that occur naturally to form fog. The air inside the hot water bottle cools quickly causing condensation. The air in the other bottle is already cold, so condensation doesn't form. (JOHN PHILLIPS, STAFF WRITER) VIEW THIS PAGE Prob'LY he did, but he went into the girl's bathroom. In mammals, testicles can be very big or very small in relation to the size of the animal itself. The size has to do with the amount of sperm that the male animal needs to make. Some types of male animal generally only have one sexual partner at a time and are called monogamous. Some types of animals generally live in a herd or flock where one male will have many female sexual partners. These males are called polygamous. More sperm is needed by "polygamous" males than by "monogamous" males. "Polygamous" males generally have larger testicles than "monogamous" males. The testicles grow larger to make more sperm. Hide Your MySpace URL: A Comprehensive Guide MySpace used to be one of the most popular social networking sites, but even today, many people still maintain their profiles there. If you're among them and want to keep your profile more private or simply tidy up your online presence, hiding your MySpace URL can help. This guide will walk you through all necessary steps with clear explanations, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines along the way. Let's get started! Step 1: Log into Your MySpace Account ------------------------------------- To begin, visit <https://myspace.com/> and enter your login credentials (email address and password) associated with your account. After logging in successfully, proceed to the next step. Key Tip: Make sure that you remember or have saved your login information as you might need it later during this process. Step 2: Access Privacy Settings ------------------------------ Once logged in, look at the top right corner of the page where your profile picture is displayed. Click on the gear icon beside it to open a drop-down menu. From this list, select "Settings" which takes you to your account settings page. Guideline: Keeping track of various privacy options across different platforms is essential for maintaining control over what others see about us online. Step 3: Navigate toPrivacy Tab ----------------------------- On the left side of the settings page, click on the tab labeled "Privacy." It contains several sections related to controlling who sees what on your MySpace profile. Step 4: Edit Profile Visibility ------------------------------- Under the "Profile Viewability" section, find the option titled "Make my profile visible to:" Here, change the setting from "Everyone" to either "Friends Only" or "Only Me," depending upon whether you wish only confirmed friends or nobody else but yourself to view your profile. Key Tip: Choosing "Only Me" effectively hides your MySpace URL since no one except you can access your profile directly using its unique web address. Step 5: Save Changes -------------------- After making these changes, scroll down to the bottom of the Privacy page and click the blue button saying "Apply Changes." This action saves your new visibility preferences and applies them immediately. Step 6: Test New Setting ------------------------ Finally, test out if your MySpace URL is now hidden by trying to access it via a private browsing window or asking someone else to attempt viewing your profile while they are logged out of MySpace. If everything worked correctly, your profile should not be accessible anymore unless the person attempting to view it is logged into an account that is listed as a friend on your MySpace profile. Additional Tips & Guidelines: * Regularly review your privacy settings on any social media platform, including MySpace, to ensure they align with your current comfort level regarding online sharing. * Be cautious when accepting friendship requests; confirm that you know the individual personally before granting them access to your content. * Remember that completely hiding your MySpace URL may prevent potential connections from finding and connecting with you. Weigh the pros and cons carefully based on your personal needs and goals. Well I can't tell you until you're all done. Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Amy. Amy loved to design things. Title: How to Get the Wedding Guest Book Signed By Everyone Introduction: A wedding guest book is a wonderful keepsake that allows you to remember everyone who attended your special day. However, ensuring that every guest signs the book can be quite a challenge. This detailed guide will walk you through the process, providing clear steps, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines to ensure you get as many signatures as possible. Step 1: Choose the Right Type of Guest Book Selecting an appropriate guest book plays a significant role in encouraging guests to sign it. Opt for something visually appealing and engaging, such as: - A beautifully bound hardcover book with high-quality paper - An interactive scrapbook filled with prompts or questions for guests - A unique alternative like a jigsaw puzzle or thumbprint tree Key Tip: Make sure your chosen guest book has enough pages to accommodate all expected attendees while still leaving room for additional messages from unexpected guests. Step 2: Strategically Place Your Guest Book Where you place the guest book significantly impacts its usage. Set up the guest book at one of these locations during critical moments throughout the event: - At the entrance of the ceremony venue as people arrive - Near the gift table during cocktail hour - Close to the dance floor where most action takes place - During dinner time when guests are seated and more likely to engage Guideline: Avoid placing the guest book in multiple spots simultaneously; this may confuse guests about where they should sign. Instead, move it periodically if necessary but only after most guests have had ample opportunity to find and sign it. Step 3: Assign Someone Responsible Appoint someone trustworthy (like a bridesmaid, groomsman, or family member) to monitor the guest book throughout the celebration. Their responsibilities include: - Ensuring the guest book remains open and visible - Encouraging late arrivals to sign - Offering pens or pencils to those who forget their own writing utensils - Explaining any specific requests or themes associated with the guest book Key Tip: Communicate clearly with your designated helper regarding expectations and timelines so they can fulfill their duties effectively. Step 4: Personalize Your Guest Book Add personal touches to make your guest book stand out and entice guests to interact with it. Some ideas include: - Including photographs of you and your partner throughout the pages - Adding fun facts, quotes, or memories related to your relationship - Providing decorative embellishments, stickers, or stamps for guests to incorporate into their messages Step 5: Announce Its Presence Make announcements throughout the night reminding guests about the presence of the guest book. For example, mention it during speeches, ask the DJ to remind listeners between songs, or request your officiant to bring attention to it before dismissing guests following the ceremony. Step 6: Follow Up After the Event If there are still blank pages post-wedding, consider sending out an email or text message asking unregistered guests to submit their well-wishes via digital means. Alternatively, invite close friends and family members over for dinner and provide them with access to the unsigned guest book then. Conclusion: Obtaining signatures from every attendee might seem daunting initially, but careful planning and execution can yield impressive results. Utilizing our comprehensive six-step approach guarantees maximum participation rates, transforming your wedding guest book into a cherished memento teeming with heartfelt sentiments and priceless memories. Every material is made up of atoms which are very small parts. Some atoms hold together quite well, which is what makes some solid materials hard. Something made of pure iron is softer than steel because the atoms can slip over one another. If other atoms like carbon are added, they are different from iron atoms and stop the iron atoms from sliding apart so easily. This makes the metal stronger and harder. [regarding GRA's walking toward the camera and controls] Imagine you have a big box full of toys and you want to organize them. You could put all the cars in one corner, dolls in another, blocks in another, and so on. This way, it would be easy for you to find a specific toy when you need it. In math, we call this kind of organization "linear operators." Now, let's say you have two friends, Alex and Jamie, who also have boxes full of toys. They both decide to organize their toys just like you did, by putting similar ones together. When Alex visits your house, he wants to take out some cars to play with, but instead of taking toys directly from your box, he asks Jamie to give him cars from Jamie's box. To make things easier, you ask Jamie to rearrange her toys in the same order as yours before giving cars to Alex. So now, whatever comes after cars in your arrangement should come after cars in Jamie's too - trucks, buses, etc. That way, Alex will get toys in the correct order without having to figure everything out himself. In mathematical language, you asked Jamie to arrange her toys the same way she would apply a function to numbers. Just as multiplying 3 by 4 gives 12, applying this function to car would result in trucks, then buses, and so on. Because Jamie arranged her toys similarly to yours, mathematicians call this "the adjoint of a linear operator." It means organizing information (or toys!) in a consistent manner. When you combine two functions or ways of organizing toys, sometimes it becomes confusing which friend should pass along the next item. But since Jamie knows exactly how you organized your toys because of the adjoint rule, she can easily hand off the proper items to Alex even if multiple friends played with your toys first! This concept works well in mathematics too - combining different ways of transforming information still makes sense due to the consistency provided by the adjoint rules. And although our example involved only two friends playing with toys, these ideas hold strong even when many people work together, allowing us to create complex systems while keeping track of each step using the power of linear algebra! [CHI is trying to put the cow in the barn] Even though air seems very light, the weight of all of the air above the outside of the Earth (air pressure) is important. Generally, from sea level to the top of the outer level of the air, a space of air one cm across has a mass of about 1.03 kg and a space of air one sq in across has a weight of about 14.7 lb. The mass of the air also keeps the Earth safe when rocks (meteorites) hit it from outer space. Without the air, the damage meteorites do would be much greater. Because of the air, meteorites generally burn up long before they get to the earth. Title: How to Beat the Second Secret Mission in Club Penguin Club Penguin is an online multiplayer game that offers various missions and activities for players to enjoy. The second secret mission, known as "The Case of the Missing Puffles," requires you to find five missing puffle buddies hidden around the island. Here's a comprehensive guide to help you complete this mission successfully: **Step 1:** Log into your Club Penguin account and waddle over to the HQ (Hidden Lake Headquarters), located behind the Ski Lodge. To access it, go to the upstairs room of the lodge, then click the orange door with the yellow keyhole. Enter your secret agent number and password if prompted. *Why this step matters:* This is where all the secret missions begin. By logging in and going to the HQ, you are initiating the start of the mission. **Step 2:** Talk to Gary the Gadget Guy when you arrive at the HQ. He will give you information about the missing puffles and ask you to find them. *Why this step matters:* Gary provides crucial details about the mission objectives, setting the stage for what needs to be done next. **Step 3:** Visit different locations on the map using the Map Screen (accessible via the lower-left corner icon) or by physically walking there from the HQ. You need to locate the following puffles: - Red Puffle: Found inside the Dance Club. Go up the stairs near the DJ booth and look for him hiding behind some speakers. - Blue Puffle: Located at the Plaza, specifically in the Pet Shop. It can be found playing with toys near the entrance. - Pink Puffle: Can be spotted outside the Stadium during sport events like Ice Fishing Tournament, Curling, or Snowboarding. If no event is happening, wait until one starts. - Black Puffle: Present at the Dock. Check the boxes near the Boiler Room hatch. - Green Puffle: Resides within the Cove, usually swimming underwater around the sunken shipwreck. Put on diving gear before searching. *Key Tips & Guidelines for locating puffles:* * Be patient while looking for these puffles since they tend to hide well. * Some puffles only appear during specific mini-games or special events. Keep checking back regularly if you don't see them initially. * When entering new rooms, take a moment to scan the entire area thoroughly. Sometimes puffles may blend into their surroundings making them hard to spot. **Step 4:** Once you have found each puffle, simply click on them to collect them. They will automatically return to the HQ once collected. *Why this step matters:* Collecting all five puffles advances the mission forward and brings you closer to completing it. **Step 5:** After collecting all five puffles, head back to the HQ and speak with Gary again. Report that you've found all the lost puffles. *Why this step matters:* Speaking with Gary confirms the completion of the mission and triggers any rewards or recognition associated with success. By diligently following these steps, you should be able to beat the second secret mission in Club Penguin - "The Case of the Missing Puffles." The only living representatives of primitive egg-laying mammals (monotremes) are the echidnas and the platypus. They are only found in Australasia, which includes Tasmania, New Guinea, and Kangaroo Island. These monotremes are totally absent in the rest of the world. Title: Understanding Big Sets in Groups Have you ever played hopscotch? It's a fun game where you toss a small object into one of the numbered squares on the ground and then hop through the squares, skipping the square with the object. Let's imagine a special kind of hopscotch court where instead of numbers, each square has a unique pair of friends' names written inside it. Your task is to jump onto the squares, always landing next to two friends who can hold hands to form a new friendship! Now, let's say your hopscotch court has 5 friends on a side, making a total of 25 friend pairs (or squares). You start wondering if there could be more than 25 friend pairs while keeping the same 5 friends on a side. That would mean some friends should shake hands more than once when standing next to different pairs! But wait, our rule was to always create new friendships - no repeats allowed. Can this work? This problem deals with something similar called "Maximum-sized product sets" but using groups instead of hopscotch courts and friends. In math language, we have a group H, which contains a smaller group A. Our goal is to find a way so that every distinct pair coming from multiplying elements within group A will result in a new element in the big group H without repetition. This makes the product set A^2 as large as possible. For example, consider these three groups: 1. Abelian Group - Friends playing hopscotch in a line taking turns shaking hands with their neighbors; everyone follows the same direction, so no need to worry about orientation. 2. Non-Noetherian Group - Imagine having baskets full of friends joining the hopscotch game continuously during playtime. With enough time, you may end up with many unique friend pairs even though you started with just 5 friends per round! 3. Infinite Exponent Group - Now think of the game lasting infinitely long, giving plenty of opportunities for creating new friend pairs along the way. In these cases, we can definitely build larger and larger sets of unique friend pairs even while sticking to our rules! However, things become tricky when dealing with groups that are NOT ABELIAN, NOETHERIAN, nor INFINITE EXPONENT because they impose restrictions on how quickly we can grow those unique friend pairs. The challenge now becomes proving whether or not we can still come up with increasingly bigger unique friend pairs under these stricter conditions. We leave this puzzle for older grades and mathematicians to figure out! Anna take it upstairs babe take it upstairs. Sara and Tom were playing in the yard. Sara had a big nail that she found in the garage. She was curious about it. She wanted to see what it could do. "Look, Tom, I have a nail!" Sara said. "It is shiny and pointy. Do you want to see?" Tom did not care about the nail. He wanted to play with his ball. He did not like Sara. He liked to tease her. "No, I don't want to see your stupid nail," Tom said. "It is boring and dirty. You are silly to play with it. You should play with a ball like me. A ball is fun and round. A nail is not." Sara felt sad and angry. She did not like Tom. She did not like his words. She wanted to make him stop. "Stop teasing me, Tom!" Sara said. "You are mean and rude. You don't know anything about nails. Nails are not boring and dirty. They are useful and strong. They can make things and break things. I will show you!" Sara ran towards Tom with the nail in her hand. She wanted to poke him with it. She wanted to make him hurt. She wanted to make him sorry. Tom saw Sara coming. He was scared. He tried to run away. But he was too slow. Sara caught him. She poked him with the nail. She poked him hard. Tom screamed. He felt a sharp pain in his arm. He saw blood. He dropped his ball. He cried. Sara saw what she did. She felt a shock. She did not mean to hurt Tom so much. She dropped the nail. She cried too. Mom heard the screams. She ran to the yard. She saw Tom and Sara. She saw the blood and the nail. She was angry and worried. She took Tom to the hospital. She scolded Sara. She punished her. Sara and Tom learned a lesson. Title: How to Get a Spoiled Pookie Bear in Club Penguin Club Penguin was a popular online multiplayer game that allowed players to explore a virtual world as penguins. One of the many sought-after items in the game was the Spoiled Pookie Bear. This item was only available during certain events or by using cheat codes. Although the game is no longer active, this guide can still serve as an interesting glimpse into its mechanics and the efforts required to obtain rare items like the Spoiled Pookie Bear. **Disclaimer:** This information pertains to a discontinued game called Club Penguin. The steps mentioned here are purely educational and cannot be applied in real life since the game has been shut down. Step 1: Create Your Account ---------------------------- To start playing Club Penguin, you needed to sign up for an account with your email address, username, password, and birthdate. Creating an account gave you access to the game's features and enabled interaction with other users within the Club Penguin community. ### Step 2: Logging Into the Game Once your account had been created, log in using your credentials. You would then arrive at the 'Town' area, where most users gathered before proceeding elsewhere in the game. Each player started with their own penguin avatar wearing basic clothing items provided upon registration. #### Step 3: Earning Coins Coins were the primary currency used in Club Penguin. Players could earn coins through various activities such as completing tasks (like delivering newspapers), participating in mini-games, or selling unwanted items from their inventory via the 'My Locker' feature. These coins were essential for purchasing furniture, clothing, accessories, and collectible items from catalogues found throughout the map. ##### Key Tips & Guidelines: * Engage in different mini-games regularly, as some offer higher coin rewards than others. For instance, games like Jet Pack Adventure and Catchin' Waves typically yielded more coins per play. * Be mindful when spending coins; consider whether the cost justifies the value of the item being purchased. * Keep checking back at stores, as new stock often appeared every week. ###### Step 4: Understanding Membership Benefits While free accounts offered plenty of fun experiences, becoming a member came with additional advantages, including exclusive parties, early access to content, more outfits, furniture pieces, and pets - one of which was the coveted Spoiled Pookie Bear. A membership could be bought directly or earned indirectly through promotions run by Disney Online Studios. ###### Step 5: Acquiring the Spoiled Pookie Bear The Spoiled Pookie Bear was initially released during the Hollywood Party event in May 2008. It returned briefly during another party but hasn't been available since. As such, obtaining this particular bear now requires either trading with existing owners or utilizing glitches/cheats, both of which go against Club Penguin's terms of service. Therefore, acquiring the Spoiled Pookie Bear today isn't possible without breaking rules or resorting to third-party transactions outside the official platform. In conclusion, while getting a Spoiled Pookie Bear once graced players with bragging rights among friends, attempting to acquire one post-game closure involves questionable practices and risks violating ethical standards set forth by developers. Revisiting guides like these offers nostalgic insights into defunct gaming communities and serves as reminders of cherished digital memories. Oh, they made mess, real mess in here. In this experiment we show how water can break glass by expansion during freezing. Children can then infer that the same thing can happen with water and rocks in nature. 1 quart jar and lid (spaghetti sauce or mayonnaise) In front of the class, fill the jar with water and seal it with the lid. Wrap the towel around the jar and freeze it overnight. Unwrap the jar in front of the class and ask for their observations. What happened to the jar? (The water expanded as it froze, forcing the glass to crack.) Explain to the class that in nature the same thing can happen when rainwater gets inside the holes and cracks rocks. This small but constant process slowly erodes the rocks and changes the way they look. "Such impudence! Why should we pay any attention to such stuff?
